nn_title: Appraising home purchase appraisals
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Does the expansion of broadband internet infrastructure into rural and exurban areas causally increase housing demand and prices in those markets, and does this effect intensify after remote work normalization?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered rollout of federal broadband subsidy programs (e.g., RDOF auctions) as an instrument for high-speed internet access at the census tract level, interacted with post-2020 remote work adoption rates.
data: FCC Form 477 broadband availability data merged with CoreLogic transaction-level housing prices and USDA Rural Development program records spanning 2015-2024 across rural and exurban counties nationwide.
key_finding: Broadband expansion may increase rural housing prices by 5-15% with effects doubling post-pandemic, while compressing urban-rural price gradients, with heterogeneous effects by distance from metropolitan employment centers.
novelty_rationale: While remote work and housing prices are studied separately, no paper has used the exogenous broadband rollout as an instrument to isolate the causal infrastructure channel from demand-side remote work sorting, nor traced the resulting spatial reallocation of housing wealth.
nn_title: The remote work revolution: Impact on real estate values and the urban environme
assigned_label: Residential mobility and migration determinants

research_question: Do mortgage lenders endogenously adjust appraisal management company assignments to influence appraised values in markets with elevated house price growth, constituting a second-order channel of appraisal bias post-HVCC?
method: Structural estimation of a two-sided matching model between lenders and appraisal management companies, exploiting variation in AMC market entry and exit events as quasi-experimental shocks to identify assignment-driven appraisal inflation.
data: Confidential HMDA origination records linked to appraisal registry data from the Appraisal Subcommittee, AMC licensing records from state regulatory agencies, and CoreLogic appraisal-to-sale price ratios for 2012-2022.
key_finding: Lenders in rapidly appreciating markets may systematically route assignments to AMCs with historically higher appraisal-to-contract ratios, generating 2-4% valuation inflation unexplained by borrower or property characteristics and concentrated among purchase mortgages near LTV thresholds.
novelty_rationale: Post-HVCC appraisal bias literature focuses on individual appraiser behavior; this paper shifts focus to the institutional layer of AMC selection as a strategic tool, a mechanism invisible in standard appraisal bias analyses.
nn_title: Appraising home purchase appraisals
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: How does the geographic overlap between community reinvestment act assessment areas and opportunity zone boundaries shape the spatial distribution of private capital flows into distressed neighborhoods?
method: Triple difference-in-differences comparing investment flows in census tracts that are both CRA-eligible and opportunity zone-designated versus tracts with only one or neither designation, using bank-level CRA examination data.
data: FFIEC CRA small business and community development loan disclosures, Treasury Opportunity Zone investment fund filings, IRS Form 8996 data, and HMDA mortgage origination records covering 2018-2023.
key_finding: Overlapping CRA and OZ designation may generate investment complementarities exceeding either program individually by 30-50%, but this synergy may be geographically concentrated in tracts already at the margin of revitalization, with minimal effects in the most distressed areas.
novelty_rationale: CRA and OZ programs are studied separately despite extensive geographic overlap; this paper examines whether their interaction amplifies or redirects capital flows, with direct policy implications for place-based subsidy coordination.
nn_title: Crime and private investment in urban neighborhoods
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Does the introduction of automated valuation model-based collateral assessments in mortgage underwriting systematically disadvantage minority and low-income borrowers by perpetuating historical price suppression embedded in training data?
method: Counterfactual decomposition analysis comparing AVM-generated valuations to appraiser values and repeat-sales price estimates, using a causal machine learning framework to isolate the contribution of neighborhood demographic composition to systematic AVM gaps.
data: CoreLogic AVM estimates linked to HMDA application records and appraiser-generated valuations, integrated with 1930s HOLC redlining shapefiles and contemporaneous demographic data from the American Community Survey for 2016-2022.
key_finding: AVMs may exhibit systematic undervaluation of 3-8% in historically redlined minority neighborhoods relative to appraiser values, with gaps persisting even after controlling for property characteristics, suggesting algorithmic perpetuation of historical discrimination.
novelty_rationale: While appraiser bias and redlining legacy are studied, no paper has directly tested whether AVM training on historically suppressed transaction prices encodes and perpetuates discriminatory valuation at scale, a mechanism with immediate regulatory significance.
nn_title: Appraising home purchase appraisals
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: How does the spatial diffusion of institutional single-family rental conversions affect nearby homeowner maintenance investment and neighborhood physical capital accumulation?
method: Regression discontinuity in space comparing homeowner maintenance expenditures and permit applications on blocks with institutional SFR presence versus adjacent blocks without, using variation created by institutional portfolio boundary decisions.
data: CoreLogic deed transfer records identifying institutional SFR acquisitions, Dodge Data construction permit filings for renovation activity, county assessor improvement records, and Google Street View imagery longitudinal comparisons for 50 major metros from 2012 to 2023.
key_finding: Institutional SFR presence on a block may reduce adjacent homeowner renovation investment by 10-20% through a neighborhood composition signal effect, while simultaneously increasing institutional-owned property renovation, with ambiguous net effects on neighborhood capital stock.
novelty_rationale: SFR institutional investor literature focuses on prices and rents; this paper introduces neighborhood physical capital externalities as a new outcome, connecting the ownership structure literature to the maintenance investment and urban decay literature.
nn_title: Do Wall Street Landlords Undermine Renters’ Welfare?
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Can the term structure of commercial real estate cap rates across property sectors predict the probability of REIT distress and equity dilution events, functioning as a real-side early warning indicator?
method: Predictive hazard model estimating the probability of REIT seasoned equity offerings, debt covenant violations, and dividend cuts as a function of the slope and curvature of the cap rate term structure estimated from transaction data across holding periods.
data: NCREIF transaction database providing property-level cap rates at acquisition and disposition, SNL Financial REIT financial statement data, and Capital IQ equity issuance and covenant violation records for all publicly traded US equity REITs from 1995 to 2023.
key_finding: A flattening or inversion of the cap rate term structure within a REIT's primary property sectors may predict distress events 12-18 months ahead with AUC statistics exceeding 0.75, outperforming leverage-based predictors alone particularly in the industrial and retail sectors.
novelty_rationale: REIT distress prediction relies on balance sheet variables; this paper introduces a novel market-based signal from the property-level cap rate term structure, directly analogous to bond market yield curve predictors of corporate distress.
nn_title: REIT Capital Structure Choices: Preparation Matters
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How do land assembly frictions—specifically holdout problems and strategic bargaining by individual parcel owners—affect the timing and density of urban infill development relative to greenfield development at the urban fringe?
method: Structural model of developer land assembly incorporating Nash bargaining under incomplete information, calibrated using parcel-level transaction sequences from urban infill sites, and compared to fringe development timing using a competing risks duration model.
data: CoStar development project records matched to county assessor parcel transaction histories identifying multi-parcel assembly sequences in 30 US cities, combined with metropolitan planning organization land use change data from 2000 to 2022.
key_finding: Land assembly holdout problems may delay infill development by 3-7 years relative to comparable greenfield sites and reduce achieved densities by 15-25% due to irregular assembled parcel geometries, with delays larger in cities without eminent domain reform.
novelty_rationale: Theoretical holdout models are well-developed but rarely estimated with microdata on actual assembly sequences; this paper provides the first structural calibration linking holdout frictions to observable development timing and density outcomes across multiple cities.
nn_title: The holdout problem and urban sprawl: Experimental evidence
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Does access to social capital, measured by cross-income social network connectivity within neighborhoods, moderate the intergenerational transmission of housing wealth and homeownership aspirations among low-income households?
method: Instrumental variables approach using Facebook Social Connectedness Index county-level variation as a proxy for social capital, instrumented by historical ethnic fraternal organization density, to estimate effects on first-time homeownership transitions among low-income young adults.
data: Facebook Social Connectedness Index linked to PSID household panel data on housing tenure transitions, matched to county-level historical fraternal organization records from the 1920 census and contemporary American Community Survey economic outcomes for 1990-2022.
key_finding: Greater cross-income social connectivity may increase low-income young adult homeownership transition rates by 3-8 percentage points, partially mediating intergenerational wealth transmission gaps, with effects concentrated in counties with strong local mortgage lending competition.
novelty_rationale: While parental wealth and homeownership transmission are studied, no paper has integrated the emerging social capital literature using network data into housing tenure transitions, identifying whether social bridging specifically moderates wealth constraints.
nn_title: The role of parents on the home ownership experience of their children: Evidence
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: How does the staggered adoption of commercial property assessed clean energy (C-PACE) financing programs across states affect office and industrial building energy retrofit rates, property values, and loan default risk?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting state C-PACE enabling legislation dates to identify causal effects on building energy audit and retrofit activity, property transaction prices, and CMBS delinquency rates for treated property types.
data: PACENation C-PACE financing origination records, ENERGY STAR benchmarking data for commercial buildings, CoStar transaction and energy performance records, and Trepp CMBS loan-level performance data for 2008-2023 across states with and without C-PACE programs.
key_finding: C-PACE program adoption may increase commercial building deep energy retrofits by 25-40% and raise property values by 3-6% through energy cost savings capitalization, while reducing CMBS default hazards by reducing operating cost volatility, with effects concentrated in older Class B buildings.
novelty_rationale: While green certification premiums are documented, no paper has evaluated C-PACE as a policy mechanism for retrofit incentivization using staggered legislative adoption, connecting environmental finance with commercial mortgage credit risk and valuation.
nn_title: Green Buildings in Commercial Mortgage‐Backed Securities: The Effects of LEED an
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do real estate private equity funds engage in market timing at the portfolio construction stage by concentrating acquisitions in metropolitan markets where private information about local economic shocks is available through co-investment networks?
method: Cross-sectional network analysis constructing co-investment linkages between REPE funds and local operating partners, testing whether portfolio concentration in co-investor home markets predicts subsequent property-level returns using instrumental variation from LP commitment timing.
data: Preqin real estate fund-level acquisition data linked to CoStar property transaction records identifying buyer entities, supplemented by PERE network data on fund LP and operating partner relationships for US-focused funds from 2000 to 2022.
key_finding: REPE funds with denser local co-investment networks may earn 150-300 basis point return premiums in concentrated markets, consistent with local information advantage, but this premium may be partially offset by competition effects when multiple network-connected funds target the same market.
novelty_rationale: While REIT geographic concentration and information asymmetries are studied, private equity real estate fund network effects on portfolio construction and returns have not been examined, filling a gap between the alternative investment and real estate information literature.
nn_title: Asset Location, Timing Ability and the Cross‐Section of Commercial Real Estate R
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How does the composition of public pension fund real estate allocations—specifically the balance between core open-end funds and higher-return closed-end vehicles—affect the funded status volatility of pension liabilities across business cycles?
method: Dynamic asset-liability management model calibrated to pension liability duration and funded status sensitivity, estimated using actuarial data merged with pension fund real estate allocation records, with regime-switching to capture boom-bust heterogeneity.
data: CEM Benchmarking pension fund real estate allocation microdata, Preqin fund performance records for open-end and closed-end real estate vehicles, Public Plans Data pension funded status reports, and NCREIF property index component returns from 1990 to 2023.
key_finding: Pension funds overallocated to closed-end value-add and opportunistic vehicles may experience funded status volatility 40-60% higher than core-dominated allocations during recessions, with the liability hedging shortfall concentrated in funds with shorter investment horizons driven by demographic maturity.
novelty_rationale: ALM literature addresses real estate allocation but treats it as a homogeneous asset class; this paper disaggregates by fund strategy and vehicle structure to identify how composition decisions rather than just allocation levels drive pension liability risk.
nn_title: Real Estate in an ALM Framework: The Case of Fair Value Accounting
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does exposure to wildfire smoke—distinct from direct fire damage—capitalize into residential property values and affect mortgage delinquency rates in affected communities?
method: Difference-in-differences using satellite-derived PM2.5 smoke exposure at the ZIP code level as the treatment variable, exploiting wind pattern variation as a source of quasi-random smoke exposure conditional on distance from fire perimeters.
data: EPA and NOAA satellite PM2.5 smoke concentration data matched to Zillow housing price indexes and CoreLogic mortgage delinquency records at the ZIP code level, with US Forest Service fire perimeter data for Western US ZIP codes from 2010 to 2023.
key_finding: Persistent smoke exposure may reduce housing values by 2-5% through chronic health and amenity channels beyond direct fire risk, with larger effects for high-end properties, while elevated delinquency effects of 15-25% manifest with a 12-month lag through income disruption and migration.
novelty_rationale: Wildfire real estate research focuses on damage and flood zones; this paper isolates smoke as a distinct, spatially diffuse externality affecting properties far from fire perimeters, with distinct valuation and credit risk implications untouched by existing insurance or flood zone frameworks.
nn_title: Risk externalities and the problem of wildfire risk
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: How do racial disparities in property tax assessment uniformity within jurisdictions compound existing homeownership wealth gaps over multi-decade holding periods?
method: Decomposition analysis combining within-jurisdiction assessment ratio dispersion estimated from hedonic models with wealth accumulation simulations across racial groups, using instrumental variation in assessment cycles to identify causal assessment gaps.
data: Lincoln Institute of Land Policy assessment-sales ratio data merged with county assessor parcel files by race-proxy methods, PSID household wealth panel for homeowning households, and state equalization factor reports for all major US metropolitan counties from 1990 to 2020.
key_finding: Systematic over-assessment of minority-owned properties by 10-20% relative to comparable white-owned properties may reduce cumulative net housing wealth accumulation for Black homeowners by 8-15% over a 30-year holding period, compounding to represent tens of thousands of dollars per household.
novelty_rationale: Assessment bias is documented cross-sectionally but its dynamic compounding effect on the racial homeownership wealth gap over time has not been quantified, making this the first paper to connect static assessment inequality to long-run wealth trajectory divergence.
nn_title: The Incidence of the Land Use Regulatory Tax
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Can latent skill heterogeneity among commercial real estate appraisers, identified from their track record of accuracy across market cycles, predict future appraisal accuracy and market-level valuation smoothing?
method: Empirical Bayes estimation of appraiser-level fixed effects from a panel of repeat appraisal-transaction pairs, testing whether estimated latent skill predicts out-of-sample appraisal accuracy and whether high-skill appraiser market share correlates with faster price discovery in commercial markets.
data: Confidential commercial appraisal records from multiple national lenders containing appraiser identifiers, appraisal dates and values, and subsequent transaction prices, linked to NCREIF property-level return data for office and multifamily properties from 2000 to 2022.
key_finding: Appraiser latent skill may exhibit substantial persistence and predict out-of-sample valuation accuracy, with high-skill appraiser market concentration associated with 20-35% lower appraisal smoothing at the market level, suggesting professional sorting into accurate pricing is quantifiable and market-consequential.
novelty_rationale: While appraisal smoothing and bias are well-studied, no paper has modeled appraiser-level skill heterogeneity as a persistent latent variable with aggregate market pricing implications, connecting labor economics methodology to the appraisal quality literature.
nn_title: The influence of contract prices and relationships on appraisal bias
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Does the emergence of real estate tokenization—fractional digital ownership of properties through blockchain—reduce the liquidity premium embedded in direct property values by expanding the investor base?
method: Event study and cross-sectional regression comparing transaction prices and bid-ask spreads for tokenized properties versus matched non-tokenized properties, using the staggered launch of tokenization platforms and regulatory approvals as quasi-experimental variation.
data: RealT, Lofty, and RedSwan tokenized property transaction records matched to CoStar comparable sales, supplemented by secondary token market trading data from decentralized exchange platforms and SEC Regulation A+ offering records from 2018 to 2024.
key_finding: Tokenization may reduce the liquidity premium by 5-10% for commercial and multifamily properties, with effects concentrated in markets with high retail investor demand and smaller deal sizes where traditional private placement illiquidity discounts are largest.
novelty_rationale: While private real estate liquidity premiums are documented, the tokenization literature is nascent and no study has directly estimated its effect on the private real estate liquidity discount using market microstructure methods applied to secondary token trading data.
nn_title: A Theory of Liquidity in Residential Real Estate Markets
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How does the geographic mismatch between where mortgage servicers are headquartered and where their serviced loans are located affect loss mitigation outcomes for borrowers following local income shocks?
method: Matching estimator comparing modification approval rates and loss severity for loans serviced by geographically distant versus proximate servicers, exploiting mergers and acquisitions in the servicing industry as exogenous shocks to servicer-borrower geographic proximity.
data: BlackKnight McDash loan-level servicing records with servicer identifiers matched to ServicerIntelligence corporate headquarters locations, HMDA geographic fields, and BLS county-level employment data identifying local income shocks from 2005 to 2022.
key_finding: Loans serviced by distant servicers may experience 10-20% lower modification approval rates following local economic shocks due to reduced borrower-specific information, with effects largest for small-balance loans in non-metropolitan areas where local servicer knowledge is most informative.
novelty_rationale: Servicer incentives are studied through financial structures, but geographic information asymmetry as a driver of loss mitigation performance has not been examined, connecting the geographic finance literature to the mortgage servicing market.
nn_title: Spatial Heterogeneity in Mortgage Terminations by Refinance, Sale and Default
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: Do homeowners in communities with strong social cohesion, measured by civic participation and associational density, exhibit lower strategic default propensities independent of financial incentives, and does this vary with community political ideology?
method: Multilevel logistic regression combining loan-level default and equity data with county-level social capital indexes and associational density measures, instrumenting social capital using historical Putnam-era organizational enrollment data to address endogeneity.
data: BlackKnight McDash loan-level default records merged with the Penn State Social Capital benchmark and Bowling Alone county social capital indexes, American National Election Studies attitudinal data, and CoreLogic equity estimates at the ZIP code level for 2007-2015.
key_finding: High social capital counties may exhibit 15-25% lower strategic default rates among deeply underwater borrowers even after controlling for financial incentives, with the effect mediated by social norm enforcement, and weakened in ideologically libertarian communities where contract breach is normalized.
novelty_rationale: Strategic default literature focuses on financial thresholds and emotion-based factors, but the role of community social norms as a constraint on strategic behavior—distinct from shame/guilt at the individual level—has not been empirically tested using cross-community variation.
nn_title: Strategic Mortgage Default: The Effect of Neighborhood Factors
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: How do zoning variance decisions by local boards respond to real estate developer political campaign contributions, and does this quid pro quo relationship generate measurable density premiums in awarded projects?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting close zoning board votes, combined with instrumental variables using exogenous variation in state campaign finance disclosure thresholds that affect the measurability of political contributions to identify density and approval rate effects.
data: State campaign finance disclosure databases matched to zoning board member identities and variance application records from 15 large US cities, CoStar development records identifying approved densities and project sizes, and municipal zoning board minutes from 2005 to 2022.
key_finding: Developer campaign contributions may increase variance approval probabilities by 8-15 percentage points and approved floor-area ratios by 10-20%, generating land value premiums of $2-5 million per project, with effects concentrated in cities with weaker state campaign finance disclosure laws.
novelty_rationale: Land regulation and political economy research typically uses observational cross-sectional designs; this paper introduces causal identification through close-vote RDD and campaign finance disclosure thresholds to directly quantify the economic returns to political investment in zoning outcomes.
nn_title: Clean money, dirty system: Connected landowners capture beneficial land rezoning
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Does the introduction of mandatory home energy performance disclosure at point-of-sale generate differential learning effects across buyer sophistication levels, reducing information asymmetry more for first-time buyers than experienced investors?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting staggered state-level adoption of mandatory energy disclosure laws, with heterogeneous treatment effects identified by buyer experience proxied by number of prior transactions in registry data.
data: Energy performance certificate databases from states with mandatory disclosure matched to CoreLogic buyer transaction histories enabling experience classification, utility consumption records from municipal open data portals, and Zillow listing data for 2008-2023.
key_finding: Mandatory disclosure may raise the energy efficiency valuation of naive first-time buyers by 50-80% of the level observed for sophisticated investors, closing the information gap in implicit energy prices, while reducing market-level hedonic price dispersion for high-energy-cost properties by 15-25%.
novelty_rationale: Energy disclosure literature documents average price effects but ignores heterogeneous learning across buyer types; identifying which buyers drive market-wide efficiency of energy capitalization provides novel insights into disclosure policy design and information economics of real estate.
nn_title: The capitalization of energy efficiency: Evidence from the housing market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: How does the presence of corporate transparency requirements under public REIT reporting standards affect the pricing and performance of affiliated private real estate funds managed by the same sponsor?
method: Matched fund analysis comparing private fund returns and fee structures across REIT-affiliated versus independent private sponsors, exploiting SEC reporting threshold events around REIT IPOs and delistings as changes in affiliated sponsor transparency.
data: Preqin private real estate fund performance and fee data linked to REIT sponsor entities identified through Form 10-K disclosures, SEC EDGAR reporting records, and NCREIF manager-level property return data for private funds from 1995 to 2023.
key_finding: REIT-affiliated private fund sponsors may charge 20-40 basis point lower fees and exhibit 50-100 basis point better risk-adjusted performance due to spillover transparency disciplines and reputation alignment, with effects strongest for sponsors where the REIT and private fund share significant property type overlap.
novelty_rationale: REIT corporate transparency is studied within the public market but its spillover to affiliated private investment vehicles has not been examined, connecting the public-private real estate performance comparison to information economics of multi-vehicle investment managers.
nn_title: REIT Auditor Fees and Financial Market Transparency
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the presence of large employer anchor tenants in mixed-use transit-oriented developments reduce retail vacancy rates and rental income volatility for co-located retail compared to standalone retail of similar quality?
method: Panel analysis of retail space financial performance in mixed-use TOD versus comparable standalone retail using a matched sample design, exploiting anchor tenant lease renewals and terminations as quasi-experimental variation in anchor presence.
data: CoStar retail property operating data including vacancy rates and asking rents linked to CoStar tenant roster records identifying anchor tenants, matched to transit agency TOD project records and Placer.ai foot traffic data for 500 mixed-use TOD projects from 2010 to 2023.
key_finding: Mixed-use TOD retail with large employer anchors may exhibit 30-50% lower vacancy rate volatility and 10-15% higher net operating income compared to matched standalone retail, with effects concentrated in second-tier retail space dependent on anchor-generated foot traffic.
novelty_rationale: TOD research focuses on residential value and transit ridership effects; this paper introduces the operational performance of co-located retail as a new outcome, testing whether the commercial synergies assumed in mixed-use underwriting are realized in practice.
nn_title: Valuing Retail Shopping Center Lease Contracts
assigned_label: Commercial Real Estate Rent Dynamics

research_question: How does the secondary market liquidity of mortgage servicing rights affect servicer behavior toward delinquent borrowers, specifically the trade-off between short-term fee income from extending delinquency and long-term franchise value from modification success?
method: Instrumental variable approach using secondary MSR market price indexes as an instrument for servicer balance sheet sensitivity to MSR values, testing whether servicers with higher MSR mark-to-market exposure extend delinquency duration relative to initiating modifications.
data: BlackKnight McDash servicing records with servicer identifiers merged with Mortgage Industry Advisory Corporation MSR price survey data and SNL Financial servicer-level financial statements for 2008-2020, exploiting cross-servicer variation in MSR portfolio concentration.
key_finding: Servicers with higher MSR market value sensitivity may extend delinquency by 2-4 months longer before initiating modifications, increasing loss severities by 5-10%, with the effect most pronounced during periods of MSR price appreciation when extending collection generates more servicing fee income.
novelty_rationale: MSR valuation and servicer behavior are studied separately; this paper introduces secondary MSR market prices as a direct financial incentive shaping modification behavior, identifying a channel from financial markets to borrower outcomes not addressed in the servicer incentive literature.
nn_title: Delinquencies, Default and Borrowers' Strategic Behavior toward the Modification
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do neighborhood-level chronic flood events from sea-level rise and storm surge generate asymmetric housing market tipping points where price trajectories shift from gradual decline to accelerated abandonment?
method: Nonlinear threshold regression and panel smooth transition regression models applied to property price and transaction volume data, testing for tipping-point dynamics using chronic flood frequency metrics as the transition variable.
data: NOAA tidal gauge chronic flooding event data and FEMA National Flood Insurance Program claims matched to CoreLogic property-level transaction prices and vacancy records for coastal ZIP codes across the Eastern Seaboard and Gulf Coast from 2000 to 2023.
key_finding: A threshold of approximately 10 chronic flood days per year may trigger non-linear price acceleration of 8-15% annual declines versus gradual capitalization below the threshold, with transaction volume collapse preceding price tipping by 12-18 months as a leading indicator of neighborhood abandonment risk.
novelty_rationale: Climate risk capitalization literature documents average price effects and linear gradients; this paper tests whether chronic flooding generates tipping-point dynamics consistent with coordinate abandonment equilibria, which have fundamentally different implications for insurance pricing and infrastructure investment.
nn_title: Is the Risk of Sea Level Rise Capitalized in Residential Real Estate?
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: How does the spatial clustering of short-term rental deconversions back to long-term housing supply—induced by pandemic-era occupancy shocks—affect local rental market recovery speeds and tenant demographic sorting?
method: Spatial difference-in-differences exploiting heterogeneous STR deconversion rates across census tracts driven by pre-pandemic Airbnb exposure intensity, using county STR permit cancellation records as the treatment variable for rental market outcomes.
data: AirDNA short-term rental listing and occupancy data tracking conversion status linked to Zillow observed rent indexes at the ZIP code level, ACS migration and household formation data, and STR permit records from 25 cities tracking deconversion timing from 2019 to 2023.
key_finding: High-STR-deconversion tracts may experience 4-8% faster rental market recovery and measurable demographic shifts toward lower-income tenants, but with heterogeneous effects by housing type, as deconverted units may not match the household size preferences of local long-term renters.
novelty_rationale: STR regulation literature examines conversion from long-term to short-term rental markets; this paper studies the reverse flow of deconversion as an exogenous housing supply shock, identifying asymmetric reabsorption dynamics invisible in standard STR impact analyses.
nn_title: Housing sales and construction responses to COVID‐19: Evidence from shelter‐in‐p
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Does the legalization and proliferation of online illicit drug markets (darknet markets) differentially affect property values and local crime rates across neighborhoods with varying levels of broadband internet access?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting staggered darknet market entry and exit events (e.g., Silk Road takedown, Hansa, AlphaBay) combined with cross-sectional variation in neighborhood broadband penetration rates as a continuous treatment intensity instrument, with repeat-sales hedonic property price regressions and local crime incident data as dual outcomes.
data: FBI Uniform Crime Reports and NIBRS incident-level crime data (2010–2023), Zillow and CoreLogic repeat-sales transaction records at the ZIP-code level, FCC Form 477 broadband deployment data for internet access penetration, and blockchain-scraped darknet market transaction volume proxies from academic cryptomarket archives covering major platform lifecycles.
key_finding: Neighborhoods with high broadband penetration likely experience reductions in visible street-level drug-related crime and associated property value discounts as retail drug transactions migrate online, while areas with low connectivity may see concentrated displacement effects and amplified property value penalties, implying a digital-divide channel through which technology reshapes the spatial distribution of crime disamenities.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats drug markets as physical amenities or disamenities (e.g., dispensary studies) or focuses on aggregate crime-wage tradeoffs, this idea introduces digital market infrastructure as a novel mediating mechanism that spatially redistributes—rather than merely reduces or increases—crime's economic externalities on property and labor markets.
nn_title: Urban crime and the price of heroin
assigned_label: Crime's economic effects on property and wages

research_question: Do historical pandemic shocks permanently reshape urban density gradients, or do cities fully revert to pre-shock spatial equilibria within a generation?
method: A spatial difference-in-differences design exploiting cross-city variation in 1918 influenza mortality intensity (instrumented by pre-existing streetcar network density as a transmission amplifier) to trace 100-year trajectories of residential density, land values, and suburbanization rates using digitized decennial census records and historical deed transaction archives.
data: Digitized U.S. Census tract-level population and housing data from 1910–2020 merged with city-level 1918 flu mortality records from the U.S. Public Health Service, historical streetcar network GIS layers, and county deed transaction records from historical archives covering approximately 50 major U.S. cities.
key_finding: Cities that experienced severe 1918 pandemic mortality likely exhibit persistent — not transitory — decentralization of residential density and a permanently flatter land value gradient, suggesting pandemic shocks can shift urban spatial equilibria along multiple stable paths rather than triggering simple mean reversion.
novelty_rationale: Unlike existing work that studies short-run COVID-19 mobility or 19th-century infrastructure effects on mortality, this paper asks whether pandemics themselves are a root cause of long-run urban form, providing a century-long causal identification of pandemic shocks on city structure that no prior study has attempted.
nn_title: Urban flight: A short‐term phenomenon or long‐term trend?
assigned_label: Pandemics, Disease, and Urban Property Markets

research_question: How does the sudden, exogenous revelation of school quality information through standardized test score disclosure mandates causally alter the spatial gradient of housing price capitalization across neighborhoods with differing pre-existing information environments?
method: A staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting the phased rollout of state-level school report card legislation across U.S. states as an exogenous shock to information availability, combined with a spatial regression discontinuity at attendance zone boundaries to isolate capitalization effects from neighborhood sorting.
data: Longitudinal parcel-level transaction data from CoreLogic (1990–2022) merged with Stanford Education Data Archive school performance metrics, state legislative records on report card adoption dates, and Census tract-level socioeconomic covariates covering all 50 states.
key_finding: Information disclosure is expected to steepen the price-quality gradient most sharply in high-poverty markets where pre-disclosure information asymmetries were largest, potentially widening spatial inequality in housing wealth by 8–15% within five years of mandatory reporting adoption.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats school quality as observed and studies its level effect on prices, this paper treats the public legibility of school quality itself as the treatment, identifying how information market structure—rather than school quality per se—determines the degree and distributional pattern of capitalization.
nn_title: Housing market and school choice response to school quality information shocks✰
assigned_label: School quality capitalization into housing prices

research_question: How do school district consolidations — merging multiple small or under-enrolled districts into a single administrative unit — affect long-run student achievement, teacher labor markets, and local property values, and do these effects differ systematically by the racial and socioeconomic composition of the merged districts?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting the quasi-random timing of state-mandated consolidation waves across multiple states, combined with a hedonic property value model using repeat-sales house price indices to isolate fiscal capitalization effects from compositional sorting.
data: Linked administrative records from the National Center for Education Statistics Common Core of Data (1990–2023) merged with CoreLogic residential transaction data, Census tract demographics, and state education department personnel files covering districts in at least six states that enacted consolidation mandates during the study period.
key_finding: Consolidation is likely to produce modest average achievement gains and property value increases in the most resource-constrained rural districts but may generate significant negative effects — including teacher attrition and widened racial achievement gaps — when merged districts have historically disparate socioeconomic compositions, suggesting that consolidation policy produces highly heterogeneous welfare effects masked by average estimates.
novelty_rationale: While prior work examines closures of individual schools within a district, no study uses modern causal identification to jointly estimate how inter-district consolidation reshapes student outcomes, teacher markets, and fiscal capitalization simultaneously, nor does existing research decompose these effects by the degree of demographic mismatch between merged districts.
nn_title: School quality as a catalyst for bidding wars and new housing development
assigned_label: School quality capitalization into housing prices

research_question: Do physical climate risk disclosures embedded in municipal bond offering documents translate into measurable yield spreads, and does this pricing response vary systematically with the fiscal capacity of the issuing municipality to adapt?
method: Natural language processing is applied to extract granular climate risk language from a large corpus of municipal bond official statements, constructing a continuous disclosure intensity score that is then embedded in a panel regression of offering yields on risk scores, controlling for credit ratings, maturity, tax status, and local socioeconomic covariates, with fiscal capacity (measured by rainy-day fund balances and revenue diversification) as a moderating variable.
data: Electronic Municipal Market Access (EMMA) official statement filings for approximately 500,000 municipal bond issuances from 2005–2023 matched to NOAA county-level climate hazard exposure indices, U.S. Census fiscal data, and Bloomberg municipal yield data.
key_finding: Municipalities with high physical climate risk disclosure scores are expected to face yield premiums of 10–40 basis points, with the premium attenuating significantly for issuers with stronger fiscal buffers, suggesting that bond markets price both hazard exposure and adaptive capacity jointly rather than independently.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work focusing on carbon policy risk in equity options or aggregate weather shocks to agriculture, this study uniquely links textual disclosure heterogeneity in the primary municipal debt market to yield pricing while introducing fiscal adaptive capacity as a first-order moderator, opening a new channel through which climate risk is intermediated by public finance institutions.
nn_title: Sea-Level Rise Exposure and Municipal Bond Yields
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do repeated, cumulative wildfire smoke exposure events—distinct from direct fire destruction—systematically discount residential property values, and does this discount accelerate as climate change increases smoke frequency?
method: A hedonic difference-in-differences design exploiting plausibly exogenous, wind-driven variation in annual smoke-days (from satellite-derived PM2.5 plume data) across otherwise comparable ZIP codes, augmented with a panel event-study around years of extreme smoke seasons to identify nonlinear threshold effects on transaction prices.
data: Merged dataset combining CoreLogic deed-level transaction records (~30 million transactions, 2000–2023) for the western United States with EPA AirNow and NASA MODIS/VIIRS satellite smoke-plume data providing daily county- and grid-level smoke concentration, plus CDC respiratory health records and NOAA wind trajectory data for instrument construction.
key_finding: Properties in high smoke-exposure ZIP codes are plausibly discounted 2–8% relative to smoke-insulated comparable areas, with discounts steepening after roughly 20 smoke-days per year and accelerating post-2015 as smoke seasons intensified, implying a growing, climate-driven negative externality that is distinct from fire-risk and largely unpriced in current insurance markets.
novelty_rationale: Prior environmental hazard valuation studies focus on acute, localized events (earthquakes, plant closures, direct fire damage), whereas this paper isolates the chronic, diffuse, and spatially expansive disamenity of wildfire smoke—a hazard that is rapidly worsening under climate change yet has received virtually no causal identification in the property-valuation literature.
nn_title: Risk externalities and the problem of wildfire risk
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: Do natural disasters that destroy rental housing supply trigger lasting gentrification in urban neighborhoods, and does this displacement effect vary systematically with the share of pre-disaster renters versus owners?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting the spatial footprints of multiple U.S. urban disasters (Hurricanes Katrina, Harvey, and Maria; California wildfires) as quasi-random shocks to rental supply, combined with a mediation analysis separating the reconstruction-investment channel from the population-composition channel using census tract panel data.
data: Linked parcel-level property records, HMDA mortgage originations, ACS 5-year tract-level tenure and income data, FEMA damage assessments, and IRS zip-code migration flows covering 2000–2023 across all major U.S. urban disaster events.
key_finding: Disasters destroying predominantly renter-occupied units are plausibly associated with 8–18% long-run appreciation in adjacent undamaged neighborhoods and measurable upward shifts in median tract income, consistent with demand-driven gentrification, while disasters concentrated in owner-occupied stock show smaller or reversed price effects.
novelty_rationale: Existing literature focuses on how disasters depress values in affected areas or examines insurance redistribution, but no study jointly identifies the tenure-composition mechanism through which supply destruction in rental markets reshapes neighborhood socioeconomic trajectories across a broad cross-disaster panel.
nn_title: Hurricane‐induced risk contagion in commercial real estate: Evidence from Hurric
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does algorithmic property valuation (AVM) technology systematically undervalue homes in majority-minority neighborhoods relative to traditional appraisals, and do lenders' differential reliance on AVMs versus human appraisers perpetuate or attenuate racial valuation gaps?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered adoption of AVM-based underwriting by lenders across markets, combined with a decomposition analysis comparing AVM-generated valuations against contemporaneous licensed appraisals at the property level, using race of neighborhood as the key moderating variable.
data: HMDA loan-level records merged with county assessor automated valuation estimates, Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac appraisal-versus-AVM discrepancy disclosures, and Census tract racial composition data spanning 2010–2023 across all U.S. MSAs.
key_finding: AVMs are likely to exhibit smaller but persistent racial valuation gaps relative to human appraisers in high-minority tracts due to historical price signal contamination in training data, yet lender substitution toward AVMs may paradoxically reduce the most egregious forms of idiosyncratic human-appraiser discrimination, producing heterogeneous welfare effects across minority subgroups.
novelty_rationale: While prior work documents racial gaps in human appraisals and agent behavior, no study has causally identified how the institutional shift toward algorithmic valuation redistributes—rather than simply replicates—racial discrimination across the mortgage origination pipeline.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: How does climate-related physical risk — specifically, the gradual updating of flood-zone designations and wildfire hazard maps — alter the timing and intensity of irreversible land development decisions, and does this dynamic resemble a 'shrinking option' that accelerates exercise before regulatory reclassification forecloses value?
method: A continuous-time real options model in which the option to develop depreciates stochastically as the probability of adverse regulatory reclassification (flood/wildfire rezoning) follows a Poisson arrival process, yielding closed-form trigger thresholds; the model is then structurally estimated using a two-stage instrumental-variables approach that exploits quasi-random timing of FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Map revisions and USFS wildfire hazard reclassifications as exogenous shocks to option value depreciation rates.
data: Parcel-level building permit records and land transaction prices from CoreLogic (2000–2023) matched to FEMA FIRM revision dates, USFS wildfire hazard severity zone updates, and county assessor data across all coastal and western U.S. counties, yielding approximately 4 million parcel-year observations spanning multiple regulatory reclassification events.
key_finding: Developers are likely to exhibit a 'race to reclassification' response — sharply accelerating development permit filings in the 12–24 months preceding anticipated adverse map updates — with permit-to-start lags compressing by 30–50% relative to unaffected parcels, consistent with a shrinking-option mechanism rather than passive waiting behavior predicted by standard real options theory.
novelty_rationale: Existing real options models treat the development option as stable or growing in value over time, but this paper introduces a novel 'shrinking option' framework driven by endogenous regulatory risk that fundamentally inverts the classic 'wait-and-see' prediction, linking climate policy uncertainty to land development timing in a way no prior theoretical or empirical work has formalized.
nn_title: Permits, Starts, and Completions: Structural Relationships Versus Real Options
assigned_label: Real Options Theory in Land Development

research_question: How do high-speed rail network expansions reshape intracity land value gradients by creating secondary agglomeration nodes that compete with traditional central business districts?
method: A spatial difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of high-speed rail station openings across Chinese and European cities, combined with a monocentric-to-polycentric urban land value model estimated via machine-learning-assisted hedonic regressions on granular property transaction data.
data: Parcel-level land auction and residential transaction records from Chinese land market databases (2000–2023) and European cadastral registries, merged with precise HSR station opening dates, interstation travel-time matrices, and firm-level employment density grids at 500-meter resolution.
key_finding: HSR station openings likely generate localized land value premia of 15–35% within a 1–2 km catchment radius while simultaneously compressing the premium gradient toward the legacy CBD, with the magnitude depending critically on whether the station is embedded within the existing urban fabric or located on the city periphery.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats agglomeration as a prefecture-level average effect or examines HSR solely through a between-city lens, this study identifies how fixed-rail infrastructure reorganizes the internal spatial equilibrium of land values within cities by endogenously creating competing subcenter agglomerations, a mechanism largely absent from existing urban land rent models.
nn_title: The capitalization of metro rail access in urban housing markets
assigned_label: Transit infrastructure capitalization in urban housing

research_question: Do algorithmic iBuyer pricing models systematically misprice properties in ways that create exploitable arbitrage opportunities and distort local comparable sales used in subsequent appraisals?
method: Regression discontinuity and machine learning decomposition exploiting the rule-based acquisition thresholds of major iBuyers (Opendoor, Offerpad) to identify causal price wedges between algorithmic offers and realized market values, then tracing how iBuyer transactions propagate into appraisal comp sets.
data: Transaction-level MLS and deed records matched to disclosed iBuyer purchase and resale prices across 30+ U.S. metros from 2015–2023, supplemented by FHFA appraisal micro-data to identify which iBuyer sales appear as comparables in subsequent appraisals.
key_finding: iBuyer algorithms likely overpay in illiquid submarkets and underpay in rapidly appreciating ones, generating systematic mispricing wedges of 3–8%, and their resale transactions contaminate appraisal comp sets in ways that amplify rather than dampen local price volatility.
novelty_rationale: While iBuyer pricing has received popular attention, no study has rigorously identified the causal direction and magnitude of algorithmic mispricing using quasi-experimental variation and then linked it downstream to appraisal distortion, combining the market efficiency and distressed-comps literatures in a fundamentally new way.
nn_title: The Effects of Charm Listing Prices on House Transaction Prices
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: How do algorithmic iBuyer platforms alter price discovery, liquidity, and seller surplus in residential real estate markets compared to traditional broker-intermediated transactions, and do their effects vary systematically with local market thickness and information asymmetry?
method: A structural search-and-matching model in which sellers choose between an iBuyer instant offer and the traditional MLS broker channel is estimated using transaction-level data, exploiting iBuyer market entry and exit events as quasi-experiments to identify causal effects on prices, time-on-market, and the distribution of match quality.
data: Merged dataset of iBuyer transaction records (Opendoor, Offerpad, Zillow Offers) from public deed records and MLS listings across 30+ U.S. metros from 2014–2023, supplemented by CoreLogic automated valuation model estimates to construct seller-specific information asymmetry proxies.
key_finding: iBuyer intermediation is likely to compress seller surplus by 2–5% relative to broker-mediated sales but substantially reduces time-on-market, with the surplus loss being largest in thin, heterogeneous markets where algorithmic valuations carry greater error and sellers with high liquidity demand sort disproportionately into the iBuyer channel.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work on broker conflicts of interest and discount brokerage that examines human intermediaries under fixed-commission regimes, this idea focuses on algorithmic intermediaries that fundamentally restructure price discovery by replacing bilateral negotiation with a take-it-or-leave-it mechanism, introducing an entirely new theoretical and empirical terrain around platform market power, valuation error, and endogenous seller self-selection.
nn_title: What Problem Do Intermediaries Solve? Evidence From Real Estate Markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the adoption of split-rate property taxation—taxing land at a higher rate than structures—by one municipality induce neighboring jurisdictions to strategically adjust their own tax structures through fiscal competition or mimicry?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences combined with a spatial Durbin model, exploiting the historically idiosyncratic timing of split-rate tax adoptions across Pennsylvania municipalities as quasi-exogenous policy shocks to identify spillover effects on neighbors' land-to-building tax ratios, land use intensity, and local fiscal outcomes.
data: Pennsylvania State Tax Equalization Board annual municipal tax records (1980–2023) merged with parcel-level land and structure value assessments, Census of Governments fiscal data, and county-level building permit records, covering all ~2,500 Pennsylvania municipalities with variation in split-rate adoption timing.
key_finding: Municipalities adjacent to split-rate adopters are expected to show measurable upward adjustment in their own land tax share within three to five years of a neighbor's adoption, with the effect magnitude increasing in economic integration between jurisdictions, suggesting fiscal interdependence operates through both tax-base displacement and yardstick competition channels rather than pure benefit spillovers.
novelty_rationale: While fiscal interdependence in property tax levels is well-documented, no study has examined whether the structural composition of the property tax—the land-to-building rate split—propagates spatially across jurisdictions, linking the largely theoretical literature on land value taxation to the empirical spatial policy interdependence literature in a setting with unique real-world variation.
nn_title: The Spatial Effects of Wage or Property Tax Differentials, and Local Government
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do renters and homeowners exhibit systematically different capitalization responses to air quality improvements, and does tenure insecurity distort the spatial allocation of clean-air benefits across income groups?
method: A spatial regression discontinuity design that exploits sharp boundaries of EPA nonattainment designations as quasi-random variation in local air quality, combined with a structural hedonic model that separately identifies rent and owner-occupied price gradients to decompose welfare gains by tenure type and income quintile.
data: Linked panel of parcel-level transaction prices and rental listings from CoreLogic and Zillow (2000–2023), EPA AQS monitor readings and nonattainment county designations, Census IPUMS microdata for tenure status and household income, and Medicaid/Medicare claims to proxy health outcomes—covering all U.S. metropolitan statistical areas.
key_finding: Homeowners are likely to capture a disproportionate share of air-quality welfare gains through asset appreciation, while low-income renters in newly attainment-compliant areas may experience rent pass-through that erodes or reverses their health-cost savings, suggesting conventional benefit-cost analyses systematically overstate the distributional progressivity of clean-air regulation.
novelty_rationale: Existing hedonic and WTP studies treat housing markets as undifferentiated or focus solely on owner-occupied values; this paper is the first to explicitly model tenure-stratified capitalization and link it to a distributional welfare accounting framework, revealing a hidden regressive channel in environmental regulation that neither the defensive-investment nor the input-output literature has addressed.
nn_title: Residential choice, hedonic prices, and the demand for urban air quality
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: Does climate-induced physical risk capitalization in commercial real estate valuations differ systematically between REIT-held properties and privately-held comparable properties, and does this gap predict subsequent REIT return anomalies?
method: A two-stage empirical design combining hedonic pricing models applied to matched REIT-disclosed property-level appraisals and arm's-length private transaction prices to estimate a 'climate discount wedge,' followed by portfolio sorts and Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional regressions linking this wedge to future REIT risk-adjusted returns and bid-ask spreads.
data: NCREIF property-level appraisal and transaction data (1995–2023), REIT property-level disclosures from 10-K filings and SNL Real Estate, FEMA flood maps and NOAA storm-surge projections matched by parcel identifiers, and CRSP/Compustat for return and market microstructure variables.
key_finding: REIT-held properties are plausibly found to under-capitalize physical climate risk relative to private transactions by 8–15%, and REITs with the largest positive wedge (most under-priced risk) subsequently earn negative abnormal returns of 3–6% annually as climate information diffuses, suggesting a systematic mispricing channel tied to institutional appraisal conventions and investor inattention.
novelty_rationale: While prior work separately examines climate risk in housing markets or REIT performance, no study exploits the unique dual-market structure of REITs to identify an appraisal-based climate mispricing wedge and trace its asset-pricing consequences through return predictability and liquidity dynamics.
nn_title: Privately Versus Publicly Held Asset Investment Performance
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the announcement and construction of a new major airport or airport expansion causally reshape intra-metropolitan land use patterns, shifting employment and residential density away from legacy urban cores toward peri-urban airport corridors?
method: A staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting quasi-random variation in the timing of airport capacity expansion announcements across global metropolitan areas, combined with granular grid-cell-level satellite-derived nighttime luminosity and land-cover change data to trace spatial reallocation of economic activity at fine geographic resolution.
data: Global Airport Development database (2000–2023) matched to NASA VIIRS/DMSP nighttime lights at 500m resolution, ESA land-cover classifications, and parcel-level transaction records from 30+ metropolitan areas across the US, Europe, and Asia with documented major capacity expansions.
key_finding: Airport capacity expansions are likely to generate significant sub-metropolitan spatial reorganization—concentrating logistics, warehousing, and tradeable-service employment within 10–20 km of terminals while simultaneously depressing commercial real estate values and slowing employment growth in legacy CBDs, with effect magnitudes varying by pre-existing transit connectivity between the airport and the urban core.
novelty_rationale: While prior work (e.g., Brueckner 2003; Sheard 2014) focuses on airports as metro-level growth engines using aggregate outcomes, this idea pivots to intra-metropolitan spatial reallocation—a distinct and under-studied mechanism—using high-resolution satellite data and a global staggered design that moves beyond US-centric IV approaches.
nn_title: Airports and Economic Development
assigned_label: Urban population density modeling and estimation

research_question: Does algorithmic zoning enforcement—where municipal authorities use machine learning to automatically flag non-compliant land uses via satellite and permit data—systematically alter property values and development patterns differently across high- versus low-income neighborhoods?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of AI-assisted code enforcement systems across U.S. municipalities, combined with a hedonic property value model and spatial regression discontinuity at zoning boundaries to isolate causal effects on land prices, new construction permits, and informal land use.
data: Parcel-level transaction records from county assessor databases (2010–2024) merged with municipal building permit archives, satellite-derived land-use classifications from Planet Labs imagery, and a novel hand-collected dataset of algorithmic enforcement adoption dates across 120 U.S. cities obtained through FOIA requests.
key_finding: Algorithmic enforcement likely raises property values in high-income zones by credibly signaling land-use certainty, while simultaneously suppressing informal mixed-use activity and depressing land values in lower-income neighborhoods where non-conforming uses substitute for absent formal amenities, widening intra-city spatial inequality.
novelty_rationale: While development control has been studied qualitatively, no research has examined how the shift from discretionary to algorithmic enforcement changes the economic information content of zoning, creating a new and measurable form of regulatory technology shock with heterogeneous distributional consequences.
nn_title: Land use regulations and the value of land and housing: An intra-metropolitan an
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: How does the composition of heterogeneous trading populations — institutional investors, retail flippers, and long-term owner-occupiers — differentially bias appraisal-based return indices, and can a mixture-model deconvolution recover asset-class-level true volatility?
method: Develop a latent-class mixture model that jointly estimates trader-type-specific transaction price processes and appraisal smoothing parameters, using Bayesian MCMC to deconvolve observed index returns into subpopulation-specific return distributions and aggregate true volatility.
data: Property-level transaction records merged with deed-transfer and mortgage origination data from CoreLogic (2000–2023, nationwide U.S.), linked to NCREIF and CoStar appraisal histories to identify appraiser-assessor pairs and trading-entity type across approximately 40 million transactions.
key_finding: Trader-type composition shifts — such as the post-GFC surge in institutional single-family rentals or the 2020–2022 iBuyer expansion — mechanically alter measured index volatility by 20–45% independent of fundamental price risk, and standard unsmoothing corrections that ignore compositional change systematically mismeasure the true equity-real estate volatility ratio.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior unsmoothing work that models a single representative appraisal process or regime-switches on macro states, this approach explicitly attributes index distortion to endogenous shifts in who transacts, opening a new dimension of measurement bias tied to market microstructure composition rather than information frictions alone.
nn_title: A Measure of Fundamental Volatility in the Commercial Property Market
assigned_label: Real Estate Return Measurement and Volatility Estimation

research_question: How do cognitive biases in homebuyer perception of neighborhood amenities cause systematic deviations from rational hedonic equilibrium, and can these deviations be identified and measured using revealed versus stated preference comparisons?
method: A structural model combining revealed preference data (actual transaction prices) with stated preference data (conjoint survey experiments) to decompose observed hedonic price gradients into a rational valuation component and a bias component, identified via experimental variation in information salience across buyer cohorts.
data: Merged dataset of MLS transaction records (2010–2023) for 10 major U.S. metros linked to original buyer survey responses collected at closing, supplemented by randomized information-provision field experiments conducted in partnership with real estate brokerages.
key_finding: Buyers systematically overweight visually salient but transient amenities (e.g., seasonal greenery, newly painted facades) and underweight permanent structural depreciation, producing hedonic price gradients that diverge from welfare-consistent implicit prices by 8–20% depending on amenity category and buyer experience level.
novelty_rationale: Unlike existing hedonic studies that treat observed prices as rational equilibria or NLP studies that extract listing-text signals, this paper directly identifies and structurally decomposes behavioral distortions in implicit price formation by exploiting the revealed-versus-stated preference wedge, an approach absent from the residential hedonic literature.
nn_title: An alternative test of the hedonic theory of housing markets
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the optimal city size distribution — defined by aggregate welfare maximization accounting for agglomeration economies, congestion costs, and fiscal externalities — systematically diverge from the observed distribution, and if so, what spatial policies could close this gap?
method: A quantitative spatial equilibrium model is calibrated to match observed U.S. city-level wages, rents, amenity values, and public good provision, then solved for the welfare-maximizing city size distribution under a social planner who can use place-based transfers and zoning instruments, with the wedge between optimal and actual distributions decomposed into agglomeration, congestion, and fiscal sources.
data: Decennial Census and American Community Survey micro-data (1980–2020) merged with local government finance data from the Census of Governments, MSA-level productivity estimates from the BEA, and land-use regulatory indices from the Wharton Residential Land Use Regulatory Index, covering all U.S. MSAs.
key_finding: The observed U.S. city size distribution likely places too much population in very large cities relative to the social optimum when congestion and housing externalities are internalized, but too little when agglomeration spillovers dominate, suggesting the welfare-maximizing distribution is intermediate and achievable through coordinated zoning liberalization in mid-size cities.
novelty_rationale: Existing literature describes or statistically characterizes observed city size distributions (Zipf, mixture models, cross-sectional dynamics) but never solves for a normative benchmark distribution and quantifies the welfare cost of the deviation, bridging urban public economics with the distributional urban systems literature in a novel way.
nn_title: The optimal size distribution of cities
assigned_label: City size distribution and optimality

research_question: How do climate-induced amenity shocks propagate through the spatial equilibrium of polycentric urban systems, and do standard monocentric welfare measures systematically mis-state the distributional consequences for households sorted by skill and mobility?
method: A quantitative spatial general equilibrium model with multiple employment centers, heterogeneous workers (skill, mobility cost, climate risk tolerance), and endogenous housing supply is estimated via simulated method of moments and used to simulate counterfactual amenity degradation paths drawn from downscaled CMIP6 climate projections.
data: Linked Census-ACS microdata (2000–2023) on wages, rents, commuting, and household demographics combined with NOAA extreme-heat and flood-inundation records at the census-tract level across all US metropolitan statistical areas, supplemented by CoreLogic transaction-level housing price data.
key_finding: Monocentric partial-equilibrium welfare estimates are likely to understate aggregate losses by 20–45% and to reverse the sign of distributional effects for low-skill renters in secondary employment centers, because they cannot capture cross-center migration responses and endogenous wage compression that general equilibrium induces.
novelty_rationale: Existing spatial equilibrium work treats amenity shocks as static and studies monocentric cities; this paper is the first to embed dynamic, spatially differentiated climate trajectories into a fully estimated polycentric framework and to demonstrate that the choice of equilibrium concept—not just the climate scenario—drives welfare and distributional conclusions.
nn_title: Transportation costs and the rent gradient
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: How do unexpected firm relocations—where workers face an exogenous shift in workplace location—causally reshape residential location choices, commuting behavior, and housing prices in the surrounding submarkets?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting quasi-random variation in firm relocation events (identified through business registry data) matched to affected workers via employer-employee linked records, combined with a hedonic housing price model to trace spatial spillovers.
data: Employer-employee matched administrative data (e.g., Danish IDA or U.S. LEHD) linked to residential address histories, commercial real estate transaction records, and property tax assessment rolls covering major metropolitan areas over 1995–2022.
key_finding: Workers whose employers relocate farther from their residence are likely to sort toward new residences closer to the new workplace within 1–3 years, generating measurable capitalization effects (2–8% price appreciation) in destination neighborhoods and mild price declines near the original workplace cluster, with heterogeneous responses by homeownership status and household income.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats commute costs as endogenous preferences or studies self-selected telecommuters, this design uses firm relocations as a plausibly exogenous shock to identify the causal, dynamic feedback loop between workplace geography, residential sorting, and local housing markets—a mechanism that existing discrete-choice and telecommuting literatures leave unidentified.
nn_title: Wages, Housing Prices and Commutes
assigned_label: Residential Location Choice and Commuting Behavior

research_question: Does the targeting efficiency of housing voucher programs improve or deteriorate when landlord participation is endogenously determined by neighborhood rent dynamics and tenant screening incentives?
method: Structural model of two-sided matching between voucher-holding tenants and landlords, estimated via indirect inference using administrative voucher records linked to property transaction data, with counterfactual simulations of alternative subsidy designs (e.g., small-area fair market rents versus metropolitan-wide caps).
data: HUD administrative records on Housing Choice Voucher holders (2010–2023) linked to CoreLogic property transaction and rental listing data, supplemented by American Housing Survey landlord modules covering approximately 40 major metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Landlord opt-out behavior likely concentrates voucher holders in low-opportunity neighborhoods regardless of subsidy generosity, but small-area rent adjustments combined with landlord recruitment incentives could improve neighborhood sorting by 20–35% relative to current policy without increasing program cost.
novelty_rationale: Existing work treats landlord participation as exogenous or analyzes it descriptively; this paper endogenizes the supply side of the voucher market within a structural matching framework, revealing how landlord screening and rent-setting behavior systematically undermine targeting goals that subsidy-level analyses cannot detect.
nn_title: Housing demand and neighborhood choice with housing vouchers
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: How does the rise of omnichannel retailing—where tenants use physical store locations primarily as fulfillment and showroom nodes rather than direct sales points—distort traditional percentage rent contracts and alter the efficient lease structure in shopping centers?
method: A structural model of lease contract design combining mechanism design with an empirical estimation of in-store versus online sales substitution elasticities, using a two-stage approach where the theoretical optimal contract is derived under omnichannel demand and then estimated via GMM on observed lease terms and retailer sales data split by channel.
data: Proprietary lease abstracts from a major REIT covering 200+ U.S. shopping centers (2010–2023) matched to SKU-level point-of-sale and online order fulfillment records from participating tenants, supplemented by foot-traffic data from SafeGraph and e-commerce revenue disclosures from public retailers.
key_finding: Traditional percentage rent breakpoints tied solely to in-store gross sales are likely to significantly under-capture landlord value as omnichannel tenants shift revenue online while leveraging physical space for logistics and brand exposure, implying that renegotiated contracts incorporating blended omnichannel revenue thresholds could increase landlord rent capture by 15–35% without reducing tenant occupancy rates.
novelty_rationale: Existing theoretical work on percentage rents assumes a clean mapping between physical occupancy and tenant sales revenue, an assumption that omnichannel retailing fundamentally breaks; this paper is the first to derive and empirically test optimal lease structures under channel-split demand, introducing a new contracting friction with direct policy and valuation implications for the $2 trillion U.S. retail real estate sector.
nn_title: Percentage Rents with Agency
assigned_label: Commercial Real Estate Rent Dynamics

research_question: How does the expiration of low-income housing tax credit (LIHTC) affordability restrictions affect neighborhood-level rent levels, displacement of incumbent tenants, and long-term rental housing supply?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting the quasi-random timing of 30-year LIHTC compliance period expirations across properties, combined with a landlord survey to identify post-expiration repositioning strategies and investment decisions.
data: National Housing Preservation Database and HUD LIHTC property records linked to parcel-level rental listings from CoStar and Zillow (2000–2024), supplemented by Census tract-level American Community Survey displacement proxies covering all U.S. metropolitan areas.
