key_finding: LIHTC expiration likely triggers rent increases of 10–25% within two years and measurable outmigration of low-income households in tight markets, while oversupplied markets may see property conversion or deterioration rather than rent appreciation, revealing a policy-induced supply cliff whose magnitude varies sharply with local vacancy rates.
novelty_rationale: While LIHTC has been studied extensively at origination, the supply-side and neighborhood spillover consequences of mass affordability covenant expirations—a looming policy cliff affecting hundreds of thousands of units—remain almost entirely unexamined empirically, and the staggered expiration calendar provides clean identification unavailable to prior descriptive work.
nn_title: Do Tenants Capture the Benefits from the Low‐Income Housing Tax Credit Program?
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do algorithmic mortgage underwriting and automated valuation models perpetuate or attenuate racial residential segregation relative to human loan officers, and through what mechanisms?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered adoption of automated underwriting systems (AUS) by lenders, comparing loan approval rates, interest rate spreads, and subsequent neighborhood racial composition changes between borrowers evaluated by algorithms versus human underwriters, instrumented by lender-level AUS rollout timing.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) records (1990–2023) merged with lender-level AUS adoption dates from bank call reports and regulatory filings, linked to Census tract racial composition from decennial censuses and ACS, covering approximately 200 million loan applications nationally.
key_finding: Algorithmic underwriting likely reduces explicit racial steering in high-scrutiny markets but may entrench segregation in lower-income markets by systematically downgrading credit scores and collateral valuations in minority-majority tracts, producing heterogeneous effects that depend on the training data vintage and local housing market liquidity.
novelty_rationale: Whereas prior segregation research focuses on human prejudice and demographic preferences as drivers, this idea isolates a novel institutional mechanism—algorithmic decision-making—whose role in reproducing or disrupting racialized spatial equilibria is theoretically ambiguous and empirically untested at scale.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does the expansion of remote work opportunities differentially reduce spatial mismatch penalties for Black workers by decoupling residential location from job accessibility, and does this effect vary by occupational skill tier and metropolitan racial segregation levels?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the quasi-exogenous shock of COVID-19-induced remote work adoption, using industry-level remote work feasibility scores interacted with pre-pandemic residential segregation indices to identify heterogeneous employment and wage effects across racial groups within metropolitan areas.
data: American Community Survey (2017–2023) linked with O*NET telework feasibility scores, LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics for commute-shed measurement, and HUD dissimilarity indices for 150+ metropolitan areas, supplemented by LinkedIn job posting data tracking remote-eligible vacancy postings by location and skill level.
key_finding: Remote work expansion likely narrows Black–white employment gaps most substantially in highly segregated metros and for mid-skill occupations, but may paradoxically widen gaps in low-skill sectors where telework is infeasible, suggesting that digitization of work restructures rather than eliminates spatial mismatch along new occupational fault lines.
novelty_rationale: While spatial mismatch literature has focused exclusively on physical job-worker proximity, this idea reconceptualizes the mismatch mechanism itself—asking whether the geography of work dissolution via remote work creates a fundamentally new channel through which racial employment inequality is either alleviated or restructured, a question prior literature cannot address because it predates mass telework adoption.
nn_title: Can Black workers escape spatial mismatch? Employment shifts, population shifts,
assigned_label: Spatial mismatch and racial employment inequality

research_question: Do transit-oriented development zoning reforms amplify or dampen the heterogeneous capitalization effects of new rail infrastructure across neighborhoods segmented by pre-existing land-use rigidity?
method: A staggered difference-in-differences design that interacts rail station opening events with a continuous measure of pre-reform zoning restrictiveness, instrumenting for zoning reform timing using state-level preemption legislation as an exogenous shifter to isolate causal mechanisms from endogenous upzoning decisions.
data: Parcel-level transaction records from CoreLogic (2000–2023) merged with digitized historical zoning ordinance archives from 40 U.S. metropolitan areas, GTFS transit feeds for precise station-opening dates, and NLCD land-cover data to measure pre-treatment development intensity.
key_finding: Neighborhoods with highly restrictive pre-reform zoning likely show suppressed short-run capitalization gains that subsequently surge post-upzoning, while flexible-zoning neighborhoods capitalize rail access immediately but plateau earlier, implying that zoning rigidity—not rail proximity per se—is the binding constraint on welfare distribution.
novelty_rationale: Existing capitalization studies treat the regulatory environment as a background condition rather than an active moderator; this paper is the first to identify zoning reform as the mechanism that converts latent rail accessibility into realized price and welfare effects, using legislative preemption as a novel instrument.
nn_title: Redevelopment along arterial streets: The effects of light rail on land use chan
assigned_label: Transit infrastructure capitalization in urban housing

research_question: How do cordon-based congestion pricing schemes affect residential sorting and long-run housing price gradients within and across pricing zone boundaries in major metropolitan areas?
method: Spatial regression discontinuity design exploiting sharp geographic boundaries of congestion pricing zones, combined with a dynamic equilibrium sorting model to decompose capitalization effects from household relocation responses over short- and long-run horizons.
data: Hedonic panel data on residential transactions from Stockholm, London, Milan, and Singapore congestion pricing schemes (1990–2023), merged with household-level commuting records, vehicle registration data, and high-frequency road sensor counts to instrument for actual travel-time savings.
key_finding: Congestion pricing zones are plausibly expected to generate discontinuous housing price premiums of 3–12% just inside the cordon boundary for high-income households while simultaneously depressing values in low-income neighborhoods just outside the boundary due to traffic displacement, implying regressive spatial welfare effects that offset aggregate efficiency gains by 20–40%.
novelty_rationale: Whereas prior work treats congestion pricing purely as a flow-management or welfare-efficiency problem, this idea reconceptualizes it as an endogenous land-use instrument whose general-equilibrium effects on residential sorting and spatial inequality have been almost entirely unexplored empirically across multiple comparable policy experiments.
nn_title: Indivisibilities and interplant transportation cost: Do they cause market breakd
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: How do large-scale climate shocks — specifically repeated flooding events — alter the persistent migration equilibria documented in the literature, and do they permanently redirect population flows or merely produce temporary displacement followed by return migration?
method: A dynamic difference-in-differences design exploiting quasi-random variation in flood inundation boundaries across U.S. counties (using FEMA 100-year floodplain maps as an instrument), combined with a structural model of sequential household location choice that allows equilibrium multiplicity and path dependence to distinguish permanent flow redirection from transient displacement.
data: Linked administrative records from the IRS county-to-county migration files (1990–2023), FEMA National Flood Insurance Program claims data, NOAA storm event databases, and CoreLogic deed and mortgage records providing property-level transaction histories and repeat-sale prices across all U.S. counties.
key_finding: Flood events above a severity threshold are plausibly found to permanently redirect persistent migration corridors — particularly away from coastal Sun Belt destinations — whereas moderate flooding reinforces incumbency bias and return migration, implying a non-linear tipping-point mechanism that existing models of migration persistence have not captured.
novelty_rationale: While prior work documents why migration patterns are persistent and models household optimization, no study has endogenously connected climate-induced shocks to the structural mechanisms sustaining migration equilibria, nor tested whether environmental stress can permanently dislodge entrenched population flow paths rather than simply shifting levels.
nn_title: Household migration: Theoretical and empirical results
assigned_label: Residential mobility and migration determinants

research_question: How does the spatial mismatch between remote-work eligibility and housing affordability reshape residential location choices and long-run neighborhood sorting across U.S. metropolitan areas in the post-pandemic era?
method: A quantitative spatial equilibrium model with heterogeneous households differentiated by remote-work eligibility, skill level, and family structure, estimated via simulated method of moments using pre- and post-2020 variation in remote-work adoption as a quasi-natural experiment, with counterfactual simulations tracing welfare and sorting effects.
data: Linked American Community Survey (2015–2023) household-level microdata on residential location, commuting mode, and occupation merged with O*NET remote-work feasibility scores, Zillow transaction-level housing prices, and the Stanford Remote Work Index covering 50 major MSAs.
key_finding: Remote-work eligibility is plausibly found to function as a shadow amenity worth 8–15% of household income in location utility, generating first-order re-sorting of high-skill households toward lower-density, lower-cost metros while amplifying housing price appreciation in amenity-rich secondary cities and deepening spatial income stratification between remote-eligible and ineligible households within superstar cities.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior location-choice models that treat workplace location as exogenous or fixed, this paper endogenizes the remote-work margin as a continuous household-level amenity and quantifies its role in disrupting the classic wage-rent arbitrage mechanism that anchors both monocentric urban theory and dual-earner location models.
nn_title: Commuting with Lost Housing Services as the Opportunity Cost
assigned_label: Household residential location and mobility decisions

research_question: How do real-time ambient population flows—captured via mobile device telemetry—diverge from residential density gradients, and what does this divergence reveal about the true productive agglomeration surface of cities?
method: A structural urban model that jointly estimates daytime and nighttime density gradients using a mixture of negative exponential and power-law kernels, identified through generalized method of moments with instrumental variables for endogenous land-use zoning, and validated against establishment-level productivity microdata.
data: Anonymized hourly GPS/cell-tower mobility data from a major telecom provider covering 15–20 large cities across three continents (2018–2024), merged with decennial census residential counts, commercial parcel records, and firm-level revenue data from administrative tax registries.
key_finding: Ambient population gradients are plausibly 30–60% flatter than residential gradients in service-dominant cities and exhibit polycentric spikes absent in residential surfaces, implying that standard residential-based density models systematically understate suburbanization of economic activity and mismeasure agglomeration economies by a similar margin.
novelty_rationale: Existing density gradient literature is anchored exclusively to residential census counts; this idea fundamentally reframes the object of estimation as a time-varying ambient density surface, opening a new empirical bridge between urban form and the actual spatial distribution of economic activity.
nn_title: Some tests of alternative urban population density functions
assigned_label: Urban population density modeling and estimation

research_question: How does the spatial mismatch between formal employment subcenters and informal housing clusters shape long-run productivity convergence across neighborhoods within rapidly growing cities in Sub-Saharan Africa?
method: A dynamic spatial equilibrium model is estimated using panel difference-in-differences, exploiting quasi-random variation in road infrastructure expansions as instruments for subcenter formation to identify causal effects of spatial (mis)alignment on neighborhood-level productivity trajectories.
data: High-resolution satellite imagery (2000–2023) combined with geolocated firm census records, mobile phone mobility data, and household survey panels from Nairobi, Dar es Salaam, Lagos, and Accra, covering over 500 distinct neighborhoods per city.
key_finding: Neighborhoods where informal residential clusters are located more than 5 km from the nearest formal employment subcenter are expected to show 15–30% lower decadal income growth, with infrastructure investments that reduce effective distance generating convergence effects roughly twice as large as comparable investments in already well-connected areas.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that characterizes Latin American or developed-country urban structures statically, this idea jointly models the endogenous co-evolution of informality and subcenter formation in African cities, using mobility big data to reveal revealed commuting geographies that traditional surveys cannot capture, and derives welfare implications from a micro-founded spatial equilibrium framework rather than reduced-form correlations.
nn_title: Malthus living in a slum: Urban concentration, infrastructure and economic growt
assigned_label: Firm location decisions and agglomeration economies

research_question: Does the sequencing of public infrastructure investment relative to private capital entry determine whether neighborhood renewal produces inclusive upgrading or rapid displacement of incumbent low-income residents?
method: A dynamic difference-in-differences design exploiting quasi-random variation in the temporal order of transit expansions, park improvements, and anchor institution investments across renewal zones, combined with a structural model of household sorting to decompose welfare effects by income quintile.
data: Parcel-level administrative records on building permits, property transactions, and assessed values linked to longitudinal household-level data from the American Community Survey and HUD administrative files across 40 U.S. cities from 2000–2022, augmented with geocoded infrastructure project timelines from municipal capital improvement plans.
key_finding: Infrastructure-first sequencing is likely to produce a 15–30% larger displacement effect among renters than private-capital-first sequencing, but may also generate 20–40% greater long-run appreciation that benefits remaining homeowners, revealing a fundamental equity-efficiency tradeoff governed by sequencing rather than renewal intensity alone.
novelty_rationale: While prior work treats renewal as a uniform treatment, this paper reframes the causal question around investment sequencing as the key policy-lever, introducing a temporal ordering dimension entirely absent from the existing gentrification literature and offering actionable guidance for municipal capital planning.
nn_title: Segregation and Tiebout sorting: The link between place-based investments and ne
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Do firms strategically co-locate with financially distressed neighbors to acquire agglomeration externalities at lower real estate costs, and does this fire-sale co-location produce differential long-run productivity gains compared to co-location in healthy clusters?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting plausibly exogenous mass-layoff events and anchor-firm bankruptcies as shocks that create localized distress vacancies, instrumenting for entrant co-location decisions with pre-shock zoning density and lease-expiration cycles to identify causal productivity and investment effects on new co-locators versus matched entrants in non-distressed clusters.
data: Linked employer-employee records from the U.S. Longitudinal Business Database merged with CoStar commercial lease and vacancy data (2000–2022), Compustat for public-firm financials, and PACER bankruptcy filings to identify anchor-firm distress events at the establishment level across all U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Entrants exploiting distress-driven vacancies in otherwise productive clusters are likely to exhibit 8–15% higher TFP growth over five years relative to greenfield entrants, but only when the distressed anchor exits cleanly rather than lingering in zombie status, suggesting that the quality of cluster restructuring—not merely low entry costs—determines whether opportunistic co-location converts agglomeration potential into realized productivity gains.
novelty_rationale: Existing agglomeration research treats cluster entry as driven by productivity-seeking behavior in healthy environments; this idea reframes co-location as a dynamic, counter-cyclical arbitrage strategy where financial distress reshapes the spatial opportunity set, introducing a supply-side real-estate mechanism that is entirely absent from canonical agglomeration models.
nn_title: Urban Vibrancy and Corporate Growth
assigned_label: Firm location decisions and agglomeration economies

research_question: Does proximity to high-frequency rail infrastructure create discontinuous manufacturing productivity gains at station boundaries, and have these gains eroded as logistics technology has democratized access to distant markets?
method: Spatial regression discontinuity design exploiting sharp catchment boundaries around high-speed rail stations, combined with a difference-in-differences framework comparing manufacturing plant productivity before and after rail network expansions across multiple decades to identify both the level effect and its secular decline.
data: Longitudinal plant-level manufacturing census microdata from China's Annual Survey of Industrial Firms (1998–2019) and Japan's Census of Manufactures (1985–2020), merged with georeferenced high-speed and conventional rail network shapefiles and firm-level freight cost records, covering hundreds of thousands of establishments within 50 km of station boundaries.
key_finding: Rail proximity likely confers a 6–14% total factor productivity premium for manufacturing plants within optimal catchment zones, but this premium is plausibly shrinking at roughly 0.4–0.8 percentage points per decade as e-logistics platforms, third-party warehousing, and last-mile automation reduce the agglomeration rent tied to fixed transport nodes.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats transport infrastructure as uniformly productivity-enhancing, this idea explicitly models the obsolescence trajectory of location-specific rail rents using a sharp geographic discontinuity design, bridging the economic geography of fixed infrastructure with the industrial-organization literature on logistics technology adoption.
nn_title: Urban spatial structure and productivity growth in the manufacturing sector of c
assigned_label: Transit infrastructure capitalization in urban housing

research_question: How do inclusionary zoning mandates affect the spatial distribution of new market-rate housing construction and neighborhood-level property values through developer avoidance and displacement of supply?
method: A spatial difference-in-differences design exploiting staggered adoption of inclusionary zoning ordinances across California municipalities, combined with a structural model of developer location choice to separate the direct price capitalization effect from the supply-displacement spillover effect on adjacent unregulated jurisdictions.
data: Parcel-level permit records from the California Department of Housing and Community Development (2000–2022) merged with CoreLogic deed-transfer and assessed-value data, municipal boundary shapefiles, and ordinance adoption dates collected from city codes via systematic legal database searches across 482 California cities.
key_finding: Inclusionary zoning mandates are likely to reduce permitted units within adopting jurisdictions by 15–25% while generating positive price spillovers in neighboring unregulated cities, implying that regional welfare costs substantially exceed single-jurisdiction cost-benefit analyses and that the affordable units produced may be more than offset by market-rate supply destruction.
novelty_rationale: Existing studies treat inclusionary zoning effects as locally contained, but this design uniquely quantifies the inter-jurisdictional supply-displacement externality and uses a structural location-choice model to disentangle developer avoidance from demand-side price effects, yielding a regionally consistent welfare estimate that prior reduced-form work cannot identify.
nn_title: Free riders and distortionary zoning by local communities
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Does the expansion of remote work technology permanently compress spatial wage gradients by decoupling labor productivity from place-based agglomeration economies, and if so, which worker skill segments experience the largest wage convergence?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting the sudden, heterogeneous adoption of broadband infrastructure upgrades across commuting zones as an exogenous shifter of remote-work feasibility, combined with an equilibrium spatial sorting model to decompose observed wage changes into amenity-capitalization, productivity, and monopsony-rent channels.
data: Longitudinal matched employer-employee records from the U.S. Longitudinal Employer-Household Dynamics (LEHD) dataset covering 2010–2023, merged with FCC broadband deployment maps, O*NET remote-work task indices, and ACS migration flows at the commuting-zone level.
key_finding: Remote-work capacity is plausibly found to compress the urban–rural wage premium by 8–18 percent for high-skill tradable occupations while leaving low-skill in-person service wages largely unaffected, implying that spatial wage inequality narrows at the top of the distribution but widens relative to non-remote workers who remain tied to thin local labor markets.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work on inter-city wage differentials driven by amenities such as air quality or education mismatch, this idea treats remote-work technology as a structural shock that endogenously re-weights the role of physical agglomeration in wage formation, requiring a new decomposition framework that separates place-based productivity rents from worker-level sorting effects under partial geographic detachment.
nn_title: Wage inequality and the location of cities
assigned_label: Spatial labor markets, wages, and worker mobility

research_question: How do place-based sectoral shocks propagate differentially across metropolitan labor markets depending on the structure of local occupational networks, and does occupational relatedness buffer or amplify employment volatility relative to industry diversification alone?
method: Construct a metropolitan-level occupational relatedness matrix using O*NET skill-distance measures, then estimate a spatial panel model with Bartik-style shift-share instruments for sector-specific demand shocks, interacting shock exposure with network centrality and clustering coefficients of each metro's occupational graph to identify heterogeneous propagation effects.
data: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) for industry-level employment covering all U.S. metros from 2000–2023, merged with O*NET occupational characteristics and the American Community Survey occupation-by-industry matrices to construct time-varying metropolitan occupational network structures.
key_finding: Metropolitan areas with denser and more interconnected occupational networks are likely to exhibit faster within-metro labor reallocation following sectoral shocks, resulting in 20–40% lower peak unemployment relative to metros of similar industrial diversity but sparser occupational linkages, suggesting occupational topology is an independent resilience mechanism beyond standard diversification indices.
novelty_rationale: Existing shift-share and agglomeration studies treat industry mix or urbanization economies as the primary unit of analysis, but this idea reframes metropolitan resilience through the lens of occupational network topology — an approach that leverages graph-theoretic methods and granular skills data to identify a previously unmeasured structural determinant of how employment shocks propagate and self-correct within labor markets.
nn_title: Do labor market networks have an important spatial dimension?
assigned_label: Spatial labor markets, wages, and worker mobility

research_question: How does the spatial mismatch between jurisdictional fragmentation and functional metropolitan boundaries distort aggregate housing supply elasticity estimates, and does consolidating supply-side decision-making within economically integrated labor market areas substantially alter measured price responsiveness?
method: A two-stage instrumental variables approach that first constructs boundary-corrected supply elasticity measures by aggregating parcel-level permit, zoning, and land availability data to commuting zone boundaries (rather than political jurisdictions), then exploits quasi-random variation in mid-century municipal annexation and incorporation events as instruments for jurisdictional fragmentation to identify its causal effect on supply responsiveness.
data: Merged dataset combining the Census Bureau's Building Permits Survey (1980–2023) at the place level, IPUMS commuting zone crosswalks, historical annexation records from the Boundary and Annexation Survey, parcel-level zoning data from the National Zoning Atlas, and CoreLogic deed and assessment records covering roughly 3,100 counties nationwide.
key_finding: Jurisdictional fragmentation likely suppresses measured metropolitan supply elasticity by 20–45 percent relative to commuting-zone-corrected estimates, with the distortion largest in polycentric metros where bedroom communities systematically underprovide housing while benefiting from neighboring employment centers, implying that cross-city comparisons in the existing literature systematically misattribute supply inelasticity to topography or regulation when political coordination failures are the primary driver.
novelty_rationale: Existing supply elasticity research treats the metropolitan statistical area or county as the natural unit of analysis without questioning whether the political unit matches the economic unit of housing production decisions; this paper directly challenges that conflation using a novel boundary-correction methodology and a credible historical instrument for fragmentation that has not previously been applied to housing supply questions.
nn_title: Why we still don't know much about housing supply elasticity
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: How does the transition from defined-benefit to defined-contribution retirement plans affect mortgage leverage choices, refinancing behavior, and default risk across the life cycle?
method: Regression discontinuity and instrumental variable design exploiting firm-level pension plan freezes as quasi-exogenous shocks to future income certainty, combined with a life-cycle structural model that jointly estimates mortgage demand and retirement saving decisions.
data: Linked employer-employee pension records from the Form 5500 filings merged with Equifax/FRBNY Consumer Credit Panel mortgage tradelines and CoreLogic loan-level origination data covering 1995–2022, with geographic and industry variation in DB-to-DC conversion timing.
key_finding: Households exposed to pension freezes are likely to select lower LTV mortgages and refinance less aggressively to build home equity as a precautionary buffer, but higher-income households may instead increase leverage to offset reduced pension wealth, generating heterogeneous default risk profiles that existing underwriting models systematically misprice.
novelty_rationale: While prior work treats income uncertainty and mortgage choice largely in isolation, this idea explicitly connects the secular shift in retirement income risk to mortgage market dynamics, offering a novel channel through which labor market institutions shape household balance sheets and systemic mortgage risk.
nn_title: Housing leverage, home value, and retirement
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: How does the spatial diffusion of remote work adoption reshape the geography of housing price gradients, and does it permanently alter the classical monocentric city model's predictions about distance-to-CBD price decay?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting cross-industry and cross-occupation variation in remote-work feasibility (using Dingel-Neiman teleworkability scores) interacted with pre-pandemic commuting zone employment shares as an instrument for local remote-work adoption intensity, embedded within a structural monocentric urban model that allows for endogenous amenity re-pricing across the distance gradient.
data: Repeat-sales house price indices constructed from CoreLogic deed-transfer records at the census-tract level (2015–2024) merged with the American Community Survey workplace-location flows, Burning Glass Technologies remote job postings, and broadband availability maps from the FCC Form 477, covering approximately 70,000 census tracts across 150 metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Metros where remote-work adoption was most intensive are likely to exhibit a statistically significant flattening of the price-distance gradient of 15–35%, with suburban and exurban tracts capturing disproportionate price appreciation, while the effect persists post-2022 only in metros with sufficient broadband infrastructure and amenity endowments, suggesting a structural rather than transitory shift in urban spatial equilibrium.
novelty_rationale: Unlike existing work that documents aggregate urban-to-suburban price shifts, this idea structurally identifies whether remote work has permanently broken the monocentric equilibrium by estimating gradient elasticities rather than level effects, using a credible shift-share instrument that cleanly separates remote-work supply from confounding demand shocks such as pandemic-era preferences for space.
nn_title: The remote work revolution: Impact on real estate values and the urban environme
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: Does exposure to climate risk information — specifically the release of granular flood risk scores at the property level — causally shift household tenure choice away from homeownership in high-risk areas, and does this effect differ systematically by household wealth and financial literacy?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of First Street Foundation's property-level Flood Factor scores across U.S. counties, comparing tenure transitions of households in newly-scored high-risk parcels against comparable low-risk parcels before and after score publication, instrumented by distance to FEMA flood zone boundaries to isolate information shocks from underlying risk exposure.
data: American Housing Survey panels (2015–2023) merged with First Street Foundation Flood Factor scores, FEMA National Flood Insurance Program policy records, CoreLogic deed-transfer data, and FINRA Financial Capability Study waves to construct wealth and financial literacy proxies at the ZIP-code level.
key_finding: Property-level climate risk disclosure is expected to reduce homeownership propensity by 4–9 percentage points in high-risk zones, with the largest shifts among lower-wealth, lower-financial-literacy households who previously lacked access to granular risk information, potentially accelerating socioeconomic sorting across climate risk gradients.
novelty_rationale: While prior work examines how flood risk affects home prices, no study causally links the arrival of standardized, property-level climate risk scores to household tenure choice decisions, nor dissects the heterogeneous information-processing role of financial literacy in moderating this behavioral response.
nn_title: When climate meets real estate: A survey of the literature
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: How does the transition from renting to homeownership affect household portfolio diversification and long-run wealth inequality, conditional on the timing of entry relative to local house price cycles?
method: A structural life-cycle model with endogenous tenure choice, risky housing, and a stock market asset, estimated via simulated method of moments and supplemented by a difference-in-differences design exploiting quasi-random variation in local mortgage credit supply shocks as instruments for entry timing.
data: Linked administrative wealth registry and tax records from Sweden and Denmark (1995–2022), combined with granular municipality-level house price indices and bank-level mortgage origination data, providing full household balance-sheet coverage across multiple price cycles.
key_finding: Households who enter homeownership near cyclical peaks accumulate 15–30% less net wealth over a 20-year horizon than otherwise identical early-cycle entrants, with the wealth penalty concentrated among low-income, low-financial-literacy households who simultaneously crowd out stock market participation, amplifying rather than reducing wealth inequality.
novelty_rationale: Unlike existing life-cycle housing models that treat entry timing as exogenous or focus solely on mortgage type choice, this paper endogenizes the interaction between house price cycle positioning, portfolio crowding-out, and long-run wealth inequality using uniquely comprehensive Scandinavian administrative data that link housing transactions to complete household balance sheets across two full price cycles.
nn_title: Limited Stock Market Participation Among Renters and Homeowners
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Does algorithmic mortgage pricing by FinTech lenders reduce or perpetuate racial and income-based disparities in mortgage rates compared to human loan officers, and does any residual disparity operate through observable risk factors versus unexplained channels?
method: Decomposition analysis (Blinder-Oaxaca and causal forest) applied to matched loan-level data, exploiting the sharp discontinuity in lender type assignment created by GSE automated underwriting score thresholds to separate algorithmic from discretionary pricing decisions.
data: HMDA loan-level origination records merged with CFPB complaint data and proprietary lender technology classification data (2010–2023), supplemented by CoreLogic servicing data to track post-origination performance across roughly 50 million mortgages.
key_finding: Algorithmic lenders likely compress unexplained racial pricing gaps relative to traditional lenders but may amplify income-driven disparities by encoding socioeconomic features correlated with race into objective risk metrics, yielding a partial displacement rather than elimination of discriminatory outcomes.
novelty_rationale: While prior work compares FinTech and traditional lenders on speed and default rates, no study structurally separates the algorithmic pricing mechanism from lender selection effects to identify whether automation genuinely neutralizes loan-officer discretion or merely launders discrimination through risk-factor encoding.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: How does local mortgage market concentration — measured by lender Herfindahl-Hirschman Index at the county level — affect mortgage pricing spreads, credit rationing, and loan terms differentially across metropolitan boom-bust cycles compared to stable regional economies?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting staggered bank merger events as quasi-exogenous shocks to local lender concentration, combined with a structural model of spatial competition to decompose pricing effects into market-power and information-advantage channels.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) loan-level records merged with FDIC Summary of Deposits data for lender market shares, CoreLogic deed and foreclosure records, and FHFA house price indices covering all U.S. counties from 2000–2023.
key_finding: Plausible outcomes range from lender concentration raising mortgage spreads by 15–40 basis points in stable markets but compressing them in boom regions where origination volume incentivizes entry, suggesting concentration effects on credit access are procyclical and geographically heterogeneous.
novelty_rationale: While prior work examines either spatial heterogeneity in terminations or broad regional growth disparities, no study explicitly identifies how lender market power interacts with the local business cycle to jointly determine mortgage pricing and credit allocation across diverse regional economies.
nn_title: Lending competition, regulation, and nontraditional mortgages
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: How does the local concentration of mortgage lending institutions — measured by lender market structure (HHI) at the county level — affect loan pricing, approval rates, and terms for borrowers across the income distribution, and does fintech entry discipline incumbent lenders?
method: Difference-in-differences and instrumental variable estimation exploiting staggered fintech lender entry across counties as quasi-exogenous shocks to local mortgage market competition, combined with a structural model of lender pricing to decompose market power from risk-based pricing.
data: HMDA loan-level records (2010–2023) merged with county-level lender concentration measures, FFIEC CRA data, fintech licensing records, and Census tract socioeconomic covariates covering all U.S. counties.
key_finding: Markets with high incumbent lender concentration likely exhibit 20–50 basis point rate premiums and meaningfully lower approval rates for moderate-income borrowers, with fintech entry partially but heterogeneously eroding these premiums depending on broadband penetration and borrower digital literacy.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work treating mortgage markets as nationally integrated or focusing solely on loan terms descriptively, this paper jointly identifies the causal effect of local lender market power and tests a specific disciplining mechanism — fintech entry — using a credible quasi-experiment, bridging industrial organization and housing finance literatures.
nn_title: The Role of Technology in Mortgage Lending
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: How do climate-related physical risks — specifically flood and wildfire exposure of underlying collateral — alter the prepayment and default option values embedded in agency MBS, and do current pricing models systematically misprice securities backed by climate-vulnerable mortgages?
method: A structural contingent-claims model is augmented with a stochastic collateral-value process that incorporates spatially-resolved climate hazard shocks (estimated from FEMA flood maps and USFS wildfire probability grids), and its pricing predictions are tested against observed agency MBS pool-level yield spreads using a panel regression with pool fixed effects and vintage-by-coupon controls.
data: Loan-level data from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac single-family disclosure files (2000–2023) merged with FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer, First Street Foundation flood/wildfire risk scores, CoreLogic property-level valuations, and Bloomberg agency MBS pool pricing data covering roughly 80 million loans.
key_finding: Securities backed by high-climate-risk collateral are plausibly mispriced by 10–40 basis points relative to model-implied spreads, with mispricing widening post-2017 major disaster events, suggesting markets have begun incorporating but not fully pricing transition and physical climate risk into MBS valuations.
novelty_rationale: Existing MBS pricing models treat collateral value shocks as diffusion or jump processes calibrated to historical house-price data, entirely ignoring the geographically concentrated, forward-looking, and correlated nature of climate hazard risk, making this the first structural pricing framework to endogenize climate exposure as a distinct state variable in the prepayment-default option model.
nn_title: Climate risk in mortgage markets: Evidence from Hurricanes Harvey and Irma
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does mandatory risk retention under the Dodd-Frank qualified residential mortgage (QRM) rule alter the composition and renegotiation behavior of mortgage-backed security pools differentially across servicer contract structures, and does this effect vary with servicer skin-in-the-game versus originator skin-in-the-game?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered implementation and exemption thresholds of the 2014 QRM rule, combined with a structural model of servicer effort that separately identifies vertical (pro-rata) versus horizontal (first-loss) retention forms and their interaction with post-default modification rates, prepayment speeds, and realized loss severities.
data: Loan-level RMBS data from CoreLogic and Bloomberg covering 2010–2023, merged with SEC Form ABS-15G risk-retention disclosures, BlackBox Logic servicer performance records, and HMDA originator characteristics, yielding approximately 8 million non-agency loans with identified retention structure and servicer identity.
key_finding: Originators holding vertical risk retention tranches are likely to produce pools with significantly lower modification rates and higher loss severities than servicers with explicit first-loss positions, suggesting that retention form — not merely retention quantity — is the binding incentive mechanism, with welfare-relevant implications for optimal regulatory design.
novelty_rationale: While prior theory establishes that servicer vertical positions dominate horizontal positions for incentive alignment, no empirical study has used the post-Dodd-Frank natural experiment to jointly test how mandated retention form interacts with servicer contract structure to produce heterogeneous real outcomes across pool types and economic cycles.
nn_title: Servicer Contracts and the Design of Mortgage‐Backed Security Pools
assigned_label: Securitization, Risk Retention, and Intermediation

research_question: Does the racial composition of a borrower's neighborhood moderate the likelihood that servicers extend forbearance versus initiating foreclosure during periods of widespread mortgage distress, and does this differential treatment amplify long-run racial wealth gaps?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of COVID-19 CARES Act forbearance take-up across ZIP codes, combined with a causal forest machine-learning estimator to detect heterogeneous treatment effects across borrower race, servicer type (bank vs. non-bank), and GSE versus non-agency loan status, while instrumenting for forbearance uptake using local broadband access as a proxy for awareness of relief options.
data: Merged dataset combining HMDA loan-level origination records (2018–2024) with Black Knight McDash performance data covering ~30 million active mortgages, Census tract racial composition, FHFA servicer identifiers, and county-level unemployment shocks from BLS, providing nationwide coverage across the full COVID-19 distress cycle.
key_finding: Servicers are plausibly found to offer forbearance at significantly lower rates in majority-minority ZIP codes conditional on equivalent delinquency severity, with the gap being largest among non-bank servicers and non-agency loans, translating into measurable reductions in home-equity retention and long-run credit score recovery for Black and Hispanic borrowers.
novelty_rationale: While prior work treats servicer forbearance as uniform or examines race in origination contexts, this paper is the first to use causal inference methods on high-frequency loan-performance data to identify racially disparate loss-mitigation decisions at the servicer level and trace their consequences for the racial wealth gap, bridging mortgage default economics with structural inequality research.
nn_title: Mortgage Modification and Strategic Behavior: Evidence from a Legal Settlement w
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: How does the introduction of income-contingent mortgage contracts—where monthly payments automatically adjust as a share of verified borrower income—affect default rates, welfare, and lender profitability compared to fixed-payment and ARM alternatives?
method: A structural dynamic model of household mortgage choice and default under stochastic income and house-price processes is calibrated and solved, with a counterfactual policy simulation comparing equilibrium outcomes under income-contingent contracts against the existing menu of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, followed by empirical validation using Danish administrative matched employer-employee-mortgage registry data where income is measured at annual frequency for the full borrower population.
data: Danish administrative registers linking individual tax records (annual labor income, 1995–2023), mortgage registry data (origination terms, payment history, default events), and property transaction prices from Statistics Denmark, covering approximately 1.2 million active mortgages with near-universal population coverage.
key_finding: Income-contingent contracts are likely to reduce default rates by 25–45% for households in the bottom income-volatility quartile while generating adverse selection among high-income-volatility borrowers who self-select into these contracts, implying that offering them alongside traditional products widens rather than narrows credit risk segmentation unless bundled with appropriate pricing spreads.
novelty_rationale: Unlike existing work on prepayment penalties, points, or deferred amortization, this idea targets the largely unexplored design space of state-contingent payment streams tied to realized income, combining a novel contract form with the rare population-level administrative data necessary to identify income-volatility heterogeneity and its interaction with mortgage performance.
nn_title: A Model of Mortgage Default
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does carbon-transition risk exposure of commercial tenants cause differential capitalization into office and industrial REIT valuations, and do option markets price this tenant-level climate risk?
method: Cross-sectional asset pricing analysis of REIT returns regressed on tenant portfolio carbon intensity (weighted by lease revenue), combined with options-market analysis of put protection costs for REITs with high versus low carbon-intensive tenant bases, following the carbon tail risk framework applied to real estate.
data: MSCI ESG carbon emissions data matched to REIT tenant rosters from CoStar and SEC 10-K filings, REIT equity options data from OptionMetrics (2010–2023), and NCREIF property-level data for private-market benchmarks.
key_finding: REITs with carbon-intensive tenant bases likely face higher downside tail risk premiums in options markets and lower equity valuations, with costs rising following major climate policy announcements, suggesting a distinct tenant-level transmission channel from carbon risk to commercial real estate pricing.
novelty_rationale: Existing literature links climate risk to physical property damage or flood zones but ignores the lease-revenue transmission of tenants' carbon-transition risk to landlords, combining options-market methodology with tenant-portfolio carbon exposure in a way not previously applied to real estate.
nn_title: Carbon Tail Risk
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How do climate system inertia and the long-run discount rates embedded in housing markets inform optimal timing of residential energy retrofit mandates?
method: Asset pricing term structure model estimated from long-horizon rent-to-price ratios across U.S. coastal and inland MSAs, following the climate-change-and-long-run-discount-rates approach, to recover implicit discount rates for deferred climate damages and simulate the welfare cost of delaying retrofit standards.
data: American Housing Survey and Zillow rent and price data at MSA level (1990–2023), EPA building energy performance data, and FEMA National Flood Insurance Program loss histories for coastal markets.
key_finding: Housing market discount rates likely exhibit a downward-sloping term structure for climate-exposed properties, implying retrofit mandates delayed even five years impose measurable welfare losses concentrated in low-income coastal neighborhoods, providing a housing-market-based argument for urgency in energy standards.
novelty_rationale: This paper uniquely uses the term structure of housing discount rates as revealed by real transactions to discipline climate policy timing models, transferring Giglio et al.'s climate-finance methodology into the residential retrofit mandate design context.
nn_title: Climate Change and Long-Run Discount Rates: Evidence from Real Estate
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does exposure to extreme heat during mortgage origination causally increase borrower default risk by reducing household income and debt-service capacity in agricultural and outdoor-labor-intensive counties?
method: Panel regression with county fixed effects exploiting random within-county variation in growing-season degree days above 32°C as an instrument for income shocks at origination, following the agricultural-output-and-random-weather-fluctuations identification strategy applied to mortgage performance data.
data: HMDA loan-level origination records matched to PRISM gridded daily temperature data (2000–2020), USDA county agricultural revenue data, and McDash/Black Knight mortgage performance panel.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation positive heat shock in agricultural counties at origination is likely to increase 90-day delinquency rates by 5–15% within two years, with effects concentrated among variable-income borrowers and spatially correlated with crop insurance participation, suggesting climate-linked underwriting risk not captured by current models.
novelty_rationale: Existing climate-mortgage research focuses on collateral value (flood, hurricane) rather than borrower income shocks; using weather-as-instrument for income at origination cleanly transfers the agricultural-economics identification strategy to mortgage default modeling.
nn_title: Federal Crop Insurance and the Disincentive to Adapt to Extreme Heat
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do households exhibit a dynamic, forward-looking willingness to pay to avoid crime risk when choosing residential locations, and does ignoring this forward-looking behavior cause standard hedonic models to underestimate crime capitalization in housing prices?
method: Dynamic hedonic pricing model allowing for forward-looking household relocation decisions in response to time-varying neighborhood crime trajectories, following the dynamic approach to willingness to pay for violent crime reduction, estimated on repeat-sales and crime incident panel data.
data: CoreLogic repeat-sales housing transactions in 20 major U.S. MSAs (2000–2022), FBI Uniform Crime Reports at the census-tract level, and ACS neighborhood demographic panel data.
key_finding: Dynamic models likely yield crime capitalization estimates 30–60% larger than static hedonic counterparts, with the gap largest in neighborhoods experiencing rapidly rising crime, implying that conventional hedonic studies substantially understate households' aversion to crime risk and the welfare cost of crime spillovers.
novelty_rationale: Although crime-housing price studies are common, none apply the fully dynamic framework from environmental economics that explicitly models forward-looking household behavior, leading to a systematic underestimation identified and corrected by this structural approach.
nn_title: Estimating the Willingness to Pay to Avoid Violent Crime: A Dynamic Approach
assigned_label: Crime's economic effects on property and wages

research_question: Does providing homeowners with expert long-run house price forecasts causally alter their home improvement spending and refinancing decisions, and do these spending responses differ between homeowners with positive versus negative equity?
method: Field experiment with randomized provision of different expert house price forecasts to homeowners (optimistic, pessimistic, and no-information control) embedded in a nationally representative survey, following the home-price-expectations-and-spending field experiment design, measuring subsequent self-reported and administrative spending outcomes.
data: Partnership with a large national mortgage servicer to recruit panel of ~10,000 borrowers stratified by LTV, with baseline and six-month follow-up survey on renovation spending, linked to servicer administrative records on refinancing and equity extraction.
key_finding: Homeowners with positive equity are likely to increase renovation spending when given optimistic forecasts but exhibit inelastic responses due to offsetting wealth effects, while underwater homeowners respond more elastically to pessimistic signals by deferring maintenance, amplifying physical depreciation in distressed neighborhoods.
novelty_rationale: This directly imports the field-experiment design from macroeconomics into the real estate literature to causally identify how house price expectations shape home improvement and maintenance decisions, a mechanism theorized but never experimentally tested in housing economics.
nn_title: Home Price Expectations and Spending: Evidence from a Field Experiment
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Does growing up in a low-quality housing project causally reduce long-run labor market earnings and increase incarceration rates for children, using quasi-random assignment to public housing waitlists as identification?
method: Quasi-experimental comparison of adult outcomes for children quasi-randomly assigned to public housing versus vouchers or market rentals exploiting waitlist lottery timing, following the 'Growing Up in the Projects' cohort methodology linked to administrative earnings and criminal justice records.
data: HUD administrative records on public housing and voucher recipients linked to Social Security Administration earnings records and state incarceration databases, covering cohorts entering housing assistance 1985–2005 and tracked through 2023.
key_finding: Children in densely concentrated public housing projects likely earn 10–20% less at age 30 and face substantially higher incarceration rates than lottery-comparable voucher recipients, identifying housing concentration itself—not poverty—as a mechanism, with implications for housing assistance program design.
novelty_rationale: While the Moving to Opportunity literature examines vouchers, no study uses lottery-based assignment to isolate the causal effect of project-based public housing specifically on long-run economic trajectories relative to alternatives, cleanly transplanting the cohort-lottery design into housing economics.
nn_title: Growing Up in the Projects: The Economic Lives of a Cohort of Men Who Came of Ag
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: Does mandatory public housing tenant choice over unit type and location improve tenant welfare but reduce targeting efficiency toward the most disadvantaged households, and what policy design resolves this trade-off?
method: Structural revealed-preference model of public housing applicant choices over unit size, location, and neighborhood quality estimated from administrative waitlist and unit acceptance data, following the targeting-in-kind-transfers-through-market-design approach to recover preference heterogeneity and simulate no-choice counterfactuals.
data: HUD and city housing authority administrative waitlist records with unit characteristics, offer acceptance rates, and post-assignment outcomes (employment, school quality, crime exposure) for five large U.S. cities (2005–2020).
key_finding: Eliminating tenant choice is likely to improve targeting of units to the lowest-income applicants but reduce average tenant welfare by 20–35% due to preference heterogeneity across family size and neighborhood preferences, with the optimal design being constrained choice within priority tiers that dominates both extremes.
novelty_rationale: Public housing allocation studies typically evaluate location or income eligibility but never formally estimate the welfare-targeting trade-off of choice versus assignment using revealed preferences from waitlist data, directly importing the mechanism design approach from the public economics literature.
nn_title: Targeting In-Kind Transfers through Market Design: A Revealed Preference Analysi
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do housing vouchers reduce labor supply and earnings among working-age recipients, and does this labor supply reduction vary by local housing market tightness and voucher lease-up rates?
method: Randomized controlled trial exploiting staggered housing voucher waitlist lotteries in multiple MSAs to estimate treatment effects on quarterly earnings, labor force participation, and benefit receipt, following the voucher-lottery-and-labor-supply design, with heterogeneous treatment effects by local vacancy rates.
data: HUD Housing Choice Voucher waitlist lottery records linked to Unemployment Insurance wage records and TANF/SNAP administrative data across ten MSAs spanning tight and slack rental markets (2010–2022).
key_finding: Vouchers likely reduce quarterly earnings by 5–15% in tight labor markets where recipients move to lower-density areas with fewer job opportunities, but show smaller or zero effects in slack markets with abundant nearby employment, suggesting that ignoring spatial labor market heterogeneity masks the true labor supply cost of housing assistance.
novelty_rationale: Prior voucher-labor-supply studies pool across market types; this design directly tests whether labor supply disincentives vary with local vacancy rates, a critical heterogeneity for policy that has not been examined due to the rarity of lottery-based identification combined with local market variation.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: Does exposure to abnormally warm temperatures during a mortgage application period reduce lender credit standards and borrower risk assessment accuracy, producing loans with systematically higher subsequent default rates?
method: Cross-sectional analysis matching daily temperature anomalies at loan origination location and date to subsequent loan-level default outcomes, following the attention-to-global-warming behavioral finance design that links local temperature to financial decision quality, with loan officer fixed effects to isolate judgment errors.
data: HMDA loan-level records matched to PRISM daily temperature data by county and origination date (2000–2019), linked to Black Knight mortgage performance data for default outcomes up to 60 months post-origination.
key_finding: Loans originated on anomalously hot days are likely to exhibit 5–12% higher default hazard rates at 24 months, with effects stronger for discretionary underwriting decisions near approval thresholds, suggesting a novel climate-cognition channel through which weather affects financial decision quality independent of collateral value.
novelty_rationale: Existing climate-mortgage research links physical climate risks to property collateral values; this study uniquely examines whether weather affects the cognitive quality of underwriting decisions at origination, importing behavioral finance methodology from the attention-to-climate-change literature into mortgage economics.
nn_title: Adverse Selection and Climate Risk: A Response to
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: How do persistent regional growth disparities amplify the cross-sectional distribution of mortgage credit risk, and does uniform GSE pricing redistribute welfare from low-growth to high-growth regions in ways that distort local housing market efficiency?
method: Spatial equilibrium model of collateralized mortgage borrowing with regional heterogeneity in expected income and house price growth, following the regional-redistribution-through-the-mortgage-market framework, calibrated to match observed default rate dispersion and simulating the welfare effects of risk-based versus uniform pricing.
data: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan-level performance data by origination MSA (2000–2020), BEA regional GDP growth series, FHFA house price indexes, and county-level unemployment dynamics.
key_finding: Uniform GSE pricing is likely to generate annual welfare transfers of $3–8 billion from high-growth coastal markets to low-growth Rust Belt markets, with economically meaningful distortions in local homeownership rates, loan sizes, and risk-taking that would be corrected by regional risk-adjusted pricing but at politically salient distributional costs.
novelty_rationale: While jumbo-conforming spreads and GSE subsidies are well-studied, no paper has used a spatial equilibrium model calibrated to regional growth heterogeneity to quantify the full welfare redistribution from uniform pricing, directly applying the regional-redistribution methodology to U.S. GSE policy.
nn_title: Regional Redistribution through the US Mortgage Market
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: Does federal crop insurance reduce farmland owners' incentives to adapt to climate-driven precipitation shocks, and does this adaptation disincentive capitalize into farmland valuations in ways detectable by hedonic methods?
method: Panel regression with county fixed effects comparing farmland price appreciation and adaptation investments (irrigation, cover cropping) between counties with above- and below-median crop insurance participation rates, exploiting county-level variation in insurance take-up as treatment and interacting with realized precipitation shocks following the federal-crop-insurance-disincentive design.
data: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service farmland value surveys (1990–2022), PRISM county-level precipitation and temperature data, USDA crop insurance participation records, and agricultural adaptation investment data from farm surveys.
key_finding: High crop insurance participation counties likely show 8–15% lower farmland price sensitivity to precipitation volatility, consistent with insured farmers underinvesting in adaptation, implying that crop insurance creates a hidden subsidy to farmland values in climate-risky areas that capitalizes differently than in uninsured markets.
novelty_rationale: Farmland value and climate adaptation literatures are largely separate; this study uniquely links crop insurance to adaptation disincentives via hedonic farmland price analysis, directly importing the moral hazard identification strategy from the agricultural economics literature into real estate valuation.
nn_title: Federal Crop Insurance and the Disincentive to Adapt to Extreme Heat
assigned_label: Climate Risk and Financial Markets

research_question: Do households that adapt to frequent extreme heat exposure over time show systematically lower willingness to pay for air-conditioned housing, and does this convergence in adaptation compress regional hedonic price gradients for climate amenities?
method: Long-run panel analysis of hedonic implicit prices for central air conditioning and shade across U.S. MSAs over 1980–2020, exploiting cross-state variation in historical heat frequency as a measure of adaptation, following the convergence-in-adaptation-to-climate-change methodology applied to housing attribute valuation.
data: American Housing Survey unit-level data on AC ownership and amenities (1985–2021) matched to PRISM county-level heat degree days and MSA-level hedonic price estimates, with Census population data for controls.
key_finding: MSAs with historically high extreme heat frequency likely show 30–50% lower hedonic premiums for central air conditioning, with regional convergence over time as heat exposure spreads nationally, suggesting that climate adaptation compresses the spatial inequality of climate amenity capitalization but at the cost of reduced investment incentives in hotter regions.
novelty_rationale: Climate adaptation in housing is typically studied through energy consumption or migration; this paper directly measures how heat adaptation compresses hedonic prices for cooling amenities over time, applying the adaptation-convergence methodology from mortality-temperature research to real estate attribute valuation.
nn_title: Does Climate Change Affect Real Estate Prices? Only If You Believe In It
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does heat exposure during the third trimester of pregnancy affect the probability of first-time homeownership among affected birth cohorts 25–35 years later, operating through human capital and earnings channels?
method: Cohort-level analysis linking county-of-birth temperature exposure during gestation (exploiting seasonal and year-to-year temperature variation) to homeownership rates and mortgage borrowing amounts at ages 25–35, following the climate-change-and-birth-weight identification strategy extended through life-cycle housing outcomes.
data: Vital Statistics birth records with county and birth month (1975–1995) matched to PRISM gestational temperature data, linked via birth-county-cohort to HMDA origination rates and ACS homeownership data for corresponding cohorts at ages 25–35.
key_finding: Cohorts exposed to high third-trimester heat are likely to have 2–5 percentage-point lower homeownership rates by age 30 and smaller average loan sizes, consistent with human capital damage reducing earnings and creditworthiness, suggesting a novel long-run climate cost operating through the housing ladder.
novelty_rationale: This study creates a new bridge between the climate-birth-outcomes literature and housing economics by tracing gestational heat exposure through to adult housing market participation, a multi-decade causal chain not previously identified in either literature.
nn_title: When climate meets real estate: A survey of the literature
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Do life insurance companies and pension funds with long-term liability horizons hold systematically different commercial real estate asset allocations than shorter-horizon institutional investors, and does this liability-driven demand distort cap rates in long-duration property sectors?
method: Asset-liability management portfolio optimization model following the real-estate-in-an-ALM-framework approach, estimated on quarterly institutional investor real estate holdings from a unique regulatory database, testing whether institutions with longer liability durations hold significantly higher allocations to net-lease and healthcare real estate sectors.
data: NAIC insurance company Schedule A real estate holdings (2000–2022), pension fund CRE allocations from CEM Benchmarking, CoStar transaction-level cap rates by property sector, and NCREIF performance data.
key_finding: Long-liability insurers and pension funds are likely to hold 40–60% more long-duration net-lease and healthcare real estate than shorter-horizon peers, with this liability-driven demand compressing cap rates in these sectors by 30–50 basis points relative to comparable-risk short-lease properties, creating a measurable wedge in CRE pricing attributable to institutional structure rather than fundamentals.
novelty_rationale: CRE institutional investor research focuses on fund flows and returns but has not formally modeled liability duration as a driver of sector-level cap rate compression, directly applying the ALM-framework logic from pension finance to commercial real estate pricing.
nn_title: Real Estate in an ALM Framework: The Case of Fair Value Accounting
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do households with life-cycle optimal housing stock accumulation face lower stock market participation rates, and does this crowd-out vary by local house price volatility and LTV ratios?
method: Life-cycle portfolio choice model with endogenous rent-versus-own decisions and serially correlated local house price returns, following the optimal-life-cycle-portfolio-choice-with-housing-market-cycles framework, structurally estimated on household-level administrative data to quantify crowd-out heterogeneity by market volatility.
data: Finnish or Danish administrative household panel data linking home ownership, mortgage LTV, and financial portfolio holdings at annual frequency (2000–2020), merged with MSA-level house price volatility measures from repeat-sales indexes.
key_finding: Households in high house-price-volatility markets likely hold 8–15 percentage points less in equities than observably similar households in low-volatility markets, with the crowd-out concentrated among high-LTV owners for whom housing is simultaneously a speculative bet and a consumption hedge, suggesting house price cycles have systematic welfare costs through underdiversification.
novelty_rationale: Existing housing-portfolio crowd-out studies use static models; incorporating serially correlated house price cycles from the housing-market-cycles portfolio-choice framework generates novel predictions about how speculative housing motives interact with equity crowd-out that are empirically testable with Scandinavian administrative data.
nn_title: Optimal Life Cycle Portfolio Choice with Housing Market Cycles
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Does optimal portfolio choice with predictable house price growth and transaction costs explain the empirical patterns of housing tenure duration and financial portfolio adjustments observed around household moves?
method: Dynamic stochastic optimization model with time-varying expected house price growth and proportional transaction costs, calibrated to match MSA-level house price autocorrelation, following the optimal-portfolio-choice-with-predictability-and-transaction-costs framework, tested against household-level panel data on move timing and portfolio rebalancing.
data: Panel Study of Income Dynamics housing and financial wealth data (1999–2019) combined with FHFA MSA-level house price indexes and ACS data on household moves, with CRSP financial return data for the financial portfolio component.
key_finding: Households in markets with high recent appreciation are likely to delay moves by 1–3 years relative to fundamentals and shift financial portfolios toward bonds before selling, consistent with the model's prediction that high expected appreciation raises the option value of waiting, implying a novel channel by which house price momentum suppresses residential turnover.
novelty_rationale: Transaction cost models of housing tenure duration exist, but none jointly optimize over time-varying expected appreciation and financial portfolio rebalancing simultaneously, generating novel cross-predictions about financial portfolio composition that precede and follow moves that are testable with panel data.
nn_title: Optimal Portfolio Choice with Predictability in House Prices and Transaction Cos
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Does segregation induced by restrictive residential zoning increase the incidence and severity of civil unrest and neighborhood-level property value crashes, analogous to how rent control and segregation interact with social conflict in other contexts?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting court-ordered rezoning events and fair housing litigation outcomes as quasi-random shocks to residential integration, measuring subsequent changes in neighborhood-level crime rates, property values, and insurance claims around affected census tracts following the segregation-rent-control-riots empirical design.
data: HUD fair housing enforcement records and court rulings (1985–2015), CoreLogic property transaction data, FBI crime incident reports at the census-tract level, and HMDA lending records to capture subsequent investment.
key_finding: Neighborhoods subject to court-ordered integration are likely to experience short-run 3–8% property value declines in adjacent tracts driven by white flight, but medium-run appreciation of 5–12% as commercial activity follows integration, with crime reduction mediating long-run values in a way missed by studies focused only on short-run capitalization.
novelty_rationale: The real estate literature on segregation focuses on discrimination in access but rarely exploits quasi-random integration shocks to trace dynamic property value and crime pathways, importing the conflict-economics empirical design into a U.S. housing market context with clean identification.
nn_title: Racial prejudice and racial residential segregation in an urban model
assigned_label: Racial residential segregation patterns and causes

research_question: Do heat-driven labor productivity losses in construction cause supply-side housing cost shocks that are spatially heterogeneous and amplify local housing affordability crises in Sun Belt metros?
method: Shift-share instrumental variables design using county-level variation in construction worker exposure to extreme heat days (interacted with industry heat sensitivity) as an instrument for residential construction costs, following the climate-shocks-and-exports identification strategy applied to housing supply, with outcomes measured as new unit costs and housing starts.
data: BLS Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages for construction workers by county (1990–2022), PRISM daily temperature data, Census Building Permits Survey, and RSMeans construction cost data at MSA level.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in annual extreme heat days likely raises per-unit residential construction costs by 3–7% in Sun Belt metros and reduces annual housing starts by 4–9%, implying that climate change will generate a supply-side affordability penalty concentrated in already-constrained warm markets that compounds demand-side migration pressures.
novelty_rationale: Climate impacts on housing focus almost exclusively on demand and collateral damage; this paper identifies a supply-side channel through construction labor productivity that has significant policy implications for housing affordability and is directly enabled by importing the climate-shocks-and-exports shift-share IV design.
nn_title: The scarring of the Great Recession on construction labor and housing supply
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Do fat-tailed climate damage distributions cause commercial real estate cap rates in flood-exposed markets to be systematically too low, creating a mispricing analogous to the social-cost-of-carbon underestimation under thin-tailed models?
method: Asset pricing model incorporating fat-tailed physical climate damage distributions (estimated from historical hurricane and flood loss data) into a cap rate decomposition, following the fat-tails-and-social-cost-of-carbon theoretical framework adapted to real estate discount rates, with empirical calibration using CMBS spread data for flood-exposed versus unexposed properties.
data: CoStar and CBRE commercial property transaction data (2000–2023) matched to FEMA flood zone designations and NOAA storm surge models, with CMBS loan-level spreads from Bloomberg for identification of flood risk premiums.
key_finding: Standard climate risk adjustments to cap rates likely underestimate required risk premiums by 40–80 basis points for properties in 100-year flood zones when calibrated with fat-tailed damage distributions, implying systematic underpricing of tail climate risk in commercial real estate that creates systemic financial stability concerns for CMBS collateral.
novelty_rationale: Climate risk in CRE focuses on mean expected damage capitalization; this paper formally applies fat-tail distribution theory from the social cost of carbon literature to derive a new upper bound on required risk premiums, generating a quantitative case for why current CRE climate risk adjustments are likely insufficient.
nn_title: Climate change and commercial real estate: Evidence from Hurricane Sandy
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does the staggered implementation of state-level carbon pricing policies generate measurable capitalization effects in commercial real estate markets, with industrial and office properties differentially affected by transition risk exposure?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting the timing of state cap-and-trade or carbon tax adoption as quasi-random treatment, comparing commercial property cap rates and transaction prices before and after policy adoption across property types, following the state-federal-climate-policy-interaction literature adapted to real estate markets.
