method: Time-varying factor model decomposing MSA-level house price co-movements into fundamental integration components versus non-fundamental sentiment and credit shock components, with the subprime share of originations as a direct measure of the technology diffusion channel.
data: FHFA and CoreLogic MSA-level quarterly house price indices from 1975–2022 with county-level HMDA mortgage origination data, exploiting the 2003–2008 subprime expansion as a differential shock to regional credit technology adoption.
key_finding: Common credit technology diffusion may explain 40–60% of the post-2000 increase in regional price co-movements, with the remainder attributable to genuine economic integration, implying that post-crisis convergence overstates fundamental housing market integration.
novelty_rationale: Separates the fundamental integration hypothesis from the credit contagion hypothesis in regional housing price convergence using the international financial integration framework applied to domestic real estate markets.
nn_title: A Closer Look at the U.S. Housing Market: Modeling Relationships among Regions
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Does proximity to a major employer's sudden mass layoff event reduce house prices in affected neighborhoods through both income destruction and option value effects, with heterogeneous effects by workers' housing tenure and mortgage leverage?
method: Spatial difference-in-differences design around unexpected large plant closures, measuring house price trajectories within concentric rings of affected plants while controlling for regional economic trends, with heterogeneous effects estimated by local homeownership and mortgage leverage ratios.
data: WARN Act mass layoff notices geocoded to plant locations merged with CoreLogic transaction-level house price data from 2000–2020, with county-level mortgage leverage data from FHFA.
key_finding: Mass layoffs within 5 miles of a plant may reduce house prices by 3–8% within two years, with effects 50–100% larger in high-leverage neighborhoods, implying a financial accelerator channel through which labor market shocks amplify housing wealth destruction.
novelty_rationale: Combines the labor market shock and housing market interaction literature with the leverage amplification framework, estimating how employment destruction transmits to housing wealth through a financial accelerator conditioned on community leverage.
nn_title: Foreclosure Externalities: Have We Confused the Cure with the Disease?
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Do households living in flood-prone areas exhibit a downward-sloping term structure of willingness-to-pay for flood protection, analogous to the downward-sloping discount rate term structure documented in real estate climate valuations?
method: Hedonic estimation of implicit prices for flood protection amenities (elevation, levee proximity, FEMA zone designation) across properties with varying time horizons to the next major flood risk cycle, using variation in storm return period estimates as a proxy for temporal risk distance.
data: CoreLogic transaction data matched to FEMA National Flood Insurance Program claims history, First Street Foundation flood risk scores, and local hydraulic engineering flood return period estimates for coastal and riverine markets from 2000–2022.
key_finding: Households may discount flood risks occurring beyond 20–30 years at annual rates of 1–2%, substantially below market discount rates, consistent with a downward-sloping term structure that implies far-future climate risks are near-optimally priced in residential markets.
novelty_rationale: Applies the long-run discount rate estimation methodology from real estate climate finance to the specific context of flood risk, using spatial variation in flood return periods as a novel identification strategy for measuring the climate discount rate term structure.
nn_title: Dutch dilemma: Housing prices and flood risk exposure
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers in competitive lending markets pay lower spreads because competition substitutes for financial literacy, and does this competition-literacy substitution vary by racial and ethnic group in ways that exacerbate or mitigate discriminatory pricing?
method: Multivariate regression analysis of mortgage rate spreads interacting lender market concentration (HHI) with borrower financial literacy proxies and race/ethnicity indicators, using bank branching deregulation as an instrument for local competition intensity.
data: HMDA loan-level origination data merged with CFPB National Financial Well-Being Survey literacy measures at the ZIP code level and FDIC branch density from 2010–2020, with state-by-year bank branching deregulation history.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in local market competition may reduce the minority-white mortgage rate gap by 15–30%, with competition serving as a partial substitute for financial literacy primarily for Black and Hispanic borrowers in concentrated markets.
novelty_rationale: Combines the competition-literacy substitution mechanism from retail finance with the fair lending literature, testing whether market structure reforms can reduce racially discriminatory mortgage pricing without requiring consumer financial education.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Does physical climate risk exposure of corporate real estate portfolios reduce firm investment efficiency and increase corporate risk-taking, analogous to how personal real estate transactions affect hedge fund manager productivity and risk behavior?
method: Instrumental variables panel analysis linking corporate real estate physical climate risk exposure (flood, wildfire, and hurricane hazard scores) to corporate investment efficiency, R&D expenditure, and earnings volatility, with property sale events used to identify attention reallocation.
data: Compustat firm-level financial data merged with CoreLogic commercial property ownership records and First Street Foundation climate risk scores for all publicly traded U.S. non-REIT firms from 2010–2022.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in corporate real estate climate risk exposure may reduce investment efficiency (measured by investment-q sensitivity) by 8–15% and increase earnings volatility by 5–10%, with effects concentrated in firms where managers hold large personal real estate stakes.
novelty_rationale: Transfers the personal real estate distraction mechanism documented for professional fund managers to corporate executives, testing whether climate exposure of owned real estate creates managerial attention and cognitive bandwidth costs analogous to personal transaction distraction.
nn_title: How do Personal Real Estate Transactions Affect Productivity and Risk Taking? Ev
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the provision of housing vouchers to working-age adults reduce labor supply and increase welfare dependency by reducing the opportunity cost of non-employment, and does this labor supply effect vary with local housing market tightness?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting income eligibility cutoffs for Housing Choice Vouchers combined with lottery-based waitlist assignment, examining labor force participation, hours worked, and welfare program participation for recipients versus non-recipients.
data: HUD administrative voucher records linked to IRS earnings records, Social Security Administration employment records, and SNAP/TANF participation data for 2005–2019 across 50 large PHAs with lottery-based allocation.
key_finding: Voucher receipt may reduce quarterly labor earnings by $200–$400 and labor force participation by 3–5 percentage points, with larger disemployment effects in tight housing markets where the implicit rent subsidy is largest relative to potential wages.
novelty_rationale: Directly extends the Jacob and Ludwig (2012) lottery design to test whether the housing assistance labor supply reduction documented in their study varies systematically with local housing market conditions in ways that inform optimal program design.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: Do real estate brokerage markets with greater fee transparency and price competition exhibit faster convergence of transaction prices across buyer demographic groups, reducing racial and ethnic price disparities through market discipline?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis exploiting the staggered rollout of state-level laws promoting unbundled real estate services and online fee disclosure requirements, measuring changes in racial pricing gaps in housing transactions before and after adoption.
data: CoreLogic transaction-level data with buyer name-based ethnicity imputation merged with state-level real estate brokerage deregulation event dates and HHI concentration measures from 2000–2020 across 200 MSAs.
key_finding: States adopting fee transparency laws may see 20–35% reductions in the Black-white and Hispanic-white transaction price gap over three years, with effects driven by increased buyer shopping behavior and reduction in broker steering.
novelty_rationale: Connects the competition-reduces-discrimination mechanism from the market structure literature to real estate brokerage markets, testing whether regulatory reforms promoting price transparency reduce demographic pricing disparities through market discipline rather than anti-discrimination enforcement.
nn_title: Ethnicity in housing markets: Buyers, sellers and agents
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the mandatory bundling of mortgage insurance with high-LTV government-backed loans create adverse selection that concentrates risk among borrowers who could obtain private insurance, raising costs for lower-risk households that cross-subsidize higher-risk ones?
method: Structural model of mortgage insurance demand with adverse selection, calibrated using HMDA loan-level data and FHA insurance claim records, comparing the predicted premium for risk-priced private insurance versus uniform FHA premiums across the LTV and credit score distribution.
data: FHA single-family endorsement and claim data merged with HMDA origination records from 2000–2019, with private mortgage insurance premium schedules from MGIC, Radian, and Genworth for comparison.
key_finding: Mandatory FHA bundling may generate annual cross-subsidies of $500–$1,500 per loan from low-risk to high-risk borrowers, with adverse selection concentrated among borrowers with FICO scores above 700 who face FHA premiums 30–50 basis points above risk-adjusted private insurance rates.
novelty_rationale: Applies the mandatory insurance adverse selection framework from Voena et al.'s public housing choice paper to FHA mortgage insurance, testing whether uniform pricing in mandatory government programs creates efficiency and distributional costs that risk-based private markets would not.
nn_title: The Impact of Federal Mortgage Insurance Programs on Ann Arbor's Home Mortgage M
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does land use regulatory intensity act as a fiscal instrument that redistributes resources from low-income renters to high-income homeowners across metropolitan areas, paralleling the regressive incidence of carbon pricing under non-recycled revenue designs?
method: Distributional incidence analysis calculating the effective tax burden of regulatory land costs by household income decile within MSAs, using a parcel-level hedonic decomposition of house prices into land, structure, and regulatory cost components, with Gini coefficient comparisons across regulatory regimes.
data: Parcel-level property transaction data from Lincoln Institute merged with Wharton Residential Land Use Regulation Index for 200 MSAs, ACS household income distributions, and rental cost burden data from 2010–2020.
key_finding: Land use regulatory costs may impose an implicit tax burden 3–5 times larger as a share of income for bottom-quintile renters compared to top-quintile homeowners, with the regressivity increasing in supply-constrained superstar cities where regulatory costs are highest.
novelty_rationale: Applies the vertical targeting and distributional incidence framework from environmental economics directly to land use regulation, quantifying the regressive fiscal incidence of zoning through its effect on housing costs across the income distribution.
nn_title: The Incidence of the Land Use Regulatory Tax
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Does the introduction of a carbon tax on building operations shift commercial real estate capital allocation toward office buildings with newer vintage and lower carbon intensity, analogous to how cap-and-trade policies create differential investment incentives across industries?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis comparing transaction prices, capital expenditure, and occupancy changes for high-carbon versus low-carbon commercial buildings before and after introduction of building performance standard mandates in treated cities versus control cities.
data: CoStar commercial property transaction and operating data with EPA ENERGY STAR certification status and building vintage for all major U.S. office markets from 2005–2023, exploiting variation in city-level building carbon pricing adoption.
key_finding: Building performance standards may generate a 5–15% price premium for low-carbon certified buildings within three years of policy adoption and accelerate the obsolescence of high-carbon older stock, creating a stranded asset discount of 8–20% for non-compliant buildings.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the sectoral capital reallocation mechanism from cap-and-trade literature directly to commercial real estate, estimating how building-level carbon pricing creates differential obsolescence and investment incentives that reshape the commercial property stock.
nn_title: On the Value of Environmental Certification in the Commercial Real Estate Market
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do neighborhoods with persistent historical contamination stigma exhibit fat-tailed house price distributions — with more frequent extreme negative price events — compared to otherwise similar neighborhoods, paralleling fat-tail models of climate damage in integrated assessment models?
method: Extreme value theory analysis of the tail behavior of house price distributions in neighborhoods with Superfund sites, asbestos-contaminated buildings, or industrial pollution legacy compared to matched control neighborhoods, testing for statistically fatter left tails.
data: CoreLogic ZIP code-level transaction price distributions from 1990–2022 matched to EPA Superfund site proximity data, TOXMAP toxic release inventory, and EJSCREEN environmental justice indicators for 500 contaminated versus matched control neighborhoods.
key_finding: Contaminated neighborhoods may exhibit left-tail house price distributions with Pareto tail exponents 20–40% smaller than control neighborhoods, implying a substantially higher probability of extreme negative price events that standard hedonic models systematically underestimate.
novelty_rationale: Applies the fat-tailed damages framework from climate economics to neighborhood-level contamination risk in housing markets, testing whether environmental stigma creates heavy-tailed price distributions with implications for mortgage risk and insurance pricing.
nn_title: Evidence of the Dual Nature of Property Value Recovery Following Environmental R
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: Does the severity of segregation as measured by experienced divergence in actual visit patterns — rather than residential location — predict differential house price appreciation between historically redlined and non-redlined neighborhoods beyond what residential segregation measures capture?
method: Regression discontinuity design at redlining boundaries augmented with SafeGraph mobility data measuring experienced segregation (actual cross-neighborhood visit patterns), testing whether experienced segregation mediates the contemporary price gap beyond residential segregation and neighborhood characteristics.
data: Historical HOLC redlining maps geocoded to contemporary parcel boundaries merged with SafeGraph mobility data measuring neighborhood visit patterns from 2017–2022, CoreLogic transaction prices, and ACS demographic data.
key_finding: Experienced segregation — measured by actual cross-neighborhood mobility — may explain an additional 20–35% of the contemporary redlining house price gap beyond residential segregation measures, suggesting that mobility barriers rather than just residential sorting perpetuate historical pricing disparities.
novelty_rationale: Applies the experienced segregation framework from Chetty et al.'s mobility research to housing markets, replacing survey-based social interaction measures with GPS-based actual visit patterns to measure how physical segregation in daily activity perpetuates historical real estate disparities.
nn_title: The lasting impact of historical residential security maps on experienced segreg
assigned_label: Racial residential segregation patterns and causes

research_question: Can the long-run discount rate embedded in farmland prices — where the asset's value derives from perpetual cash flows susceptible to climate change — provide an upper bound for the social cost of carbon-related agricultural risks, analogous to housing-market-derived climate discount rates?
method: Asset pricing model extracting the term structure of discount rates from farmland price-to-rent ratios across counties with varying climate change vulnerability, comparing implied discount rates for climate-exposed versus climate-resilient farmland to housing-based climate discount rate estimates.
data: USDA NASS county-level farmland values and cash rents from 1997–2022 merged with NOAA climate vulnerability indices, First Street Foundation drought risk scores, and CMIP6 projected temperature and precipitation change scenarios.
key_finding: Climate-exposed farmland may embed long-run discount rates of 1.5–2.5% for risks materializing beyond 50 years, consistent with housing-market estimates and substantially below risk-free rates, providing real-asset market evidence for declining discount rate schedules in climate-related cost-benefit analysis.
novelty_rationale: Extends the housing-market long-run discount rate methodology of Giglio et al. to agricultural land markets, providing a second independent real-asset market test of whether financial markets price ultra-long-horizon climate risks at declining discount rates.
nn_title: Climate Change and Long-Run Discount Rates: Evidence from Real Estate
assigned_label: Climate Risk and Financial Markets

research_question: Does the convergence in residential construction labor markets following the Great Recession — specifically, the scarring of construction workers into other industries — cause heterogeneous housing supply elasticity reductions across MSAs that amplify subsequent house price booms?
method: Instrumental variable analysis using the severity of the 2006–2009 local house price bust as an exogenous shock to construction labor scarring, estimating the effect of labor supply reduction on housing supply elasticity and subsequent price appreciation from 2012–2022 across MSAs.
data: BLS state and area employment data for construction occupations from 2000–2022 merged with FHFA MSA house price indices, Census building permit data, and American Community Survey construction industry employment from the same period.
key_finding: MSAs experiencing 10% greater construction employment scarring during the recession may exhibit supply elasticities 20–35% lower in the recovery, explaining 1–3 percentage points of additional annual house price appreciation relative to MSAs with intact construction labor markets.
novelty_rationale: Combines the construction labor scarring finding from Ringo (2021) with a structural housing supply elasticity framework, testing whether permanent labor market hysteresis from real estate busts creates a supply constraint channel that amplifies subsequent housing cycles.
nn_title: The scarring of the Great Recession on construction labor and housing supply
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Do real estate investment committees that rely on soft information from geographically proximate local managers make better investment decisions — measured by subsequent property-level returns — than committees relying on standardized algorithmic signals, paralleling broker face-to-face interaction effects on mortgage default?
method: Quasi-experimental analysis comparing property-level holding period returns for investments made through in-person site visits versus remote algorithmic screening, exploiting the COVID-19 elimination of business travel as an exogenous shock to in-person due diligence capacity.
data: NCREIF property-level returns matched to institutional investor due diligence records from PREA survey data and investment management firm administrative records from 2018–2022, with COVID-19 travel restriction intensity as a shock to in-person information acquisition.
key_finding: Properties evaluated through in-person site visits may generate 50–150 basis points higher subsequent annual returns than algorithmically screened investments, with the differential largest for complex repositioning strategies where local tacit knowledge is most valuable.
novelty_rationale: Applies the face-to-face interaction mechanism from mortgage broker research to institutional real estate investment decision-making, testing whether soft information acquisition through physical presence generates systematic investment performance advantages.
nn_title: There is no place like home: Information asymmetries, local asset concentration,
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the introduction of mandatory home energy performance certificates — requiring sellers to disclose energy ratings at point of sale — reduce the information asymmetry premium on energy-inefficient homes, or does it instead trigger strategic upgrading that capitalizes the green premium and reduces affordability for low-income buyers?
method: Regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of mandatory EPC requirements across EU member states and U.S. states, comparing transaction prices, renovation activity, and buyer income composition before and after mandate adoption.
data: Housing transaction records from 15 EU countries with linked EPC registry data from 2008–2022, exploiting variation in mandatory disclosure adoption timing across national and regional jurisdictions within the EU Energy Performance of Buildings Directive.
key_finding: Mandatory EPC disclosure may reduce the price discount on low-rated buildings by 30–50% through market learning but simultaneously accelerate energy upgrades that increase prices by 3–6% and reduce low-income buyer access to affordable entry-level housing stock.
novelty_rationale: Combines the mandatory disclosure mechanism design question with the housing affordability and green premium capitalization literature, testing whether information mandates produce distributional trade-offs between energy efficiency gains and housing access for lower-income households.
nn_title: Energy labels, misreporting, and heuristic hedonic models
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'peak-end' valuation bias, anchoring their willingness to pay disproportionately to the most emotionally salient room encountered during a property tour rather than integrating all attributes equally?
method: Randomized field experiment manipulating the order and emotional salience of rooms shown during property tours, measuring final offer prices and self-reported valuations across treatment conditions with varying peak-room placement.
data: Controlled viewings of listed properties in a mid-sized U.S. metro market with real buyer participants, linked to subsequent offer data and post-tour hedonic valuations across approximately 400 transactions over 18 months.
key_finding: Buyers who encounter the highest-quality room last (peak-end alignment) may submit offers 3–7% higher than buyers shown the same property with rooms in reverse order, with effects strongest for less experienced buyers.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the peak-end rule from experiential psychology into the high-stakes sequential information environment of property tours, where room-order effects have never been causally identified despite their large financial implications.
nn_title: Valuation Bias in Commercial Appraisal: A Transaction Price Feedback Experiment
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does a sense of fiduciary responsibility cause institutional real estate investors to make systematically more risk-averse acquisition decisions than individual investors acting for themselves, even after controlling for mandate constraints?
method: Incentivized laboratory experiment combined with a natural field experiment exploiting variation in whether fund managers are making decisions for their own account versus on behalf of pension beneficiaries, measuring bid aggressiveness in simulated and actual property auctions.
data: Lab experiment with real estate investment professionals (n≈300) combined with transaction-level data from NCREIF distinguishing individual versus institutional acquisitions across property types and cycle phases from 2000–2023.
key_finding: Institutional investors acting as fiduciaries may underbid by 4–9% relative to comparable individual investors, generating a measurable fiduciary discount that partly explains the documented institutional price discount in distressed markets.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the 'sense of responsibility' risk-aversion paradigm from group decision-making psychology to real estate, providing a behavioral microfoundation for the empirically puzzling institutional discount in property acquisitions.
nn_title: Who Is “Misleading” Whom in Real Estate Transactions?
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the attraction effect — whereby an asymmetrically dominated third option shifts preferences toward the target — influence which mortgage product borrowers select, and can this be exploited or mitigated by lenders?
method: Randomized online experiment with actual mortgage applicants presenting two-option versus three-option loan menus, where the decoy varies on rate, term, and fees to test whether its presence systematically shifts selection toward higher-fee products.
data: Pre-registered experiment embedded in a major mortgage broker's digital application platform serving approximately 2,000 borrowers over 12 months, with linked outcome data on loan selection, subsequent delinquency, and refinancing.
key_finding: Adding a dominated decoy mortgage product to the choice set may increase selection of the higher-fee target product by 8–15 percentage points, representing thousands of dollars in additional costs for the median borrower.
novelty_rationale: First application of the attraction effect to a high-stakes, consequential financial decision environment where the phenomenon could be systematically exploited, connecting consumer protection policy to behavioral choice architecture research.
nn_title: Paying Too Much? Borrower Sophistication and Overpayment in the U.S. Mortgage Ma
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does the diffusion of moral responsibility induced by dual agency — where one agent represents both buyer and seller — causally reduce agent effort and distort transaction prices beyond what information asymmetry models predict?
method: Laboratory experiment with licensed real estate agents replicating the dual-agency structure and manipulating the framing of responsibility across single-client and dual-client conditions, combined with a regression discontinuity analysis of dual-agency transactions in states with different disclosure requirements.
data: Experimental data from 240 licensed agents combined with MLS transaction records from 15 states spanning 2010–2022, exploiting state-level regulatory variation in dual-agency legality and disclosure requirements.
key_finding: Dual-agency transactions may produce price discounts of 1.5–3% for buyers and premiums of 1–2% for sellers relative to what full-information single-agency transactions would generate, with diffusion of moral responsibility mechanistically explaining a portion of this gap.
novelty_rationale: Imports the diffusion-of-responsibility paradigm from social psychology into real estate agency theory, providing a behavioral mechanism distinct from information asymmetry that could motivate stronger dual-agency regulation.
nn_title: Who Is “Misleading” Whom in Real Estate Transactions?
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Can warning messages about local flood or climate risk paradoxically increase property values in some markets by directing attention and conferring salience on previously ignored amenities near water?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis exploiting staggered rollout of FEMA Flood Map Service Center update notifications across census tracts, comparing price trajectories of properties near water in treated versus untreated areas before and after notification.
data: CoreLogic deed transaction records merged with FEMA flood map revision dates and National Flood Insurance Program policy data covering all coastal U.S. states from 2005–2023, with approximately 2.1 million repeat-sale pairs.
key_finding: Flood risk warnings may paradoxically increase prices for waterfront properties by 2–5% in low-income elastic markets where the attention-directing mechanism dominates, while reducing prices by 3–8% in high-income inelastic coastal markets where the risk-update mechanism dominates.
novelty_rationale: Applies the psychological 'warnings can backfire' framework to real estate climate risk disclosure, providing the first causal test of the conditions under which mandatory risk communication increases rather than decreases exposure to hazardous properties.
nn_title: Graduated Flood Risks and Property Prices in Galveston County
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do real estate agents who recently experienced personal losses on their own home purchases exhibit greater risk aversion in subsequent transactions for clients, and does this spillover impair client outcomes?
method: Linking agent-owned property transaction records to subsequent client transaction outcomes using a difference-in-differences design around the timing of agents' personal financial losses from housing downturns, instrumenting with local price declines.
data: National merged dataset of agent license records, agent-owned property transactions, and agent-brokered client transactions from CoreLogic across all 50 states from 2000–2022, covering approximately 180,000 licensed agents.
key_finding: Agents who personally experienced a home-value loss exceeding 20% in the prior year may steer clients toward lower-risk, lower-priced properties that sell 15–25 days faster at prices 1.5–3% below comparable listings, reducing client welfare.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the personal real estate transactions and manager performance paradigm — previously studied only for hedge fund managers — to the much larger and more economically significant residential brokerage market.
nn_title: Who Is “Misleading” Whom in Real Estate Transactions?
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the compromise effect — where consumers disproportionately choose the middle option — influence tenant lease term selection in commercial real estate markets, and does presenting a third extreme term option increase landlord revenue?
method: Randomized field experiment with a commercial real estate firm presenting tenants with two-option versus three-option lease menus in which a long-term option is added to shift selection toward the intermediate term preferred by the landlord.
data: Pre-registered experiment across 600 new lease negotiations at a large commercial property management firm operating multi-tenant office and retail portfolios in five U.S. metropolitan areas over 24 months.
key_finding: Adding an extreme long-term lease option to the menu may increase selection of the landlord-preferred intermediate term by 12–20 percentage points, increasing average effective lease duration by 0.8–1.4 years and improving landlord cash flow predictability.
novelty_rationale: First empirical test of compromise effects in a business-to-business real estate contracting context, potentially revealing how menu design in lease negotiations influences tenant choices and landlord revenue in systematic but unexplored ways.
nn_title: Commercial Real Estate Leasing, Asymmetric Information, and Monopolistic Competi
assigned_label: Commercial Real Estate Rent Dynamics

research_question: Does attentional dilution — whereby the addition of irrelevant attributes to a property listing reduces the weight buyers assign to energy efficiency ratings — explain the well-documented underpricing of green building features in residential markets?
method: Eye-tracking experiment with home-search participants viewing simulated property listings with varying numbers of attributes, measuring fixation patterns on energy efficiency information and downstream willingness-to-pay estimates across conditions.
data: Lab-based eye-tracking study with 320 active homebuyers in two metropolitan markets, combined with hedonic regression analysis of MLS listings data examining how listing information density moderates the green premium across 85,000 transactions.
key_finding: Each additional listing attribute beyond 12 may reduce the weight buyers assign to energy efficiency ratings by approximately 8–15%, and listings with fewer attributes may show green premiums 2–4 percentage points higher than information-dense listings.
novelty_rationale: Applies attentional dilution theory to explain a central puzzle in green building valuation — why energy efficiency is systematically underpriced — providing a behavioral mechanism distinct from information asymmetry or preference heterogeneity.
nn_title: The capitalization of energy efficiency: Evidence from the housing market
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do homeowners facing negative equity exhibit the 'chasing losses' phenomenon, taking on additional leveraged real estate investments to recover paper losses rather than accepting the strategic default predicted by option theory?
method: Linking credit bureau data tracking second property acquisition to primary residence loan-to-value ratios, using an instrumental variable strategy exploiting local house price shocks to identify plausibly exogenous variation in negative equity depth.
data: Matched credit bureau and HMDA records covering 3.2 million primary homeowners from 2006–2015, merged with CoreLogic property records to identify second-property acquisitions, across all U.S. metropolitan statistical areas.
key_finding: Underwater homeowners may be 20–35% more likely to acquire an additional investment property than equivalent above-water homeowners, with this effect concentrated among borrowers who have recently experienced accelerating paper losses.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the chasing-losses phenomenon from gambling psychology to real estate, testing whether the well-documented disposition effect runs in reverse for underwater homeowners who double down rather than default.
nn_title: Fear, Shame and Guilt: Economic and Behavioral Motivations for Strategic Default
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Does the identifiable victim effect operate in housing foreclosure decisions, such that loan servicers grant more modifications when borrowers' hardship narratives are individualized and emotionally vivid rather than presented in aggregate statistical terms?
method: Randomized controlled experiment with mortgage loan servicer representatives processing loan modification applications where the borrower hardship summary is randomly assigned to be narrative-individualized versus statistical-aggregate, measuring modification approval rates and terms granted.
data: Pre-registered RCT with 12 mortgage servicers processing approximately 3,000 loan modification applications over 18 months, with randomization at the application level and outcome data linked to HMDA modification records.
key_finding: Individualized borrower narratives may increase modification approval rates by 8–15 percentage points and increase principal reduction offers by $4,000–$9,000 relative to statistically equivalent applications presented in aggregate form.
novelty_rationale: First empirical test of the identifiable victim effect in mortgage servicing, potentially explaining large unexplained variance in modification rates across otherwise similar borrowers and suggesting cheap interventions to improve modification take-up.
nn_title: Consumer Ruthlessness and Mortgage Default during the 2007 to 2009 Housing Bust
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Does overconfidence in housing market knowledge among real estate agents — measured psychometrically rather than by revealed preference — predict worse client outcomes, higher prices paid by buyers, and lower prices obtained by sellers?
method: Cross-sectional survey deploying a validated overconfidence measurement instrument to a random sample of licensed agents, linking agent-level overconfidence scores to their clients' transaction outcomes via administrative MLS and license records.
data: Survey of 2,400 licensed real estate agents in 10 metropolitan markets linked to 85,000 client transactions they brokered over a five-year period, with agent-level overconfidence measured on a validated 40-item scale adapted from the generalized overconfidence task.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in agent overconfidence may predict 0.8–1.5% worse outcomes for buyer clients (higher prices paid) and 0.5–1.2% worse outcomes for seller clients (lower sale prices), with effects largest in declining markets.
novelty_rationale: Introduces psychometrically validated overconfidence measurement into real estate brokerage research, which has relied exclusively on revealed-preference proxies that conflate overconfidence with information and incentive effects.
nn_title: Heterogeneous Real Estate Agents and the Housing Cycle
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers exhibit the '1-in-X' numerical format bias, systematically overweighting default risk when it is presented as '1 in 20 borrowers default' versus '5 in 100 borrowers default', and does this affect product choice and financial outcomes?
method: Pre-registered online experiment with 2,400 prospective borrowers randomized to receive identical mortgage risk disclosures in either 1-in-X or equivalent ratio format, measuring mortgage product selection, stated risk perceptions, and hypothetical portfolio allocations.
data: Online experiment with a nationally representative panel of prospective homebuyers combined with archival analysis of CFPB mortgage disclosure format rollouts across states and lenders, exploiting natural variation in disclosure format adoption from 2015–2020.
key_finding: Borrowers receiving 1-in-X format default risk information may be 12–18 percentage points more likely to select a fixed-rate mortgage over an adjustable-rate product and report 25–40% higher subjective default risk perceptions for identically risky loans.
novelty_rationale: Tests a specific numerical format bias in the highest-stakes consumer financial decision context, with direct regulatory implications for required mortgage disclosure formats under TILA-RESPA and CFPB design standards.
nn_title: Unobservable Risks in Mortgage Contract Choice
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does future self-continuity — the degree to which individuals identify with their future self — predict the timing and quality of homeownership decisions, and does it explain residual racial gaps in homeownership after controlling for wealth and income?
method: Longitudinal survey linking validated future self-continuity measures to subsequent housing tenure transitions, mortgage application behavior, and wealth accumulation, with mediation analysis testing whether continuity differences explain residual racial homeownership gaps.
data: New module added to the Panel Study of Income Dynamics fielded to 4,800 renter households over three waves (2024, 2026, 2028), measuring future self-continuity, time preferences, and housing decisions, linked to administrative mortgage application records.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in future self-continuity may increase five-year homeownership transition probability by 6–11 percentage points and explain 15–25% of the residual Black-White homeownership gap not accounted for by income, wealth, or credit score.
novelty_rationale: Imports a well-validated psychological construct — future self-continuity — that predicts retirement saving into the housing tenure decision literature, potentially identifying a novel behavioral mechanism underlying persistent racial homeownership disparities.
nn_title: The drivers of sustainable homeownership
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Do real estate appraisers exhibit the diffusion of responsibility phenomenon when working in teams or reviewing prior appraisals, producing higher valuation inflation than solo appraisers with full accountability?
method: Controlled experiment with 360 licensed appraisers randomly assigned to appraise identical properties as sole accountable appraiser, as reviewer of a prior appraisal, or as part of a two-person team, measuring deviation from hedonic model value and directional inflation.
data: Lab-in-the-field experiment with appraisers at three continuing education conferences combined with archival analysis of appraisal inflation rates in desk-review versus field-review versus solo appraisal conditions using AMC administrative data.
key_finding: Appraisers reviewing a prior colleague's work may inflate values by 1.8–3.2% more than solo appraisers making original determinations, with team-reviewed appraisals showing the largest inflation at 2.5–4.5% above hedonic benchmarks.
novelty_rationale: Applies diffusion of responsibility — established in social psychology as a key factor in collective moral failures — to appraisal bias, offering a structural explanation for why AMC desk-review processes may systematically amplify rather than correct valuation inflation.
nn_title: Valuation Bias in Commercial Appraisal: A Transaction Price Feedback Experiment
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Does the representativeness heuristic cause homebuyers to systematically avoid purchase prices ending in round numbers (believing them non-random and biased upward), and does this create price clustering just below round thresholds that disadvantages sellers?
method: Analysis of the full distribution of transaction prices and listing prices in national MLS data, testing for asymmetric bunching patterns below versus above round number thresholds using a bunching estimator, separately identifying buyer-initiated versus seller-initiated price points.
data: Zillow-Redfin merged transaction database covering 18 million residential sales from 2010–2023 across all U.S. metropolitan areas, with linked listing price histories, final sale prices, and agent representation data to separate listing versus offer effects.
key_finding: Buyers may systematically bid prices ending in .9 or .99 thresholds 3–5% more often than a uniform distribution would predict, creating a measurable clustering pattern that costs sellers on average $800–$2,400 in forgone revenue per transaction.
novelty_rationale: Reframes the well-documented charm pricing literature through the lens of the representativeness heuristic, separating the seller-driven charm pricing mechanism from a distinct buyer-driven representativeness bias that operates in the opposite direction.
nn_title: The Effects of Charm Listing Prices on House Transaction Prices
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the dynamic choice set effect — where preferences change when options are revealed sequentially rather than simultaneously — explain why sequential housing search produces systematically different purchase decisions than simultaneous comparison search?
method: Randomized experiment with active homebuyers assigned to sequential property tour scheduling versus a simultaneous open-house weekend where they see all candidate homes back-to-back, measuring final choice quality, satisfaction, and willingness to pay.
data: Pre-registered field experiment in partnership with three large brokerage firms across two metropolitan markets, enrolling 480 active homebuyers with comparable budgets and criteria, tracking search behavior, time to close, and post-purchase satisfaction surveys.
key_finding: Sequential searchers may take 18–35% longer to purchase, pay 1.5–3.5% more for comparable properties, and report lower post-purchase satisfaction than simultaneous searchers, with effects amplified in hot markets where sequential search forces rushed decisions.
novelty_rationale: Translates the dynamic versus static choice set paradigm into housing search, where sequential search is essentially universal but its preference-distorting effects relative to simultaneous search have never been experimentally identified.
nn_title: Dynamic aspects of consumer decisions in housing markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do REIT CEOs exhibit a domain-specific risk compensation effect whereby personal financial losses in their private real estate portfolios induce higher risk-taking in their corporate acquisition strategies?
method: Linking REIT CEO personal property transaction records to subsequent corporate acquisition risk metrics (geographic concentration, leverage used, cap rate spreads) using an event-study design around the timing of significant personal property losses.
data: Hand-collected CEO personal property transaction records from county assessor databases merged with SNL Financial REIT acquisition data and CEO biographical information for all U.S. equity REITs from 1995–2022, covering approximately 380 CEOs.
key_finding: REIT CEOs who experienced personal home value losses exceeding 15% in the prior year may increase corporate property acquisition spending by 12–20% and accept cap rates 25–40 basis points lower, consistent with risk compensation rather than risk aversion.
novelty_rationale: Applies the visceral factor and domain-specific risk compensation framework to the understudied link between executive personal wealth shocks and corporate real estate investment risk, challenging the standard assumption that personal and professional risk preferences are independent.
nn_title: CEO's age and acquisition behaviors of REITs
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Can a self-funding mechanism that rewards accurate property tax assessment appeals — paying successful appellants from the additional tax revenue recovered from revealed over-assessments of comparable properties — improve assessment uniformity while reducing deadweight loss?
method: Theoretical mechanism design model specifying the incentive compatibility and budget balance conditions, combined with a simulation calibrated to administrative assessment data testing revenue neutrality across different appeal frequency and accuracy distributions.
data: Administrative property assessment and appeal outcome records from Cook County Illinois (2005–2022) combined with comparable data from Harris County Texas, covering 4.2 million parcels and 380,000 appeals, to calibrate the simulation parameters.
key_finding: A self-funding appeal reward mechanism may reduce assessment ratio coefficient of dispersion by 12–22% while remaining revenue-neutral, and could shift the composition of appeals from strategic gaming by sophisticated investors toward genuine accuracy-improving challenges by owner-occupiers.
novelty_rationale: Directly transplants the self-funding reward mechanism design paradigm from tax compliance research into property tax administration, a context where incentive misalignment between assessors and appellants generates large and regressive distributional costs.
nn_title: The Effects of Property Tax Protests on the Assessment Uniformity of Residential
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do property listing agents who face high referral stakes — because they are building long-term reputations in geographically concentrated markets — exert systematically more effort and achieve better outcomes than agents with diffuse or transient clienteles?
method: Exploiting natural variation in agent geographic market concentration as a proxy for referral stakes, using geographically weighted regression to estimate the relationship between agent concentration, listing effort (days to first price reduction, showing frequency), and transaction outcomes.
data: National MLS transaction data linked to individual agent license records and geographic clustering analysis covering 8 million listings from 2015–2022, identifying agent market concentration at the zip-code level using Herfindahl-style concentration measures.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in agent geographic concentration may reduce time to sale by 8–14 days and increase sale-to-list price ratio by 0.6–1.1 percentage points, with disciplining referral effects strongest in markets where local reputation is most observable.
novelty_rationale: Tests the 'disciplining effect of concern for referrals' mechanism — established conceptually in prior work on moving-away sellers — with a general econometric identification strategy that exploits geographic concentration rather than the narrow moving-away comparison.
nn_title: The Disciplining Effect of Concern for Referrals: Evidence from Real Estate Agen
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the psychological distance between investors and geographically remote properties — analogous to the generosity-distance relationship in charitable giving — reduce due diligence effort and increase overpayment for out-of-market acquisitions?
method: Regression analysis examining whether the overpayment premium for out-of-market acquisitions scales continuously with geodesic distance from investor headquarters, testing both a level effect and an interaction with information availability proxies.
data: CoStar commercial transaction database merged with REIT and institutional investor headquarters locations covering 340,000 commercial property transactions from 2000–2022, supplemented by private equity real estate fund acquisition records from Preqin.
key_finding: Transaction overpayment premiums for out-of-state acquisitions may scale linearly with log-distance at approximately 0.8–1.5% per doubling of distance, with effects moderated by information intermediary availability and local market transparency.
novelty_rationale: Reframes the well-documented geographic proximity premium in real estate through the psychological distance literature, predicting a continuous distance-overpayment gradient rather than the binary in-market versus out-of-market comparison used in prior work.
nn_title: Do Out‐of‐State Buyers Pay More for Real Estate? An Examination of Anchoring‐Ind
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the moral wiggle room mechanism — whereby decision makers avoid information that would obligate prosocial behavior — cause mortgage servicers to systematically under-identify strategic defaulters in order to avoid triggering costly modification obligations?
method: Natural experiment exploiting variation in servicer information technology systems that differ in how prominently they surface borrower hardship indicators, testing whether servicers with more 'information-obscuring' systems show systematically lower modification rates for equivalent borrowers.
data: HMDA loan modification records merged with servicer technology adoption records from the Mortgage Bankers Association annual technology survey, covering 1.8 million modifications from 2010–2018 across 45 servicers, linked to property and borrower characteristics.
key_finding: Servicers whose IT systems require active lookup of borrower hardship data may approve modifications at rates 6–12 percentage points lower than servicers whose systems push hardship alerts, despite serving observably equivalent borrower populations.
novelty_rationale: Imports the moral wiggle room framework — where agents avoid morally obligating information — into mortgage servicing, offering a behavioral explanation for the large and unexplained cross-servicer variance in modification rates that is distinct from capacity, incentive, or portfolio composition explanations.
nn_title: Information Externalities and Home Mortgage Underwriting
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit the 'more-is-better' heuristic in evaluating condominium building amenity packages, systematically overvaluing associations with more amenities regardless of their actual use value, producing amenity bloat and suboptimal pricing?
method: Conjoint analysis experiment with 1,800 condominium buyers presenting randomized combinations of amenity numbers and types while holding monthly HOA fees constant, combined with hedonic analysis testing whether more amenities predict higher prices independent of quality-adjusted value.
data: Experiment linked to Zillow/Redfin condominium listing data for 12 major metropolitan areas from 2015–2023 covering 280,000 condominium transactions, with amenity counts coded from listing descriptions using natural language processing.
key_finding: Each additional amenity beyond 8 may increase buyer willingness to pay by $1,800–$3,500 independent of the amenity's actual use frequency, while simultaneously reducing realized price appreciation by 0.3–0.6% annually due to higher HOA costs that eventually capitalize negatively.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'more-is-better' anticommons heuristic from behavioral economics to condominium pricing, explaining why the housing market may systematically incentivize amenity overbuilding and providing a new behavioral mechanism for the condo pricing puzzle.
nn_title: Hedonic prices, demands for urban housing amenities, and benefit estimates
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the gradual materialization of neighborhood decline — as opposed to sudden shocks — reduce homeowner salience and maintenance response, producing larger eventual depreciation than equivalent sudden negative shocks of the same total magnitude?
method: Difference-in-differences design comparing neighborhoods exposed to gradual retail vacancy accumulation versus neighborhoods experiencing sudden anchor-store closures of equivalent magnitude, using Yelp business closure data and CoreLogic property transaction and assessment records.
data: Yelp business closure records (2010–2022) merged with CoreLogic AVM time series and maintenance permit data for 850 U.S. zip codes, identifying gradual multi-year decline sequences versus sudden single-quarter shock events of comparable cumulative vacancy magnitude.
key_finding: Neighborhoods experiencing gradual retail decline may show 15–28% less maintenance permit activity and 3–6% lower property value trajectories over five years than neighborhoods experiencing equivalent sudden shocks, consistent with an 'out of sight, out of mind' salience failure.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the gradual versus sudden public bad experiment paradigm into the neighborhood externalities literature, providing the first causal test of whether the temporal pattern of decline — not just its magnitude — independently affects homeowner maintenance and property values.
nn_title: Characteristics of Depreciation in Commercial and Multifamily Property: An Inves
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do real estate investors exhibit the disposition effect when managing rental property portfolios — holding underperforming properties too long and selling appreciating properties too early — and does this produce measurable return shortfalls relative to passive strategies?
method: Tracking rental property portfolio decisions of individual investors using county assessor and deed records linked to rental registration databases, constructing investor-level disposition measures and comparing realized returns to simulated passive hold strategies.
data: Administrative rental registration and property transfer records from Seattle, Denver, and Atlanta (2010–2022) merged with county assessor valuation histories, covering approximately 45,000 individual investors holding two or more rental properties for at least three years.
key_finding: Small portfolio rental investors may exhibit a disposition ratio of 1.4–2.1 (selling appreciated properties 40–110% more frequently than depreciated properties relative to the baseline), with an annual return shortfall of 1.2–2.8 percentage points versus passive hold strategies.
novelty_rationale: Extends the disposition effect — well-documented in stock portfolios — to individual landlord real estate investment decisions, a market with much higher transaction costs and less frequent trading where disposition biases may produce larger and more persistent welfare losses.
nn_title: Characteristics of Depreciation in Commercial and Multifamily Property: An Inves
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the group norm learning asymmetry — whereby low-status members are perceived as more informative about true norms — cause homebuyers to weight the revealed preferences of recent first-time buyers over experienced repeat buyers when forming neighborhood quality beliefs?
method: Experimental vignette study with active homebuyers presenting neighborhood demand signals from attributed first-time versus experienced repeat buyers, combined with a hedonic analysis testing whether first-time buyer transaction prices predict subsequent market prices more strongly than repeat buyer prices.
data: Vignette experiment with 960 active homebuyers combined with HMDA purchase-type data (first-time versus repeat buyer flags) merged with CoreLogic price series for 2,200 zip codes from 2012–2022, testing Granger-causality of different buyer-type price signals.
key_finding: First-time buyer willingness to pay may predict subsequent market-wide price appreciation 1–3 quarters ahead better than repeat buyer prices (out-of-sample R² advantage of 0.04–0.09), and vignette participants may weight first-time buyer revealed preferences as more informative about true neighborhood values.
novelty_rationale: Applies the counterintuitive 'low-ranking members as best norm sources' finding to housing markets, where first-time buyers — analogous to low-status group members without prior neighborhood exposure — may actually be more reliable demand signals than experienced repeat buyers.
nn_title: Appraisal overvaluation: Evidence of price adjustment bias in sales Comparisons
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do real estate agents exhibit a 'superstar halo effect' whereby the reputation of a listing agent independently biases buyers' perceived quality of the property itself, leading to measurable price premiums unrelated to objective property characteristics?
method: Field experiment randomizing the prominence of high-reputation versus low-reputation agent identity signals in otherwise identical online property listings, with hedonic regression to isolate reputation-induced price and time-on-market effects from property fundamentals.
data: Online property listing platform data from a major U.S. MLS paired with agent reputation metrics (transaction volume, ratings, years of experience) across 50,000+ listings in 10 metropolitan areas, 2018–2024.
key_finding: High-reputation agent identity may inflate buyer willingness to pay by 2–6%, independent of property attributes, with effects concentrated among out-of-state buyers facing higher information asymmetry.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the superstar reputation bias from sports performance perception to real estate brokerage, testing whether agent celebrity independently distorts asset valuation rather than merely reflecting genuine service quality differences.
nn_title: Asymmetric information and personal affiliations in brokered housing transaction
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does prenatal testosterone exposure, proxied by the digit ratio of real estate investors, predict domain-specific risk tolerance in commercial versus residential investment decisions, explaining cross-sectional heterogeneity in portfolio leverage and asset concentration?
method: Survey-based collection of digit ratios from individual real estate investors combined with revealed preference measures of portfolio risk (leverage ratios, geographic concentration, asset class mix) and standard risk tolerance questionnaires, with regression controlling for experience and wealth.
data: Survey data from individual accredited real estate investors recruited through investor networks and real estate investment associations, with linked portfolio records for approximately 500 participants across the U.S.
key_finding: Lower digit ratios (higher prenatal testosterone) may predict 10–20% higher leverage ratios and greater geographic concentration, with effects stronger in commercial than residential portfolios, suggesting biological factors explain a non-trivial share of investor heterogeneity.
novelty_rationale: Introduces biological determinants of investor behavior into real estate finance, testing whether prenatal hormonal exposure shapes the domain-specific risk appetite that drives observed cross-sectional variation in real estate portfolio risk-taking.
nn_title: How do Personal Real Estate Transactions Affect Productivity and Risk Taking? Ev
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'zero-price effect' when evaluating seller concessions — disproportionately overvaluing offers with zero closing costs relative to economically equivalent price reductions — leading to systematic mispricing in negotiated transaction outcomes?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis comparing final sale prices and concession structures across transactions where sellers offered zero closing costs versus equivalent price discounts, exploiting variation in local market conventions and agent practices as instruments for concession form.
data: Transaction-level MLS data merged with public deed records capturing seller concession amounts and types across 200,000+ residential sales in 15 U.S. metropolitan areas from 2015–2023.
key_finding: Buyers may overpay by 1.5–3.5% when receiving zero-cost concessions relative to equivalent price reductions, with effects larger for first-time buyers and in markets with higher listing complexity, consistent with zero-price psychological overvaluation.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the zero-price effect from consumer goods markets into real estate negotiation, testing whether the cognitive overvaluation of 'free' services causes systematic mispricing in high-stakes property transactions where economically equivalent offers take structurally different forms.
nn_title: Bargaining over residential real estate: evidence from England
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do commercial real estate fund managers who receive performance feedback framed as 'below benchmark' subsequently increase portfolio risk-taking — analogous to the loss-chasing after paper losses — even when absolute returns are positive?
method: Panel regression linking quarterly fund manager performance feedback (relative to benchmark) to subsequent portfolio risk-taking measures (leverage, geographic concentration, illiquidity of holdings), with fund and manager fixed effects and instrumental variables based on benchmark construction rules.
data: NCREIF and Preqin fund-level data on open-end and closed-end commingled real estate funds with manager-level performance tracking from 2000–2023, covering approximately 400 funds.
key_finding: Managers receiving negative relative performance feedback may increase portfolio volatility by 15–25% in subsequent quarters, concentrating in higher-risk asset classes, with effects stronger when manager compensation is tied to benchmark-relative returns.
novelty_rationale: Adapts experimental evidence on chasing emotional losses to the institutional real estate fund context, testing whether benchmark-relative framing — rather than absolute performance — triggers risk escalation that cannot be explained by rational rebalancing motives.
nn_title: Further Evidence on the Performance of Funds of Funds: The Case of Real Estate M
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers exhibit a 'preference for quicker offers' bias — disproportionately accepting the first mortgage offer received when evaluated in isolation but switching preferences when offers are compared jointly — generating systematic welfare losses from suboptimal lender selection?
method: Randomized controlled trial embedded in an online mortgage comparison platform, varying whether borrowers see loan offers sequentially (separate evaluation) or simultaneously (joint evaluation), with measurement of final lender choice, accepted interest rate, and self-reported decision satisfaction.
data: Platform-level data from a U.S. online mortgage marketplace generating approximately 20,000 randomized borrower assignments across sequential and simultaneous offer presentation conditions, 2022–2024.
key_finding: Sequential offer presentation may increase acceptance of first-received offers by 20–35% and raise accepted interest rates by 8–15 basis points, with effects concentrated among financially less sophisticated borrowers, implying substantial aggregate welfare losses.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the evaluation mode dependence of temporal offer preferences into mortgage markets, testing whether the architecture of how loan offers are presented — rather than borrower financial literacy alone — drives the well-documented failure of mortgage shopping.
nn_title: Paying Too Much? Borrower Sophistication and Overpayment in the U.S. Mortgage Ma
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does the experience of winning or losing a prior property auction causally affect subsequent bidding behavior and cheating on property disclosures in the next transaction, with losers bidding more aggressively and being more likely to conceal defects?
method: Quasi-experimental analysis using auction clearance outcomes at property auctions as plausibly exogenous variation in win/loss experience, linking bidder identities across sequential auction participations to subsequent bidding increments and disclosure complaint records.
data: Australian residential property auction records linked to bidder identities and subsequent transaction histories over 2010–2022, combined with consumer protection complaint data on disclosure violations, covering approximately 150,000 auction events.
key_finding: Prior auction losers may bid 5–12% more aggressively in subsequent auctions within 90 days and show 30–50% higher rates of disclosure complaints, consistent with competitive loss escalating both effort and ethical boundary violations.
novelty_rationale: Translates experimental evidence on how winning and losing competitions affects subsequent cheating and effort into real property auctions, providing the first field-based test of whether competitive outcome history shapes both bidding intensity and seller disclosure ethics.
nn_title: Cooling Auction Fever: Evidence from the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do real estate investors exhibit a 'sense of responsibility' effect — becoming more risk-averse when they know they are deciding on behalf of a principal (e.g., pension fund beneficiaries) rather than themselves — and does this explain the documented underperformance of fiduciary real estate mandates?
method: Laboratory experiment presenting real estate investment scenarios to experienced investors randomly assigned to decide for themselves versus as fiduciaries, measuring risk choices, expected return targets, and diversification decisions, with follow-up field validation using institutional fund performance data.
data: Laboratory sample of 300 real estate investment professionals supplemented by NCREIF institutional fund performance data decomposing returns by mandate type (discretionary versus advisory fiduciary), 2005–2023.
key_finding: Fiduciary framing may reduce willingness to take on idiosyncratic property risk by 20–30% and increase benchmark-hugging behavior, explaining 30–50 basis points of the return gap between discretionary and non-discretionary institutional real estate mandates.
novelty_rationale: Applies experimental findings on responsibility-induced risk aversion to institutional real estate investment, proposing that the fiduciary framing itself — not only agency conflicts — mechanically reduces risk-taking and contributes to observed performance patterns.
nn_title: How do Personal Real Estate Transactions Affect Productivity and Risk Taking? Ev
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'gist reasoning deficit' — relying on categorical rather than verbatim numerical representations of price-to-income ratios — causing systematic errors in affordability self-assessment that predict subsequent mortgage distress?
method: Survey instrument measuring gist versus verbatim numerical reasoning ability among recent mortgage applicants, linked to ex post mortgage performance outcomes, with mediation analysis testing whether affordability misassessment at origination mediates the gist-default relationship.
data: Linked HMDA origination data with credit bureau performance records for 50,000 recent first-time buyers, merged with a survey measuring fuzzy-trace theory numeracy constructs administered at loan origination through lender partnerships.
key_finding: Borrowers relying on categorical gist reasoning ('this seems affordable') rather than verbatim ratio calculations may show 40–60% higher 90-day delinquency rates within 3 years, with effects concentrated at LTV ratios near regulatory thresholds where exact calculations are most consequential.
novelty_rationale: Introduces fuzzy-trace theory's distinction between gist and verbatim numerical processing into mortgage origination, arguing that the form of affordability cognition — not just financial literacy levels — independently predicts mortgage default beyond conventional underwriting variables.
nn_title: Do borrowers know their mortgage terms?
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does the sequential order in which property attributes are processed during online search create an incumbency advantage for first-viewed listings, causing buyers to evaluate later-discovered properties unfavorably even when they are objectively superior?
method: Eye-tracking and click-stream experiment randomly varying the order in which property attributes (price, size, location, photos) load in online listing presentations, measuring information search patterns, attribute weighting, and final choice consistency with stated preferences.
data: Laboratory eye-tracking study with 400 homebuyer participants viewing randomized property listing sequences, supplemented by field data from a real estate platform's A/B test of listing order randomization linked to inquiry and offer rates for 100,000 listings.
key_finding: First-viewed listing attributes may receive 25–40% higher weight in final preferences regardless of quality, with photographs viewed first creating the strongest incumbency effect and causing buyers to pay premiums of 2–4% relative to reservation prices elicited before viewing.
novelty_rationale: Transplants query theory's memory retrieval ordering mechanism from political candidate evaluation to property search, testing whether the incumbency advantage in decision-making operates through search sequence rather than genuine preference differences in high-stakes housing markets.
nn_title: What Problem Do Intermediaries Solve? Evidence From Real Estate Markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do landlords who receive negative payment history feedback about tenants exhibit a systematic 'lie deflator' moral reminder effect — temporarily reducing subsequent discrimination in tenant screening — or does feedback instead escalate screening stringency for minority applicants?
method: Audit correspondence experiment randomly varying whether landlords receive simulated negative payment feedback about fictional prior tenants before receiving rental inquiries from names signaling different racial and ethnic identities, measuring response rates and invitation to view rates.
data: Correspondence audit experiment sending 8,000 paired rental inquiry emails to private landlords in 20 U.S. cities, randomizing both the presence of negative prior-tenant payment feedback and the racial signal of applicant names, 2023–2024.
key_finding: Negative payment feedback may reduce overall response rates by 15–25% while paradoxically increasing racial disparities in screening by 10–20 percentage points, as landlords use the moral reminder to justify more intensive screening of minority applicants rather than reducing discrimination.
novelty_rationale: Adapts polygraph feedback's moral reminder mechanism to rental market discrimination, testing whether negative information about prior tenants functions as a moral license that amplifies rather than suppresses discriminatory screening contrary to the lie deflator's original prosocial effect.
nn_title: Do landlords discriminate in the rental housing market? Evidence from an interne
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does the cultural tightness of local institutional environments — measured by the stringency and enforcement of local zoning and building codes — predict the creative adaptation of commercial real estate spaces (adaptive reuse, mixed-use conversion) through its effect on developer and tenant experimentation?
method: Cross-sectional regression of adaptive reuse rates and mixed-use conversion frequency on a multi-dimensional index of local regulatory cultural tightness constructed from zoning variance denial rates, code enforcement actions, and permit processing times, with instrumental variables based on historical civic governance traditions.
data: CoStar property transaction and use-change database covering 300 U.S. metropolitan areas from 2000–2022, merged with local government regulatory enforcement records and a novel regulatory tightness index constructed from public records.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in regulatory cultural tightness may reduce adaptive reuse rates by 20–35% and delay mixed-use conversion by 3–5 years, with formal tightness having larger effects than informal norms, suggesting regulatory culture constrains the creative renewal of commercial property stock.
novelty_rationale: Transplants organizational psychology's cultural tightness framework into real estate land use change, proposing that the institutional norm-enforcement stringency of local governance — not just specific regulations — independently suppresses the creative adaptation that drives urban commercial property renewal.
nn_title: Entrepreneurial land developers: Joint production, local externalities, and mixe
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Do real estate market participants exhibit a 'cherry-picking tolerance' asymmetry — accepting selective presentation of favorable market comparables by agents as legitimate while perceiving outright misrepresentation as clearly unacceptable — even when both strategies produce equivalent mispricing?
method: Factorial vignette experiment presenting homebuyers and sellers with scenarios varying whether price misinformation is conveyed through cherry-picked comparables versus fabricated data, measuring perceived agent trustworthiness, intention to file complaints, and willingness to pay for the agent's services.
data: Online experiment with 2,000 participants recruited from a nationally representative homebuyer panel, using 2×2×2 design crossing information type (cherry-picking vs. fabrication), market direction (rising vs. falling), and participant role (buyer vs. seller).
key_finding: Cherry-picking of comparables may be rated as approximately 40% less morally objectionable than equivalent fabrication and generate 60% fewer stated complaint intentions, despite producing similar price distortions, implying that selective disclosure conventions create systematic moral blind spots in real estate agency oversight.
novelty_rationale: Introduces the cherry-picking tolerance asymmetry from media truthfulness research into real estate agency, testing whether the form of information distortion — selective omission versus outright misrepresentation — systematically affects moral accountability in a context where comparable selection is ubiquitous and consequential.
nn_title: Asymmetric information and personal affiliations in brokered housing transaction
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms
