novelty_rationale: While prior work unsmooths appraisal bias using linear or regime-switching time-series filters, this idea imports the high-frequency financial econometrics toolkit—specifically jump-diffusion decomposition of realized variance—into real estate, treating the irregular transaction record as the analogue of a tick-by-tick price series and directly identifying the discontinuous price-discovery component that all existing unsmoothing approaches implicitly assume away.
nn_title: A Measure of Fundamental Volatility in the Commercial Property Market
assigned_label: Real Estate Return Measurement and Volatility Estimation

research_question: How do large language model-derived semantic embeddings of neighborhood identity—extracted from hyperlocal community platforms like Nextdoor and local news archives—capitalize into residential prices beyond conventional hedonic controls, and do these perception premia exhibit spatial sorting consistent with household preference heterogeneity?
method: A two-stage hedonic framework in which sentence-transformer embeddings of geo-tagged neighborhood text are reduced via UMAP to latent 'place identity' dimensions, then entered as regressors in a repeat-sales-augmented hedonic model with spatial error correction to identify perception premia net of physical amenities and school quality.
data: Zillow Transaction and Assessor Dataset (ZTRAX) for sale prices and structural attributes across major U.S. metros (2010–2022), combined with publicly archived Nextdoor neighborhood posts obtained via the platform's research data access program and ProQuest historical local newspaper archives for semantic neighborhood content.
key_finding: Latent neighborhood identity dimensions are expected to explain 3–8% of residual price variation beyond standard hedonic controls, with perception premia concentrated in transitional neighborhoods and exhibiting asymmetric capitalization that accelerates during gentrification cycles.
novelty_rationale: While prior work extracts value from property-level text, this paper is the first to operationalize community-level semantic identity as a time-varying hedonic attribute, directly testing whether collective neighborhood narrative—distinct from measured amenities—is priced by the market and drives spatial sorting.
nn_title: An Empirical Examination of Traditional Neighborhood Development
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the optimal city size distribution implied by productivity and amenity gradients systematically diverge from the observed Zipf distribution, and what frictions explain the gap?
method: A quantitative spatial equilibrium model calibrated to match observed wages, rents, and amenity values across U.S. metros is used to back out the welfare-maximizing city size distribution via a planner's problem, then a decomposition identifies how housing supply regulations, migration costs, and agglomeration externalities each account for deviations between the efficient and observed distributions.
data: American Community Survey (2005–2023) for wages, rents, and migration flows at the metro level; Wharton Residential Land Use Regulatory Index for supply restrictions; EPA Smart Location Database for density and amenity proxies; Census LEHD for worker-level productivity estimates across metros of different sizes.
key_finding: The efficient distribution is likely more dispersed than the observed one—favoring mid-sized cities relative to the largest metros—with housing supply restrictions explaining the majority of the divergence by suppressing population in high-productivity cities, while migration costs account for persistence of oversized declining metros.
novelty_rationale: Prior work treats city size distributions as purely descriptive or tests statistical laws; this paper is the first to structurally identify the gap between the observed and socially optimal size distribution and to quantify which specific market and policy frictions generate that wedge.
nn_title: An indirect test of efficient city sizes
assigned_label: City size distribution and optimality

research_question: How do remote work shocks differentially reshape spatial equilibrium across the urban hierarchy, and do standard monocentric models systematically mis-predict the magnitude and heterogeneity of rent and population adjustments?
method: A quantitative spatial general equilibrium model with endogenous amenity valuation and heterogeneous workers (by skill and remote-work eligibility) is estimated and then subjected to a calibrated telework shock, with model predictions validated against realized post-2020 rent and migration outcomes to identify structural mis-specification in canonical urban models.
data: Zillow Research Data (ZORI rent indices at ZIP-code level, 2015–2024), IRS Statistics of Income county-to-county migration flows, American Community Survey occupation-level remote-work eligibility rates (Dingel–Neiman classification), and LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics for commuting patterns across all U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Standard monocentric spatial equilibrium models are likely to underpredict rent declines in dense urban cores and overpredict them in peripheral suburbs by 30–50%, with the gap attributable to the model's omission of endogenous agglomeration externalities that attenuate as remote work reduces effective density.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior urban equilibrium work that treats income as exogenous or focuses on static comparative statics, this paper uses a large, quasi-experimental shock (COVID-era telework adoption) as an external validation device to formally test and diagnose structural failure modes in the urban spatial equilibrium framework itself, turning the pandemic into a natural stress-test of urban theory.
nn_title: The remote work revolution: Impact on real estate values and the urban environme
assigned_label: Spatial labor markets, wages, and worker mobility

research_question: How does the spatial mismatch between affordable housing locations and job centers differentially affect residential location choice and commuting burden across income quintiles in the post-pandemic era of hybrid work?
method: A two-stage structural model combining a discrete residential location choice framework with a commuting cost minimization problem, instrumented with historical zoning restrictiveness indices to address endogeneity between housing affordability and job accessibility.
data: Linked American Community Survey microdata (2018–2023) merged with LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics (LODES), HUD Fair Market Rent areas, and the Wharton Residential Land Use Regulatory Index for approximately 200 metropolitan statistical areas across the U.S.
key_finding: Low-income households are plausibly found to bear two to three times greater commuting cost burdens relative to income than high-income households, with hybrid work policies having widened rather than narrowed this spatial inequality gap by enabling affluent workers to suburbanize without proportional commute penalties.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior discrete choice studies that estimate average price and commute elasticities or ethnic-group comparisons of commuting propensity, this paper structurally identifies how hybrid work has reshaped the spatial mismatch mechanism asymmetrically across the income distribution, a question neither the telecommuting literature nor the spatial mismatch literature has addressed using post-pandemic data with income-stratified structural estimation.
nn_title: Wages, Housing Prices and Commutes
assigned_label: Residential Location Choice and Commuting Behavior

research_question: Does the spatial targeting of housing vouchers toward low-poverty neighborhoods improve long-run labor market and health outcomes for recipients, and does any such benefit come at the cost of displacing unassisted low-income renters through rent inflation in those destination neighborhoods?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of HUD's Small Area Fair Market Rent (SAFMR) rule across metropolitan areas—which tied voucher subsidy levels to zip-code-level rents rather than metro-wide averages—to simultaneously estimate recipient-level outcomes and neighborhood-level rent spillovers using a two-sided treatment framework.
data: HUD administrative voucher records linked to IRS Form 1040 earnings and SNAP/Medicaid enrollment data (accessible via the HUD-IRS data-sharing agreement used in prior Moving to Opportunity follow-up studies), combined with CoreLogic deed and rental listing transaction data and American Community Survey tract-level rent indices, covering 2010–2023 across the roughly 24 MTAs subject to the SAFMR pilot and subsequent rule.
key_finding: Spatial retargeting likely improves 10-year earnings and health outcomes for movers by 8–15 percent relative to control-group voucher holders, but plausibly generates 3–7 percent rent increases for unassisted renters in destination zip codes, implying meaningful distributional trade-offs that aggregate welfare analyses of voucher expansion have not yet quantified.
novelty_rationale: Existing Moving to Opportunity and SAFMR studies examine either recipient outcomes or neighborhood rent effects in isolation; this design is the first to jointly identify both margins from the same policy variation, directly quantifying the equity-efficiency trade-off between assisted and unassisted low-income households that general-equilibrium shelter-allowance models have theorized but never empirically resolved.
nn_title: Housing demand and neighborhood choice with housing vouchers
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: How does the rise of e-commerce penetration differentially erode asking and effective rents across commercial retail submarkets, and does this erosion vary systematically with the share of experiential versus goods-selling tenants in a center?
method: A panel difference-in-differences design exploiting county-level variation in broadband internet rollout speed as an instrument for e-commerce adoption, combined with submarket-level rent indices, to identify causal effects on asking rents, effective rents, and the experiential-versus-retail tenant composition share simultaneously.
data: CoStar Group's commercial lease transaction database (asking and effective rents, tenant category, lease type, submarket, 2000–2023) merged with FCC Form 477 broadband deployment data and Census County Business Patterns data on retail store counts by NAICS category, covering all major U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: E-commerce penetration likely compresses effective rents for goods-selling tenants by 8–15% but leaves experiential-tenant rents largely unaffected or slightly elevated, implying that centers with higher experiential shares are significantly insulated from online retail displacement and command a growing rent premium over time.
novelty_rationale: Existing theoretical work models tenant mix and rent determination in isolation from the digital retail channel; this paper provides the first causal empirical estimate of how the goods-versus-experiential tenant composition mediates the pass-through of e-commerce shocks into commercial rents, bridging the agglomeration-tenant-mix literature with the empirical digital disruption literature.
nn_title: Competition, Concentration and Percentage Rent in Retail Leasing
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Does the adoption of algorithmic rent-setting software (e.g., RealPage's YieldStar) by large multifamily landlords cause supracompetitive rent increases in local markets, and does market concentration amplify this effect?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting plausibly exogenous variation in the timing of algorithmic pricing software adoption across metropolitan markets, using building-level adoption dates obtained via litigation discovery records and industry contract data, instrumenting for concentration with historical zoning restrictiveness as a supply-side shifter.
data: CoStar/RealPage transaction and asking-rent panel data (2010–2023) covering roughly 13 million multifamily units nationally, merged with HUD Fair Market Rents, Census ACS rental cost burden measures, and algorithmically adopting landlords identified through publicly available court filings from the DOJ antitrust investigation and class-action lawsuits against RealPage.
key_finding: Algorithmic adoption is likely to raise asking rents by 3–8% in markets where the top-four landlords jointly cover more than 30% of units, with effects attenuating toward zero in fragmented markets, suggesting collusion requires both the coordination mechanism and sufficient market power to be effective.
novelty_rationale: While the antitrust allegations against RealPage have attracted legal scrutiny, no peer-reviewed study has credibly estimated a causal rent effect using the staggered adoption design combined with a concentration moderator, linking algorithmic pricing to the broader literature on landlord market power rather than treating it as a binary presence/absence question.
nn_title: Structural change in the US office market after 2019: Evidence from lease‐level
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Does the algorithmic sorting of mortgage applicants by automated underwriting systems perpetuate or amplify racial residential segregation beyond what can be explained by individual creditworthiness differences?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting sharp credit-score cutoffs in Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac automated underwriting systems to estimate race-differential approval rates and subsequent neighborhood sorting among applicants near the threshold, with spatial equilibrium modeling to link credit-market exclusion to segregation dynamics.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) loan-level data (1990–2023) merged with Census tract-level racial composition from the Decennial Census and American Community Survey, supplemented by CoreLogic deed and mortgage records available via institutional research agreements to recover property locations and subsequent mobility outcomes.
key_finding: Applicants from minority racial groups who are marginally rejected relative to observably equivalent white applicants are displaced into higher-segregation tracts, with the effect growing substantially after the 1995 and 2004 expansions of automated underwriting adoption, suggesting algorithmic credit rationing accounts for a measurable share of post-1990 segregation persistence.
novelty_rationale: Existing segregation research focuses on prejudice, preferences, or historical policy, but no study uses a quasi-experimental credit-threshold design to isolate how algorithmic underwriting rules—largely invisible to prior theoretical frameworks—translate credit-market exclusion directly into spatial racial sorting.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does the expansion of ride-hailing services (Uber/Lyft) differentially reduce spatial mismatch and narrow racial employment gaps by lowering effective transportation costs for Black workers in transit-poor suburban job corridors?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered market entry of ride-hailing platforms across metropolitan areas, interacted with neighborhood-level racial composition and transit access scores, to estimate heterogeneous employment effects by race using Census LEHD origin-destination employment data.
data: Uber/Lyft city-level launch dates (documented in prior published research and company disclosures), Census LEHD Longitudinal Employer-Household Dynamics data (2010–2019), American Community Survey tract-level race and commuting data, and GTFS transit network files from the National Transit Database.
key_finding: Ride-hailing entry plausibly increases employment rates for Black workers in low-transit-access neighborhoods by 1–4 percentage points relative to white workers in the same areas, with larger effects where suburban job growth is concentrated far from legacy transit lines.
novelty_rationale: Prior spatial mismatch research focuses on fixed infrastructure (transit lines, job suburbanization) and residential sorting, but ignores on-demand mobility platforms as a potentially race-differentiated shock to effective job accessibility that can be identified causally through staggered platform entry.
nn_title: Is Uber a substitute or complement for public transit?
assigned_label: Spatial mismatch and racial employment inequality

research_question: Does the announcement versus opening of Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) infrastructure generate differential capitalization effects in housing markets compared to rail transit, and how does BRT's inferior 'permanence signal' moderate investor expectations?
method: Regression discontinuity in time (RDiT) design exploiting precise announcement and opening dates of BRT corridors across multiple U.S. cities, with a triple-difference specification comparing BRT versus contemporaneous light rail openings in comparable metropolitan contexts to isolate mode-specific credibility discounts in capitalization.
data: CoreLogic or Zillow transaction-level repeat-sales data (2000–2023) merged with GTFS historical feed archives and FTA Capital Investment Grant project timelines covering BRT and LRT corridors in cities including Cleveland, Kansas City, Los Angeles, and Pittsburgh, supplemented by parcel-level distance calculations using OpenStreetMap network geometries.
key_finding: BRT corridors are plausibly expected to generate 30–60% smaller capitalization effects than equivalent rail investments at announcement, with the gap narrowing but not closing at opening, consistent with rational discounting of infrastructure impermanence and route-change risk by housing market participants.
novelty_rationale: While extensive literature documents rail capitalization, no study rigorously isolates the mode-specific credibility discount by directly comparing BRT and LRT capitalization trajectories from announcement through opening using a unified identification strategy, leaving the 'permanence premium' of rail as an untested mechanism.
nn_title: Valuing public transit: The L‐train shutdown
assigned_label: Transit infrastructure capitalization in urban housing

research_question: How does the introduction of congestion pricing on urban toll corridors capitalize into residential property values heterogeneously across the income distribution, and does this capitalization effect offset or amplify the regressive burden of tolls on lower-income households?
method: Difference-in-differences hedonic price regression exploiting the staggered rollout of congestion pricing schemes across U.S. and European cities, combined with a structural model that separates the travel-time amenity value gain from the direct toll cost burden to decompose net welfare effects by income quintile.
data: Zillow Transaction and Assessment Database (ZTRAX) or CoreLogic deed-level transaction records for residential properties within 5 km of tolled corridors in Stockholm, Gothenburg, Milan, and U.S. express-lane corridors (SR-91, I-405, I-95) from 1995–2023, merged with U.S. Census tract-level income data and INRIX historical traffic speed data.
key_finding: Congestion pricing is plausibly found to generate positive property value capitalization of 3–8% near tolled corridors for high-income neighborhoods while producing negligible or negative capitalization in low-income neighborhoods that rely on tolled routes for employment access, implying that standard welfare analyses understate the distributional costs of pricing schemes.
novelty_rationale: Existing congestion pricing literature focuses on aggregate welfare gains or traffic flow outcomes, while the real estate literature treats tolls as simple disamenities; this paper is the first to jointly identify the amenity capitalization channel and the income-heterogeneous burden channel using quasi-experimental variation in toll introduction, directly linking transportation pricing policy to housing wealth inequality.
nn_title: The incidence of congestion tolls on urban highways
assigned_label: Congestion pricing, tolls, and capacity optimization

research_question: How do school district boundary discontinuities interact with life-cycle stage to differentially accelerate or suppress residential mobility, and does this mechanism explain a meaningful share of observed neighborhood income sorting?
method: A regression discontinuity design exploiting sharp attendance-zone boundaries, combined with a duration model of residential spell length, estimated separately by household age-of-youngest-child cohorts to identify boundary-crossing mobility premiums as children approach school-entry and transition ages.
data: Linked American Housing Survey panel waves (1985–2021), CoreLogic deed-transfer records with buyer demographics, NCES Common Core school district boundary shapefiles, and GreatSchools or Stanford Education Data Archive test-score ratings for school quality measurement across U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Households with children aged 4–6 living just outside high-rated attendance zones are plausibly 30–50% more likely to move within two years than observationally similar households on the high-rated side, generating a durable sorting wedge that standard mobility models omit by ignoring the non-linear interaction between child age and boundary proximity.
novelty_rationale: While school quality effects on house prices are well-documented and mobility studies note life-cycle patterns, no existing paper jointly identifies the causal timing mechanism — that boundary-induced moves are concentrated in narrow developmental windows — and traces its aggregate contribution to income-based neighborhood sorting.
nn_title: The desegregating effect of school tracking
assigned_label: School quality capitalization into housing prices

research_question: How does the sudden, exogenous shift to remote work following COVID-19 alter the residential location choices of single- versus dual-earner households differently, and what does this reveal about the latent demand for space versus proximity to employment centers?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting cross-industry and cross-occupation variation in remote-work feasibility (using Dingel-Neiman teleworkability scores) to identify causal effects on household relocation decisions, distance moved from employment centers, and housing consumption (size, tenure, density), separately estimated for single- and dual-earner households.
data: Linked American Community Survey (2018–2023) microdata providing household composition, earnings, industry, occupation, and residential location combined with USPS address-change records and Zillow transaction-level data covering millions of home purchases across U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Dual-earner households are expected to relocate significantly less—and to shorter distances—than single-earner households even when both workers are fully remote, consistent with the geometric median problem of joint commute minimization, implying that removing the commute constraint disproportionately liberates single-earner households and generates larger housing-consumption upgrades for them.
novelty_rationale: While prior work models dual-earner location constraints theoretically or studies general pandemic-era moves, no study has used teleworkability as a quasi-experiment to separately identify how the commuting constraint binds differently for single- versus dual-earner households and quantify the resulting divergence in spatial sorting and housing-consumption responses.
nn_title: A theory of residential location decisions of two-worker households
assigned_label: Household residential location and mobility decisions

research_question: How does the three-dimensional vertical density of urban development—captured through building height distributions—alter the relationship between horizontal population density gradients and urban land values, and does incorporating vertical density resolve the systematic mis-pricing anomalies documented in standard monocentric gradient models?
method: A structural spatial equilibrium model that augments the classic negative exponential density gradient with a floor-area-ratio index derived from LiDAR-based building height data, estimated via instrumental variables using historical zoning height limits as instruments for endogenous vertical density, with land value gradients identified from transaction-level data.
data: USGS 3DEP LiDAR point-cloud data covering ~80 U.S. metros, ZTRAX parcel-level transaction records (2000–2022), Lincoln Institute land value estimates, and historical zoning ordinances digitized by the National Zoning Atlas, providing full 3-D urban form coverage across multiple decades.
key_finding: Incorporating vertical density is expected to reduce gradient mis-specification bias by 30–50% in high-rise metros and reveal that standard monocentric models systematically underestimate centrality premiums in cities where height restrictions were relaxed after 2010, with heterogeneous effects across sunbelt versus legacy industrial metros.
novelty_rationale: Existing density gradient literature is exclusively two-dimensional, treating the urban surface as flat; this paper introduces a volumetric reformulation of the monocentric model that links airspace utilization to land pricing, a conceptual advance enabled only recently by the widespread availability of public LiDAR coverage and parcel-level transaction databases.
nn_title: Constraints on land consumption and urban rent gradients
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: How does the spatial fragmentation of land tenure regimes—specifically the mosaic of formal, informal, and hybrid property rights within a single metropolitan area—shape intra-city productivity gradients and long-run urban form?
method: A spatial regression discontinuity design exploiting sharp cadastral boundaries between formally titled and informally occupied parcels within the same metropolitan area, combined with a dynamic structural model of land use transition to identify causal effects of tenure security on floor-area ratios, firm agglomeration, and commuting patterns.
data: Brazilian IBGE Census microdata (2000, 2010, 2022) merged with INCRA land-title registry shapefiles, municipal cadaster records from the SEFIN fiscal databases of São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro, and OpenStreetMap building-footprint extracts—collectively covering tens of millions of parcels across the two largest Latin American metros.
key_finding: Tenure fragmentation likely generates discontinuous productivity cliffs of 15–35% at cadastral boundaries and induces a polycentric urban structure skewed toward informal commercial nodes, implying that standard monocentric models systematically misattribute agglomeration premia that are actually tenure-driven wedges.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats Latin American urban structure descriptively or models slum growth in aggregate, this design uses quasi-experimental variation at the micro-parcel level to causally identify how within-city property-rights heterogeneity—rather than infrastructure or density alone—endogenously generates the distinctive fragmented spatial structures observed in the region.
nn_title: Externalities, Indivisibility, Nonreplicability, and Agglomeration
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: Do opportunity zone designations generate genuine neighborhood revitalization or merely accelerate displacement of low-income residents without improving their welfare?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the arbitrary eligibility cutoff in the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act opportunity zone selection criteria, comparing census tracts just below and just above the qualifying income threshold, with a stacked event-study specification to separately identify effects on property values, new construction, small-business formation, and incumbent resident outcomes (poverty rates, residential mobility, and commute-adjusted income).
data: IRS opportunity zone tract designations merged with LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics (2012–2023), HMDA mortgage origination records, CoStar commercial transaction data, USPS vacancy files, and individual-level longitudinal earnings from the Census Bureau's Longitudinal Employer-Household Dynamics database, covering all 8,764 designated zones nationally.
key_finding: Opportunity zone designation likely raises commercial property values and new construction activity by 10–20% in treated tracts, but incumbent low-income residents experience net welfare losses through rent appreciation and displacement, with employment gains accruing primarily to higher-skill in-migrants rather than pre-existing residents.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior opportunity zone studies that focus on aggregate investment flows or property prices alone, this design uses longitudinal worker-level earnings linked to tract-of-residence to decompose whether capital tax incentives improve incumbent resident welfare or merely restructure neighborhood demographics—directly reframing the policy debate from place-based to people-based outcomes.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Do firms systematically misprice commercial real estate location decisions by underweighting agglomeration spillovers from knowledge-intensive neighbors, and does this misallocation predict subsequent productivity divergence across otherwise similar establishments?
method: A two-stage empirical design: first, estimate a revealed-preference model of firm location choice using a conditional logit framework with establishment-level fixed effects to recover implicit valuations of neighbor characteristics; second, exploit quasi-random variation in neighbor composition generated by large anchor-tenant relocations and plant closures as instruments to identify the causal effect of knowledge-neighbor proximity on total factor productivity growth, testing whether firms' ex-ante location valuations predict ex-post productivity outcomes.
data: U.S. Census Bureau Longitudinal Business Database (LBD) and Census of Manufactures linked to CoStar commercial lease transaction records (used in published real estate research) for establishment addresses and lease terms, supplemented by USPTO patent data for knowledge spillover proxies and BLS Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages for local labor market controls, covering approximately 1995–2019.
key_finding: Firms are likely to systematically underprice proximity to high-patent-intensity neighbors by 20–40% relative to the productivity gains such proximity generates, with the misallocation gap largest for young firms and in sectors with high tacit-knowledge intensity, implying a quantifiable welfare loss from suboptimal location sorting.
novelty_rationale: While prior work separates consumption from production agglomeration or documents attenuation of cluster benefits, no study has directly tested whether firms' own location-choice valuations are systematically biased relative to realized productivity outcomes, framing agglomeration as a behavioral finance problem of corporate real estate misallocation rather than a reduced-form spillover estimation exercise.
nn_title: The proximity-concentration trade-off with profit shifting
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: Does proximity to navigable waterways, historically a primary determinant of manufacturing location, still generate measurable productivity premiums for modern manufacturers, and has the advent of intermodal freight infrastructure fundamentally altered this geographic rent?
method: A difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered opening and capacity expansions of inland port terminals and intermodal rail facilities as quasi-exogenous shocks to effective transport access, comparing total factor productivity trajectories of manufacturing plants near legacy waterway corridors against those newly connected via rail-truck intermodal hubs, using plant-level production function estimation (Olley-Pakes/Levinsohn-Petrin) to recover TFP residuals.
data: U.S. Census Bureau's Longitudinal Business Database and Annual Survey of Manufactures (plant-level employment, payroll, output, and capital expenditure from 1987–2022), merged with Army Corps of Engineers waterway freight tonnage data, the Surface Transportation Board's carload waybill sample, and FHWA intermodal connector GIS shapefiles to construct time-varying transport access measures at the plant level.
key_finding: Waterway proximity likely retains a statistically significant but diminishing TFP premium (estimated 3–8%) concentrated in bulk-commodity industries, while intermodal terminal access generates a comparable or larger premium in high-value, time-sensitive manufacturing sectors, suggesting a structural rotation in the geography of transport-induced agglomeration economies.
novelty_rationale: Prior work contrasts large-city versus small-city productivity without isolating the specific transport-infrastructure channel; this design directly attributes geographic productivity variation to a measurable, time-varying infrastructure shock, separating waterway legacy effects from modern intermodal substitution in a single unified framework.
nn_title: Transport rates structure, optimum location, and theory of production: Reexamina
assigned_label: Firm location decisions and agglomeration economies

research_question: How does the staggered adoption of inclusionary zoning mandates affect land values, housing prices, and the composition of new residential development across U.S. municipalities?
method: A staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting quasi-random variation in the timing of inclusionary zoning ordinance adoptions across municipalities, using a heterogeneity-robust estimator (Callaway-Sant'Anna) to separate effects on land prices, market-rate unit prices, and total permitted units while testing for anticipatory responses and spillovers to neighboring jurisdictions.
data: Inclusionary zoning ordinance adoption dates and requirements from the Furman Center's National Inclusionary Housing Database and Grounded Solutions Network surveys (covering ~1,000 U.S. jurisdictions since the 1970s), linked to parcel-level transaction prices from CoreLogic/ATTOM, Census Bureau Building Permits Survey, and FHFA house price indices at the zip-code level.
key_finding: Inclusionary zoning mandates are plausibly found to depress raw land values by 8–20% in treated municipalities—capitalizing the affordable-unit obligation onto landowners—while simultaneously reducing total permitted units by 5–15%, with larger negative supply effects in high-demand coastal markets where the implicit tax exceeds developer profit margins.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats inclusionary zoning as a cross-sectional binary treatment or focuses solely on affordable unit production, this design leverages the full staggered rollout to causally decompose effects along the entire land-to-housing price chain, directly testing the theoretical prediction that affordability mandates are land-value taxes rather than taxes on construction.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Does remote work adoption differentially compress or widen spatial wage gradients across skill levels, and has the post-2020 telecommutability shock permanently altered the compensating differential structure that ties wages to local amenities?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting cross-occupation variation in telecommutability (using Dingel-Neiman 2020 scores as a continuous treatment intensity) interacted with pre-pandemic local amenity indices (air quality, climate, urban density) to estimate how the amenity-wage tradeoff shifted before and after March 2020, with event-study designs and synthetic control validation for high-telecommutability metro areas.
data: American Community Survey (2015–2023) merged with BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics for wage outcomes; Dingel-Neiman telecommutability scores by 6-digit SOC code; EPA AQS and NOAA climate normals for amenity measures; LinkedIn Workforce Insights aggregate mobility data (available via research partnership, used in published studies) for revealed relocations.
key_finding: Telecommutability likely eroded 20–40% of the traditional amenity-wage compensating differential for high-skill remote-capable workers while leaving low-skill workers' spatial wage gradients largely intact, producing a bifurcated spatial equilibrium that diverges sharply from classical Rosen-Roback predictions.
novelty_rationale: While prior work examines either amenity valuation or remote work in isolation, no study uses the discrete telecommutability shock as a quasi-experiment to test whether the Rosen-Roback compensating differential framework itself has structurally broken down for a specific segment of the labor market, generating fundamentally new implications for urban wage theory.
nn_title: Work location, residence location, and the intraurban wage gradient
assigned_label: Spatial labor markets, wages, and worker mobility

research_question: How do sudden, large-scale sectoral shocks—specifically the differential rollout of remote work adoption across industries—reshape the geographic distribution of employment and wages across metropolitan areas, and do pre-shock agglomeration economies amplify or dampen these spatial reallocation effects?
method: Shift-share instrumental variables design using pre-pandemic industry composition interacted with industry-level remote-work feasibility scores (Dingel and Neiman 2020) as a Bartik instrument, combined with a differences-in-differences estimator exploiting staggered return-to-office mandates to identify causal effects on metro-level employment, wages, and establishment entry/exit across skill tiers.
data: Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) at the county-industry level from 2018–2024, merged with BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics for remote-work feasibility scores, Census Bureau LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics for worker-level commuting flows, and CoStar/REIS commercial real estate vacancy data as a complementary outcome measure of structural urban adjustment.
key_finding: Metro areas with high pre-shock agglomeration in knowledge-intensive tradable sectors likely experienced the sharpest employment decentralization and wage compression at the core, while smaller secondary metros with moderate remote-work-feasible industry shares plausibly saw net employment and wage gains, with the agglomeration premium attenuating by 15–30% in the most affected sectors.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior shift-share or decomposition studies that treat sectoral shocks as cyclical demand fluctuations, this design treats remote-work adoption as a permanent technology shock with a credibly exogenous cross-industry intensity variable, allowing the first clean causal identification of whether agglomeration economies act as a stabilizing force or an amplifier of spatial labor market disruption during a structural reorganization of where work occurs.
nn_title: Wage inequality and the location of cities
assigned_label: Spatial labor markets, wages, and worker mobility

research_question: Does the advent of remote work permanently shift the geographic gradient of housing supply elasticity by decoupling labor market thickness from residential location choice, and how does this reshuffle the urban hierarchy of price responsiveness?
method: A two-stage instrumental variables approach that exploits cross-industry variation in pre-pandemic remote-work suitability (O*NET task content) as an instrument for the post-2020 shift in residential demand across metros, combined with a structural urban model that embeds supply elasticity estimates (derived from permit-to-price-change ratios) to separate demand shifters from supply responses across MSAs over 2015–2023.
data: U.S. Census Bureau Building Permits Survey and American Community Survey (MSA-level, 2015–2023), Zillow Research Data (monthly median list prices and inventory by MSA), BLS Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages for remote-work penetration rates, and O*NET occupational task scores for the remote-work suitability index.
key_finding: Supply elasticity is plausibly found to be significantly higher in mid-tier and smaller MSAs that received remote-work migrants relative to large gateway cities, suggesting that remote work has effectively flattened the historical inverse relationship between city size and supply responsiveness, with smaller metros absorbing demand shocks through quantities rather than prices.
novelty_rationale: Prior supply elasticity research treats the cross-city distribution of elasticities as structurally stable and driven by physical or regulatory fundamentals, but ignores how a fundamental shock to the spatial sorting of households can endogenously alter which margins of supply are activated, making the elasticity distribution itself a dynamic outcome of labor-market geography rather than a fixed city characteristic.
nn_title: Why we still don't know much about housing supply elasticity
assigned_label: Household residential location and mobility decisions

research_question: Does mortgage forbearance—by suspending the threat of foreclosure and reducing required cash outlays—crowd out voluntary mortgage prepayment and equity accumulation among liquidity-constrained households, creating a long-run wealth trap despite short-run consumption relief?
method: Regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences design exploiting the sharp eligibility cutoffs and staggered servicer-level rollout of COVID-19 CARES Act forbearance, using loan-level mortgage performance data matched to credit bureau records to trace post-forbearance prepayment, refinancing activity, and net housing equity over a four-year horizon.
data: Black Knight (McDash) loan-level servicing data covering ~75% of U.S. mortgages linked to Equifax or TransUnion credit panel data, supplemented by HMDA refinance records and FHFA house price indices at the zip-code level, spanning 2018–2024.
key_finding: Forbearance-eligible households are plausibly 15–30% less likely to prepay or refinance during the forbearance window despite falling rates, resulting in measurably lower accumulated equity and higher total interest costs over a five-year horizon relative to otherwise-similar ineligible households, with effects concentrated among lower-income and minority borrowers who face the steepest refinancing frictions.
novelty_rationale: While existing work examines forbearance's short-run consumption and default effects, no study has isolated the causal long-run wealth-accumulation cost of suspending repayment incentives—specifically the suppression of voluntary deleveraging and equity building—reframing forbearance as a potential double-edged instrument with heterogeneous distributional consequences.
nn_title: Interest Rate Pass-Through: Mortgage Rates, Household Consumption, and Voluntary
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: How do short-term rental platform penetration (e.g., Airbnb) differentially amplify or dampen local housing price cycles depending on underlying housing supply elasticity?
method: Two-stage instrumental variables strategy exploiting plausibly exogenous variation in Airbnb's city-level rollout timing and platform-level regulatory crackdowns as instruments for short-term rental intensity, interacted with the Saiz (2010) land-unavailability measure of housing supply elasticity to identify heterogeneous price-cycle effects.
data: Inside Airbnb and AirDNA listing-level panel data covering 2012–2023 across U.S. metro areas, merged with CoreLogic/Zillow repeat-sales house price indices at the ZIP code level, HMDA mortgage origination records, and Census Bureau housing permit data.
key_finding: Short-term rental expansion likely amplifies boom-bust price cycles in supply-constrained markets (estimated 3–8 percentage point larger peak-to-trough swings) while acting as a stabilizing demand buffer in elastic-supply markets, with the asymmetry driven by the conversion of long-term rental stock reducing effective housing supply.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work that treats second-home buying or subprime credit as the margin of speculation, this paper identifies the short-term rental market as a structurally distinct demand channel whose interaction with supply constraints generates non-linear amplification of housing cycles, a mechanism absent from the existing boom-bust literature.
nn_title: Airbnb or not Airbnb? That is the question: How Airbnb bans disrupt rental marke
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: How do unexpected climate-risk disclosures—specifically the first-time public release of high-resolution flood risk scores—causally shift renter-to-owner tenure transition probabilities, and does this effect differ systematically by household income and local rental market tightness?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered, zip-code-level rollout of First Street Foundation's Flood Factor scores (2020) as a quasi-exogenous information shock, matched to household-level tenure transitions and linked to local rental vacancy rates to test heterogeneous treatment effects across income quintiles and market-tightness terciles.
data: American Housing Survey panel waves (2019–2023) and the Current Population Survey Annual Social and Economic Supplement for tenure outcomes, merged with First Street Foundation Flood Factor public release data by Census tract, FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area boundaries, and CoStar/HUD Fair Market Rent series for rental market tightness measures.
key_finding: Households in newly disclosed high-flood-risk areas plausibly exhibit a 5–15 percentage point reduction in renter-to-owner transition rates relative to untreated areas, with low-income renters showing larger suppression due to constrained exit options, while rental market tightness moderates the effect by limiting the outside option of remaining a renter.
novelty_rationale: Existing tenure-choice literature treats risk perceptions as static or unobserved; this design isolates a clean, dated information shock to identify how forward-looking climate risk—distinct from realized disaster experience studied elsewhere—reshapes the ownership calculus, and it uniquely interacts that shock with market structure rather than treating all local housing markets as homogeneous.
nn_title: When risk does not discount: Flood history and rising property valuations
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: How does the intergenerational transfer of housing wealth through inter vivos gifts and bequests shape the tenure decisions and portfolio composition of recipient households across the wealth distribution?
method: A two-stage empirical strategy combining an event-study design around inheritance receipt with an instrumental variables approach using variation in parental mortality rates by birth cohort and region to identify causal effects on homeownership timing, mortgage leverage, and risky financial asset holdings.
data: French Enquête Patrimoine (Wealth Survey) panels linked to administrative inheritance tax records (FICOSUP/DMTG fiscal data accessible via CASD research agreements), providing longitudinal wealth, housing tenure, and transfer receipt data for approximately 20,000 households across multiple waves from 2004 to 2021.
key_finding: Windfall housing wealth transfers are likely to accelerate homeownership among middle-wealth recipients by relaxing down-payment constraints, but may crowd out diversified financial portfolio accumulation, with heterogeneous effects suggesting transfers reinforce rather than compress long-run wealth inequality.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work decomposing housing wealth accumulation into consumption versus investment motives or modeling optimal mortgage choice, this study identifies how exogenous intergenerational transfer shocks jointly reshape the consumption-investment balance and risk exposure in household portfolios, explicitly tracing wealth inequality dynamics across generations using linked administrative and survey data.
nn_title: Housing Costs and Bequest Motives
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Do mortgage lenders engage in algorithmic price discrimination by exploiting borrowers' digital footprints—such as device type, browsing behavior, or application timing—to infer willingness-to-pay and set individualized interest rates above competitive benchmarks?
method: Regression discontinuity and matched-pair design exploiting quasi-random variation in borrowers' observable digital characteristics (e.g., mobile vs. desktop application, time-of-day submission, browser type) linked to final loan pricing, using HMDA rate-spread data combined with proprietary loan-level origination data from a large aggregator to decompose residual pricing variation unexplained by credit risk, LTV, and borrower financials.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) enhanced data (2018–present, which includes rate spread, loan purpose, and channel identifiers) merged with loan-level origination files from a commercial mortgage data vendor such as Black Knight McDash or CoreLogic, which together cover tens of millions of originations with application metadata and final pricing terms.
key_finding: Borrowers applying via mobile devices or during off-peak hours—proxies for lower search intensity—are plausibly charged 10–25 basis points more conditional on identical credit risk profiles, implying that algorithmic screening of behavioral signals generates lender rents and represents a new, technology-enabled dimension of mortgage market inefficiency distinct from traditional demographic discrimination.
novelty_rationale: While prior work examines FinTech speed and borrower sophistication in rate-setting, no study has tested whether lenders exploit real-time digital behavioral signals—accessible through application metadata—as a mechanism for price discrimination, representing a fundamentally new channel of market inefficiency enabled by platform-based mortgage origination.
nn_title: Shopping around: Empirical analysis of borrower comparison shopping and mortgage
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: How does local banking market concentration shape the geographic pass-through of Federal Reserve monetary policy to mortgage rates, and does this transmission heterogeneity systematically disadvantage rural and minority-majority counties?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting plausibly exogenous variation in county-level Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) from historical bank merger waves as an instrument for market concentration, interacted with Federal Funds Rate change episodes, to estimate heterogeneous monetary policy pass-through to 30-year conforming mortgage rates across counties.
data: Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey and HMDA loan-level originations data (1990–2023) merged with FDIC Summary of Deposits for county-level HHI construction, covering all U.S. counties, supplemented by Census demographic data for heterogeneity analysis.
key_finding: Counties in the top quartile of banking concentration are likely to exhibit 30–50 basis point lower pass-through of Fed rate cuts to mortgage rates relative to competitive markets, with the attenuation effect amplified in rural and majority-minority counties, implying that monetary policy easing generates systematically smaller household balance-sheet relief in already-disadvantaged credit markets.
novelty_rationale: While prior work documents regional mortgage rate dispersion or spatial default heterogeneity, no study has used banking market structure as a mechanism to explain why monetary policy transmission to retail mortgage markets varies so sharply across geography, linking industrial-organization theory of bank conduct to regional credit inequality in a unified empirical framework.
nn_title: Regional Redistribution through the US Mortgage Market
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: How does the local market concentration of mortgage lending institutions affect loan pricing, denial rates, and terms offered to borrowers across different demographic and income groups?
method: Instrumental variable estimation exploiting historical bank branch network expansions and state-level deregulation events as quasi-exogenous shocks to local lender concentration, using a Herfindahl-Hirschman Index constructed from institution-level origination data to identify causal effects on mortgage outcomes.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) data (1990–2023) providing loan-level origination records with lender identity, borrower demographics, loan terms, and denial decisions, merged with FDIC Summary of Deposits data for branch-level market share and Federal Reserve Flow of Funds data for institutional characteristics.
key_finding: Higher local mortgage market concentration is plausibly associated with 15–40 basis point increases in mortgage spreads and 8–20 percentage point higher denial rates for minority and low-income applicants, with effects attenuated in markets where nonbank lenders hold substantial share.
novelty_rationale: While industrial organization studies examine bank concentration broadly, no existing work causally identifies how mortgage-specific lender concentration shapes the joint distribution of price, access, and demographic disparities in lending, distinguishing between depository and nonbank lender competitive dynamics.
nn_title: Lending competition, regulation, and nontraditional mortgages
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: How do climate-related physical risks—specifically flood zone reclassifications by FEMA—propagate into agency MBS pricing through pool-level geographic concentration, and do standard prepayment and default models systematically misprice securities with elevated climate exposure?
method: A two-stage empirical approach: first, construct pool-level climate risk exposure scores by merging loan-level geographic data with FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer reclassification events, then estimate a reduced-form MBS pricing model using option-adjusted spreads as the dependent variable to identify the incremental pricing impact of climate risk beyond conventional prepayment and credit factors, augmented by an out-of-sample mispricing test comparing model-implied versus observed spreads before and after major reclassification events.
data: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan-level performance datasets (publicly available, covering ~2013–present) merged with FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer historical reclassification records, eMBS or Bloomberg MBS pool-level OAS data (commercially available and used in published fixed-income research), and NOAA storm event records for identification of exogenous flood-risk shocks.
key_finding: Securities with pools concentrated in newly designated Special Flood Hazard Areas likely carry a 15–40 basis point OAS premium unaccounted for by standard prepayment models, suggesting that conventional contingent-claims frameworks systematically underprice climate transition risk and that reclassification events trigger measurable repricing discontinuities.
novelty_rationale: While prior MBS pricing research focuses on interest rate, prepayment, and credit risk, no published study has structurally embedded regulatory flood-zone reclassification as an exogenous, granular shock into pool-level OAS pricing models, offering a clean identification strategy for climate risk transmission into fixed-income valuations.
nn_title: Climate risk in mortgage markets: Evidence from Hurricanes Harvey and Irma
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does mandatory risk retention under the Dodd-Frank Act's qualified residential mortgage (QRM) rule alter the composition of mortgage-backed security pools in ways that shift unretained risks toward borrower-level quantity risk (prepayment, default volume uncertainty) rather than price risk (loss severity), and does this substitution affect MBS pricing efficiency?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered implementation of risk retention rules across loan categories and originator types, decomposing MBS spread variance into quantity-risk and price-risk components using a factor model calibrated to loan-level performance data before and after rule adoption.
data: Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae single-family loan-level performance datasets (1999–present), SEC EDGAR ABS-EE filings providing post-2015 private-label MBS loan-level disclosures, CoreLogic MBS Analytics for deal-level spread and tranche structure data, and HMDA for originator-level volume and borrower composition.
key_finding: Risk retention mandates likely induce originators to screen out price-risk-intensive loans (high LTV, low documentation) while retaining volume-sensitive exposures, producing MBS pools with lower loss severity but elevated prepayment volatility, with spreads that underprice residual quantity risk by an estimated 15–40 basis points relative to pre-rule benchmarks.
novelty_rationale: Existing risk-retention literature focuses on originator screening and skin-in-the-game incentives but has not mapped the regulatory shift onto the theoretical quantity-versus-price-risk distinction, leaving unexplored how mandated vertical retention structures systematically reallocate the composition—not just the level—of retained versus transferred risk in MBS pools.
nn_title: Informational Efficiency in Securitization after Dodd-Frank
assigned_label: Securitization, Risk Retention, and Intermediation

research_question: Does the racial composition of a neighborhood moderate the relationship between negative equity and mortgage default, and if so, how much of the Black-white mortgage default gap can be attributed to differential lender loss-mitigation responses (forbearance, loan modification) rather than borrower financial fundamentals?
method: A triple-difference hazard model exploiting zip-code-level racial composition, loan-level equity position (instrumented by local house-price declines), and temporal variation in HAMP/loss-mitigation eligibility rules to disentangle borrower strategic behavior from lender-side forbearance decisions in a competing-risks default-prepayment-modification framework.
data: Merged dataset combining Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac single-family loan-level performance files (2000–2022, publicly released), HMDA origination records for borrower race, CoreLogic automated valuation model estimates for contemporaneous LTV, and the National Mortgage Database (NMDB) accessible via FHFA research agreement.
key_finding: Conditional on identical equity shortfalls, Black borrowers in majority-minority zip codes are plausibly 15–30% less likely to receive proactive loss-mitigation outreach, implying that a substantial share of the observed racial default gap reflects asymmetric lender forbearance rather than differential ruthlessness or income volatility.
novelty_rationale: Prior default research treats lender forbearance as a uniform background condition or ignores it entirely; this design explicitly identifies forbearance as a racially heterogeneous treatment and separates it from borrower-side equity-trigger default, reframing the racial default gap as a market-conduct question rather than a borrower-risk question.
nn_title: Strategic Mortgage Default: The Effect of Neighborhood Factors
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: How does the introduction of shared appreciation mortgage (SAM) contracts affect borrower self-selection, default risk, and lender profitability relative to conventional fixed-rate mortgages across heterogeneous housing markets?
method: A structural model of mortgage contract choice that jointly estimates borrower risk type and equity-sharing preferences using revealed preference logic, combined with a difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered rollout of SAM pilot programs by U.S. credit unions and community banks to identify causal effects on default and prepayment hazards.
data: HMDA loan-level origination data linked to CoreLogic deed and performance records (default, prepayment, foreclosure) covering 2018–2024, supplemented by NCUA credit union call reports identifying SAM-offering institutions and FHFA house price indices at the ZIP-code level for measuring realized appreciation sharing outcomes.
key_finding: SAMs are expected to attract borrowers with high income uncertainty but optimistic house-price beliefs, producing adverse selection on default risk in appreciating markets but reducing default in stagnant markets where borrowers value the downside insurance, yielding an overall lender return distribution with fatter tails than conventional portfolios.
novelty_rationale: Unlike prior work on adverse selection in points-based contracts or deferred-amortization products, this paper introduces equity-contingent payoff structures as the screening instrument and empirically identifies selection and moral hazard effects separately by exploiting real-world SAM adoption variation, a dimension entirely absent from the existing mortgage contract design literature.
nn_title: Prepayment Penalty as a Screening Mechanism for Default and Prepayment Risks
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does carbon transition risk — exposure to decarbonization policies — create a systematic pricing factor in commercial real estate returns, distinct from physical climate risk?
method: Cross-sectional asset pricing tests using a carbon transition risk factor constructed from REIT portfolio carbon intensity (scope 1-3 emissions weighted by property holdings), following the Global Pricing of Carbon-Transition Risk methodology applied to REIT cross-sections and private CRE funds.
data: MSCI/IPD property-level returns matched to EPA energy use intensity data and ENERGY STAR scores for U.S. commercial properties (2010–2023), supplemented by REIT 10-K carbon disclosures from CDP and GRESB.
key_finding: Carbon-intensive commercial properties likely earn a risk premium of 50–150 basis points annually, with the premium strengthening after major climate policy announcements, consistent with investor compensation for stranded-asset exposure.
novelty_rationale: Existing CRE climate research focuses on physical flood or hurricane risk; this paper is the first to isolate transition risk as a priced factor in both listed and unlisted CRE using the carbon-intensity methodology pioneered in equity markets.
nn_title: Climate Change and Long-Run Discount Rates: Evidence from Real Estate
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do abnormally warm local temperatures causally increase households' stated willingness to pay for energy-efficient homes, as revealed through hedonic price responses around temperature anomaly events?
method: Regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences design exploiting county-level temperature anomaly weeks (analogous to the 'Attention to Global Warming' approach) matched to Zillow/CoreLogic transaction data, with green certification as the treated attribute.
data: CoreLogic transaction records merged with NOAA daily temperature anomaly data and ENERGY STAR/LEED certification dates for U.S. residential properties, 2005–2022, covering approximately 40 million transactions.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation positive temperature anomaly in the month of listing is likely to raise the green certification premium by 0.5–1.5 percentage points, with larger effects in politically moderate counties and dissipation within 60–90 days.
novelty_rationale: Prior green premium studies treat demand as static; this paper is the first to use salient weather shocks as instruments for time-varying climate salience, bridging the attention-to-global-warming literature with hedonic real estate analysis.
nn_title: Does Climate Change Affect Real Estate Prices? Only If You Believe In It
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: How do life-cycle housing market cycles — the serial correlation of house prices — alter optimal mortgage type choice, leverage, and stock market participation across the household life cycle?
method: Structural life-cycle portfolio choice model incorporating serially correlated house price returns (calibrated from MSA-level Case-Shiller data), extending the Optimal Life Cycle Portfolio Choice with Housing Market Cycles framework to allow joint choice over mortgage type (ARM vs. FRM), LTV, and equity share.
data: Survey of Consumer Finances (2001–2022) for calibration of preference parameters, FHFA MSA-level house price indices for cycle estimation, and American Housing Survey for tenure and mortgage type distributions.
key_finding: The model likely predicts that households in high-autocorrelation markets hold lower equity shares and choose ARMs more frequently, generating a speculative motive for leverage that amplifies housing cycles and crowds out retirement savings by 10–20%.
novelty_rationale: Existing mortgage choice models assume i.i.d. house price shocks; incorporating persistent cycles as in the life-cycle finance literature produces qualitatively new predictions about the ARM-FRM choice and stock market participation that are testable in cross-MSA data.
nn_title: Optimal Life Cycle Portfolio Choice with Housing Market Cycles
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Does a field experiment varying information about long-run house price forecasts affect homeowner renovation spending and home equity extraction decisions, paralleling evidence that home price expectations shift spending among renters?
method: Pre-registered online field experiment randomizing participants to receive either optimistic, neutral, or pessimistic 5-year house price forecast information from a professional source (following the Home Price Expectations and Spending experimental design), with outcomes measured via linked credit bureau records.
data: Experiment recruited from a U.S. online panel (n ≈ 5,000 homeowners), linked via consent to Equifax or TransUnion credit files to measure subsequent home equity loan originations, credit card balances, and renovation contractor payments over 12 months.
key_finding: Homeowners receiving pessimistic price forecasts likely reduce home equity borrowing by 8–15% but increase precautionary saving, consistent with wealth effects dominating for homeowners; the spending elasticity to forecasts is expected to be near zero for primary-residence owners.
novelty_rationale: The existing housing wealth effect literature is entirely observational; this paper is the first pre-registered field experiment linking exogenous house price expectation variation to objectively measured borrowing and renovation behavior using administrative credit data.
nn_title: Home Price Expectations and Spending: Evidence from a Field Experiment
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Do institutional investors in REITs shift their portfolio allocations away from carbon-intensive property types in response to climate policy uncertainty, analogous to retail investors selling carbon-intensive stocks after warm temperature anomalies?
method: Panel regression analysis of quarterly 13-F institutional ownership data for equity REITs, regressing changes in ownership on lagged carbon-intensity quintile interacted with climate policy uncertainty (EPU climate index), exploiting the variation in investor headquarters location and local temperature anomalies as instruments.
data: SEC 13-F filings (2010–2023) merged with GRESB/EPA energy intensity scores by REIT property type, Baker-Bloom-Davis Climate Policy Uncertainty Index, and NOAA county-level temperature anomalies at institutional investor headquarters.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in climate policy uncertainty likely reduces institutional ownership of the highest-carbon-intensity REITs by 1–3 percentage points, with effects concentrated among ESG-mandated funds and pension investors subject to ERISA scrutiny.
novelty_rationale: Prior REIT ownership studies focus on size and liquidity preferences; this paper is the first to connect climate salience shocks to institutional portfolio rebalancing across the carbon-intensity spectrum of property types.
nn_title: Doing good and doing well: The relationships between ESG and stock returns of RE
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Can option markets on REIT equities be used to extract market-implied tail risk premiums for physical climate hazards — specifically flood and hurricane exposure — in underlying property portfolios?
method: Option market analysis estimating the cost of downside put protection across REITs with varying property-portfolio flood risk exposure (FEMA NFIP map coverage), following the Carbon Tail Risk methodology adapted to climate physical risk, with event windows around major hurricane landfall announcements.
data: OptionMetrics daily options data for all publicly traded equity REITs (2000–2023) matched to MSCI Real Estate property-level location data and FEMA flood zone classifications, with hurricane event dates from NOAA HURDAT2.
key_finding: REITs with above-median coastal flood exposure likely face 15–40 basis points higher annualized put option costs than low-exposure REITs, with premia spiking by 30–60% in the 10-day window around major hurricane landfall, revealing that option markets efficiently price physical climate tail risk.
novelty_rationale: REIT climate risk research uses stock returns or NOI; applying option-market tail-risk methodology to extract the market's implicit climate risk premium from the options surface for property portfolios is entirely novel.
nn_title: Climate change and commercial real estate: Evidence from Hurricane Sandy
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: How does the dynamic life-cycle interaction between homeownership, housing leverage, and stock market participation change when house prices are predictable, and do households with high price-growth expectations optimally reduce equity holdings?
method: Dynamic stochastic life-cycle optimization model with time-varying expected house price growth (estimated via a VAR with price-to-rent ratio and vacancy rates), calibrated to PSID and SCF microdata, following the Optimal Portfolio Choice with Predictability in House Prices and Transaction Costs framework but incorporating realistic mortgage contract choice.
data: Panel Study of Income Dynamics (1999–2021) for household-level tenure, wealth, and portfolio data; FHFA MSA price indices for house price predictability estimation; Federal Reserve Flow of Funds for aggregate calibration targets.
key_finding: The model likely predicts that a one-standard-deviation increase in expected price growth raises the optimal housing expenditure share by 4–8 percentage points and reduces stock market participation by 5–12 percentage points, with the substitution effect strongest for middle-wealth households.
novelty_rationale: Existing life-cycle housing models assume random-walk prices; introducing MSA-level predictability creates novel crowd-out predictions between housing speculation and financial portfolio diversification that are testable against PSID wealth transitions.
nn_title: Optimal Portfolio Choice with Predictability in House Prices and Transaction Cos
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Does regional redistribution occur through uniform national conforming loan limit pricing by the GSEs, and what are the welfare implications of risk-adjusted regional pricing relative to the current uniform pricing regime?
method: Spatial equilibrium model of collateralized mortgage borrowing calibrated to MSA-level default risk, house price volatility, and income distributions, following the Regional Redistribution through the US Mortgage Market methodology, to quantify cross-subsidies from low-risk to high-risk MSAs under uniform guarantee fee pricing.
data: FHFA loan-level mortgage performance data (2000–2022), Freddie Mac Single Family Loan-Level dataset, MSA-level FHFA house price indices, and Census Bureau ACS income data for all U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Uniform GSE guarantee fees likely generate annual cross-subsidies of $2,000–$6,000 per household flowing from low-volatility Midwestern MSAs to high-volatility coastal metros; risk-adjusted pricing would improve aggregate welfare but concentrate homeownership declines in already-unaffordable markets.
novelty_rationale: While jumbo-conforming spread research estimates average GSE subsidies, no paper uses a spatial equilibrium framework to quantify the full regional redistribution map under uniform pricing, which has direct policy relevance for GSE reform debates.
nn_title: Regional Redistribution through the US Mortgage Market
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: Do housing voucher recipients reduce labor supply and increase public assistance take-up when vouchers substantially reduce effective housing costs, and does this response vary by local labor market tightness?
method: Instrumental variables analysis using voucher lottery outcomes from HUD's Housing Choice Voucher program administrative data as a randomization device, following the Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply design, extended to measure heterogeneous treatment effects by local unemployment rate at time of voucher receipt.
data: HUD Public Use Microdata on voucher lotteries in multiple PHAs (2005–2018), linked via Social Security Administration earnings records to quarterly employment and earnings outcomes and state SNAP/TANF administrative data.
key_finding: Voucher receipt likely reduces quarterly earnings by $250–$500 and employment probability by 3–5 percentage points on average, with the labor supply reduction approximately 50% larger in tight labor markets where housing cost-to-income ratios are highest, suggesting affordability stress rather than preference drives the labor supply response.
novelty_rationale: Prior housing assistance labor supply papers use single-city lotteries; a multi-PHA pooled analysis with heterogeneous treatment by local labor market conditions is methodologically novel and directly informs federal voucher expansion debates.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: Do children displaced from demolished public housing projects experience long-run improvements in employment, earnings, and criminal justice outcomes analogous to the Moved to Opportunity findings, and are these effects heterogeneous by destination neighborhood quality?
method: Quasi-experimental comparison of young adult outcomes between children displaced by HOPE VI public housing demolitions and non-displaced peers in the same housing projects, exploiting staggered demolition timing and distance to alternative public housing as instruments, following the Moved to Opportunity design.
data: HUD HOPE VI demolition records matched to IRS individual tax records (via Census Bureau) for earnings and employment, and FBI/state criminal justice records for arrest outcomes, covering demolitions in 50 cities from 1993–2010 with 20-year follow-up.
key_finding: Children displaced before age 13 likely earn 8–15% more annually by age 30 and have 20–35% lower violent arrest rates than non-displaced peers, with gains concentrated among children relocated to neighborhoods with above-median school quality, consistent with neighborhood effects dominating peer composition effects.
novelty_rationale: The original MTO was a controlled randomization; using HOPE VI's staggered quasi-experiment covers far larger geographic and demographic variation, allows estimation of dose-response curves with respect to destination quality, and tests external validity of the MTO findings at national scale.
nn_title: Moved to Opportunity: The Long-Run Effects of Public Housing Demolition on Child
assigned_label: School quality capitalization into housing prices

research_question: Does targeting in-kind housing transfers through market design — allowing LIHTC tenants choice over unit characteristics versus administrative assignment — improve tenant welfare while maintaining program targeting efficiency?
method: Revealed preference analysis of LIHTC tenant unit choice data using a preference model estimated from waitlist and placement records, following the Targeting In-Kind Transfers through Market Design methodology, to quantify welfare losses from administrative assignment relative to constrained choice mechanisms.
data: HUD LIHTC property-level data merged with tenant-level data from state housing finance agency administrative records in states with waitlist data (California, Texas, New York), covering approximately 500,000 tenant placements from 2005–2020.
key_finding: Administrative assignment likely reduces tenant welfare equivalent to a 15–30% rent subsidy reduction relative to constrained choice, with the efficiency-targeting tradeoff particularly acute for households with children whose school-quality preferences diverge from administrative placement algorithms.
novelty_rationale: LIHTC research focuses on rent-burden and neighborhood effects; applying mechanism design methodology from public economics to housing program allocation reveals the welfare cost of ignoring tenant preferences, a first in the affordable housing literature.
nn_title: Targeting In-Kind Transfers through Market Design: A Revealed Preference Analysi
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: How does climate change exposure — measured by extreme heat degree days — causally affect residential construction productivity, housing supply elasticity, and ultimately house prices in heat-exposed metropolitan areas?
method: Instrumental variables panel regression using county-level random variation in annual heat exposure (degree days above 90°F relative to historical baseline) as an instrument for residential construction employment and permit issuance, following the Economic Impacts of Climate Change on agricultural output design adapted to construction labor.
