key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in future self-continuity is associated with 3–5 percentage point lower LTV at origination and a 20–30% reduction in 90-day delinquency over five years, with effects concentrated among low-income borrowers where present-bias is theoretically most binding.
novelty_rationale: Future self-continuity predicts retirement savings in representative samples; transplanting this construct to mortgage leverage and homeownership sustainability identifies a psychological mechanism for the well-documented but poorly explained income-conditional default puzzle.
nn_title: The drivers of sustainable homeownership
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do real estate agents exhibit a 'superstar halo effect'—rating the same property more favorably and pricing it higher after being told it is listed by a high-reputation agent—and does this bias produce systematic mispricing in thin markets?
method: Controlled experiment where licensed agents are shown identical property photographs and characteristics and are randomly told the listing agent is either a top-decile producer or an average producer, measuring their recommended list price and willingness-to-show ratings, then comparing to actual transaction outcomes for the same properties.
data: Experiment recruiting 500 agents from a national brokerage network (e.g., Keller Williams or RE/MAX), using anonymized property scenarios from past MLS transactions in their metro area as stimuli, with actual outcomes known to the researchers but not participants.
key_finding: High-reputation agent attribution increases recommended list prices by 2.1–3.8% and willingness-to-show ratings by 0.4 standard deviations for identical properties, with the bias larger in low-liquidity rural markets; this implies that reputation-based halo effects artificially inflate sold prices for top-agent listings by 1–2% above fundamental value.
novelty_rationale: The superstar perception effect is established in sports contexts; applying it to real estate agents tests whether reputation creates self-fulfilling price premiums independent of actual service quality, with implications for agent compensation and market efficiency.
nn_title: Asymmetric information and personal affiliations in brokered housing transaction
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do housing investors who frame a neighborhood's recent price trajectory as a gain (prices recovered) versus a loss (prices still below peak) exhibit systematically different acquisition prices for identical properties, consistent with contextual loss framing increasing overbidding?
method: Exploit the geographic and temporal variation in whether a zip code has returned above its 2006 price peak by 2013 to create a natural gain/loss frame, and test whether investor purchases (identified by LLC/trust buyer names in deed records) show higher price premiums in loss-frame (not-yet-recovered) zip codes than in economically matched gain-frame (already-recovered) zip codes.
data: CoreLogic or ATTOM deed transaction records with buyer entity type linked to Zillow ZHVI zip-code-level price history, covering investor purchases in 2012–2015, matched within metro areas on median income, density, and pre-2006 price trajectory.
key_finding: Investor purchases in loss-frame zip codes (not yet recovered to 2006 peak) carry a 3–6% price premium over observably identical transactions in gain-frame zip codes, with the premium concentrated among out-of-state investors who lack local market context, consistent with contextual loss framing intensifying anchoring to the prior peak.
novelty_rationale: Loss framing effects on risk-taking are well-established in psychology; applying the gain/loss frame defined by whether a housing market has 'recovered its losses' to investor overbidding provides a novel channel for the well-documented investor heterogeneity in acquisition prices.
nn_title: Reference Points Spillovers: Micro-Level Evidence from Real Estate
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Can a news-media based real estate anxiety index constructed from semantic analysis of local newspaper content predict neighborhood-level house price volatility above standard economic fundamentals, and does this effect vary with housing supply elasticity?
method: Construct a zip-code-level housing anxiety index from ProQuest or NewsBank newspaper archives using semantic analysis (counting negative affect terms co-occurring with housing terms), and regress subsequent 12-month house price volatility on the index controlling for macro fundamentals, testing interaction with Saiz supply elasticity.
data: ProQuest Historical Newspapers or NewsBank local newspaper archives for 200 US metros (2000–2020), linked to Zillow ZHVI zip-code price volatility, FHFA conforming mortgage origination data, and Saiz (2010) supply elasticity estimates.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in the local housing anxiety index predicts a 0.8–1.4 percentage point increase in subsequent annual price volatility, with the effect twice as large in supply-inelastic markets where demand uncertainty cannot be resolved by new construction, and the index significantly outpredicts the national REU measure for local market forecasting.
novelty_rationale: Existing real estate uncertainty measures are national or use model-based approaches; applying local newspaper semantic analysis from the news-based uncertainty literature creates a geographically granular, text-derived measure that captures market-specific anxiety independent of aggregate economic conditions.
nn_title: Quantifying Sentiment with News Media across Local Housing Markets
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Does the framing of mortgage prepayment penalties as a 'fee to exit' (loss frame) versus a 'reward for staying' (gain frame) affect borrowers' refinancing sensitivity to interest rate incentives, and does this asymmetry explain part of the documented failure-to-refinance puzzle?
method: Randomized experiment where mortgage borrowers are shown identical prepayment penalty information in either loss-frame ('you will pay $X to leave') or gain-frame ('you currently hold $X in contract value') framing, measuring willingness to refinance at various interest rate spreads, calibrated to real-market break-even thresholds.
data: Online experiment with 3,000 current mortgage holders recruited via Prolific, using scenarios calibrated to actual prepayment penalty structures from Black Knight/CoreLogic loan-level data on FHA and HELOC loans; survey linked to stated current mortgage balance and rate for external validity.
key_finding: Loss-framed penalty presentation reduces willingness to refinance at economically rational thresholds by 15–22 percentage points, with effects concentrated among households with moderate financial literacy, suggesting that approximately 25–35% of the failure-to-refinance gap documented in prior literature could be explained by loss aversion toward prepayment penalty framing.
novelty_rationale: Failure-to-refinance studies focus on transaction costs and inertia; this is the first design to isolate whether the loss-frame presentation of penalties—rather than their monetary magnitude—independently discourages rational refinancing, providing a testable, low-cost policy fix.
nn_title: Do Borrowers Make Rational Choices on Points and Refinancing?
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do homeowners who delegate the listing price decision entirely to their agent exhibit higher sale prices and lower time on market than those who retain control, and does this delegation benefit vary with agent accountability mechanisms (referral vs. cold-contact relationships)?
method: Exploit variation in the degree of price-setting delegation documented in agent-client contracts and email communications (obtained from a brokerage partner), comparing outcomes for properties where listing prices closely track agent initial recommendations versus those where sellers override recommendations, with referral status as a moderating variable.
data: Brokerage-level transaction records from a large national franchise including initial agent price recommendation, final list price, and whether the client was a referral or cold contact, linked to MLS outcomes for 50,000+ listings, covering 2018–2023.
key_finding: Full delegation to the agent (list price within 1% of agent recommendation) is associated with 1.8–3.2% higher sale prices and 10–18 fewer days on market, but this benefit is larger for referral clients (where agent accountability is high) and absent for cold-contact clients, consistent with the delegation-cost hypothesis operating through accountability rather than information.
novelty_rationale: The downside-of-delegation literature from organizational behavior identifies interpersonal costs of delegation; in real estate, the direction reverses—delegation improves outcomes—but the boundary condition of accountability through referral networks has never been tested, connecting to the broader agent-incentive literature.
nn_title: Who Is “Misleading” Whom in Real Estate Transactions?
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do renters who are shown energy efficiency information in attribute-by-attribute format (comparing each building's insulation, HVAC, and appliance ratings separately) place greater weight on energy costs than those shown option-by-option summaries, and does this evaluation-mode effect affect green premium willingness to pay?
method: Randomized experiment where apartment seekers viewing listings on a rental platform receive either attribute-by-attribute energy feature comparisons or a single aggregated energy cost estimate, measuring click-through rates on green-certified listings and stated willingness to pay premiums for energy labels.
data: Platform experiment conducted through CoStar/Apartments.com or Zillow Rental Manager, randomizing listing display format for LEED/Energy Star certified units in 10 major metros, with approximately 50,000 listing views per condition over 6 months, linked to application submission rates.
key_finding: Attribute-by-attribute display increases click-through rates on green-certified units by 18–28% and stated willingness to pay for LEED certification by $45–$90 per month relative to the aggregated format, with the evaluability advantage larger for technical energy attributes (insulation R-value) and absent for easily comparable attributes (monthly utility cost estimate).
novelty_rationale: The evaluability-mode effect on food choice is well-documented; applying it to energy-efficiency information in rental listings tests whether the documented failure of green premiums to capitalize fully in rental markets is partly a presentation problem rather than a preference problem.
nn_title: Green Noise or Green Value? Measuring the Effects of Environmental Certification
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do institutional real estate investors who concentrate their portfolios geographically (local information advantage) exhibit more extreme risk-taking after experiencing peer portfolio losses, consistent with loss-chasing behavior amplifying market cycles?
method: Construct a quarter-by-quarter measure of unrealized losses for each institutional investor's local market portfolio using NCREIF property-level returns, and test whether larger unrealized losses predict subsequent increases in leverage, shorter holding periods, or higher cap rate compression in the same markets, compared to investors with geographically diversified portfolios.
data: NCREIF Property Database (NPD) with investor identity codes linked to PREA/NCREIF investor characteristics, covering institutional real estate fund transactions 1995–2022, with investor-level leverage from NCREIF ODCE fund data.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in an institutional investor's unrealized local-market loss predicts a 0.8–1.4 percentage point increase in subsequent quarter leverage and a 15–25 basis point cap rate compression in the same markets, with the effect absent for geographically diversified investors, consistent with loss-chasing amplifying local market cycles.
novelty_rationale: Loss-chasing is well-documented for individual investors and gamblers; identifying it among institutional real estate investors—who are typically assumed to be rational—provides a behavioral mechanism for the institutional capital flow pro-cyclicality documented in the REIT literature.
nn_title: There is no place like home: Information asymmetries, local asset concentration,
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do homebuyers who cognitively represent their neighborhood in terms of simple gist categories ('good school district,' 'safe') rather than specific attribute bundles pay price premiums that cannot be explained by hedonic factors, and does this categorical thinking intensify in hot markets?
method: Administer a novel Housing Gist Representation Scale measuring buyers' tendency to categorize neighborhoods versus enumerate specific attributes, link scale scores to transaction price residuals from a rich hedonic model, and test whether buyers with stronger gist representation pay larger unexplained premiums, with hot market conditions as a moderator.
data: Survey instrument administered to recent buyers (within 6 months) through a real estate platform or title company, linked to their purchase transaction addresses and hedonic price residuals from a local AVM model (CoreLogic or Zillow), covering 2,000 buyers in 5 markets (2021–2023).
key_finding: Buyers in the top quartile of the Housing Gist Scale pay price residuals 2.5–4.2% above matched buyers in the bottom quartile after controlling for income, education, and financial literacy, with the gist premium doubling in zip codes with above-median price appreciation, suggesting that categorical neighborhood thinking intensifies overbidding in competitive markets.
novelty_rationale: Gist-based representation has been studied in health risk and financial numeracy but never applied to housing location valuation; developing a real-estate-specific gist scale identifies a novel cognitive mechanism for the well-documented unexplained variance in hedonic residuals across buyer types.
nn_title: An Empirical Examination of Traditional Neighborhood Development
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers who receive a single 'best offer' recommendation from a comparison website accept worse terms than those who see a ranked list of options, because the zero-search-cost default triggers a 'more-is-better' acceptance heuristic?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting a platform policy change on a major U.K. mortgage comparison website that switched from ranked lists to single recommended offers, comparing accepted interest-rate spreads and LTV choices before and after the change.
data: U.K. FCA product sales database (PSD) containing origination-level mortgage terms, combined with timing of the comparison-site interface change documented in regulatory filings, covering approximately 2015–2022.
key_finding: Borrowers exposed to single-recommendation interfaces are plausibly predicted to accept spreads 8–18 basis points wider and LTV ratios 3–6 percentage points higher than comparable borrowers who see ranked alternatives.
novelty_rationale: Transfers the 'more-is-better anticommons heuristic' from experimental bargaining games to a real-world mortgage origination setting using a natural policy experiment rather than a lab study.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does windfall housing wealth — specifically, appreciation accruing to homeowners who purchased just before an unanticipated zoning upzoning — increase charitable giving and prosocial financial behavior relative to homeowners who purchased just after?
method: Regression discontinuity comparing IRS charitable deduction claims and consumer credit behaviour (from credit bureau panel data) among homeowners whose purchase dates straddle announced upzoning events in U.S. cities.
data: CoreLogic deed records merged with IRS Statistics of Income zip-code charitable deduction data and TransUnion or Equifax consumer credit panel, covering U.S. upzonings documented in the National Zoning Atlas (2010–2022).
key_finding: Homeowners who received windfall appreciation through upzoning plausibly increase charitable giving by 15–30% and reduce credit card balances by 5–10%, consistent with the 'windfall income enhances donation' laboratory finding.
novelty_rationale: Applies the windfall-income prosocial spending mechanism — established in laboratory dictator games — to a causal real-world setting using a quasi-experimental identification strategy unique to real estate.
nn_title: What Is a Tree Worth? Green‐City Strategies, Signaling and Housing Prices
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Does the sudden public moralization of landlord roles during the COVID-19 eviction moratorium era — when landlords were reframed as social villains or heroes — alter their subsequent rent-setting and eviction-filing behavior?
method: Difference-in-differences using variation in state-level media tone (measured via natural language processing of local newspaper archives) during 2020–2021 moratoria, comparing eviction filing rates and asking rents in high- versus low-moralization media environments after moratoria lifted.
data: Princeton Eviction Lab eviction records, Zillow asking-rent panel at the ZIP-code level, and Media Cloud newspaper corpus for tone coding, all publicly available for 2018–2023.
key_finding: Landlords in high-moralization media environments plausibly file 10–20% fewer evictions post-moratorium and raise rents 3–7% less aggressively, or conversely show compensatory backlash, consistent with contested outcomes in gig-worker moralization literature.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the 'heroes from above but not from within' gig-worker moralization framework to the landlord context, using NLP-coded media salience as the treatment variable in a large observational panel.
nn_title: The rise of corporate landlords: An examination of behavioral differences in the
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do homebuyers who purchase properties with dynamic online listing formats — where comparable listings are revealed sequentially over the search period — converge on different price-quality trade-offs than buyers who see the full choice set at once?
method: Matched hedonic regression comparing purchase outcomes (price paid relative to AVM estimate, dwelling characteristics) between buyers whose MSA-level platform shifted from static to dynamic listing feeds, exploiting staggered roll-outs across Zillow/Redfin market launches.
data: Zillow Transaction and Assessment Dataset (ZTRAX) deed records merged with Zillow listing API archive and platform roll-out dates from Zillow's public investor presentations, covering 2012–2020.
key_finding: Buyers on dynamic platforms plausibly overpay by 1.5–3.5% relative to AVM benchmarks and select less objectively optimal dwellings, consistent with the laboratory finding that evolving choice sets distort revealed preferences.
novelty_rationale: Translates the 'evolving versus static choice sets' preference-distortion finding from laboratory consumer choice into a large-scale natural experiment in residential real estate search.
nn_title: To Sell or Not to Sell: Measuring the Heat of the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do real estate agents who occupy central positions in local professional referral networks charge systematically higher commissions and achieve higher sale prices, consistent with network centrality increasing social influence susceptibility in their clients?
method: Construction of referral network graphs from NAR agent co-listings in MLS data, computing eigenvector centrality scores for each agent, and regressing client-level outcomes (commission rate accepted, final sale price relative to list) on agent centrality controlling for agent and market fixed effects.
data: CoreLogic MLS transaction data identifying co-listing and co-representing agent pairs across 50 U.S. MSAs (2010–2020), combined with state license records to construct career-length network ties.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in agent network centrality is plausibly associated with 5–12 basis point higher effective commission rates and a 0.5–1.5% higher sale-to-list price ratio, mediated by client deference.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'embedding forces of network commitment' social influence framework to the real estate brokerage market, treating agent referral networks as an empirically measurable substitute for laboratory network manipulations.
nn_title: The Disciplining Effect of Concern for Referrals: Evidence from Real Estate Agen
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does receiving a favorable automated valuation model (AVM) estimate before listing a home cause sellers to set higher asking prices that persist even when the AVM is known to be imprecise, reflecting incidental confidence transfer across domains?
method: Regression discontinuity exploiting AVM estimate rounding thresholds (e.g., values just above versus just below round-number AVM outputs) to identify exogenous positive confidence shocks, comparing subsequent list prices and time-on-market.
data: Zillow Zestimate history merged with ZTRAX listing and transaction records, restricting to properties where Zestimate was publicly displayed at the time of listing, covering 2015–2022 in U.S. metros.
key_finding: Sellers whose AVM estimate just exceeds a round-number threshold plausibly set asking prices 0.8–2.2% higher and experience 5–12% longer time-on-market, consistent with incidental confidence inflating price aspirations in an unrelated valuation domain.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'incidental confidence transfer' behavioral mechanism to listing price formation, providing a causal identification strategy based on AVM rounding discontinuities rather than survey-based confidence measures.
nn_title: Listing Price, Time on Market, and Ultimate Selling Price: Causes and Effects of
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does a sense of responsibility toward family members — operationalized as the presence of dependents — make homebuyers more risk-averse in mortgage choice, selecting lower LTV mortgages even when financially capable of higher leverage?
method: Instrumental variables estimation using exogenous variation in household size (twin births as instrument) to identify the causal effect of dependent-induced responsibility on LTV choice and fixed-versus-adjustable-rate selection, conditional on income and wealth controls.
data: American Housing Survey (AHS) household panel with mortgage detail and household composition, merged with HMDA origination records for rate-type information, covering 2005–2021.
key_finding: An exogenous increase in dependents plausibly reduces LTV by 3–6 percentage points and increases fixed-rate selection probability by 8–14 percentage points, consistent with group-representative risk aversion under heightened responsibility.
novelty_rationale: Transfers the 'group representatives are more risk-averse' experimental finding into a causal household finance design using the twin-birth instrument to avoid selection into parenthood.
nn_title: Unobservable Risks in Mortgage Contract Choice
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do homeowners with greater future self-continuity — their sense of connection to their future selves — accumulate more home equity through voluntary extra mortgage payments and are less likely to extract equity through cash-out refinancing?
method: Cross-sectional IV regression using survey-based future self-continuity measures (from the Health and Retirement Study's time-preference questions as a proxy) linked to administrative mortgage servicing records, with state retirement savings laws as instruments.
data: Health and Retirement Study (HRS) merged with Black Knight McDash mortgage servicing data through the NBER-HRS link, covering homeowners aged 50–70 from 2010–2020.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in future self-continuity is plausibly associated with 15–25% lower probability of cash-out refinancing and $8,000–$18,000 more equity accumulation over a five-year period.
novelty_rationale: Extends the nationally representative future self-continuity and financial well-being finding specifically to the housing equity domain using an individual-level panel linkage not previously exploited in the literature.
nn_title: Housing leverage, home value, and retirement
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Does the urgency framing of housing inventory scarcity messages in real estate listing platforms — 'only 2 homes left in this neighborhood' versus a neutral display — increase buyer willingness to pay and accelerate purchase decisions in ways that raise prices?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting staggered introduction of scarcity-urgency badges across ZIP codes by a major listing platform (identified from web-scraping archives), comparing sale prices and time-to-contract in treated versus untreated ZIPs before and after badge introduction.
data: Wayback Machine web archives of Zillow/Redfin listing pages to identify badge roll-out timing, merged with ZTRAX transaction records for price and time-on-market outcomes, covering 2016–2022.
key_finding: Urgency-badge introduction plausibly increases sale prices by 1–3% and reduces time-to-contract by 8–15 days in treated ZIPs, consistent with urgency messaging increasing engagement and accelerating costly commitment.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'urgency and engagement' large-scale field intervention framework to the real estate platform context, where scarcity messaging is not randomly assigned but can be studied via staggered platform roll-outs.
nn_title: To Sell or Not to Sell: Measuring the Heat of the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do real estate agents who receive gender-concordant client inquiries — same-gender agent and buyer — respond more quickly and provide more information, reflecting homophily and identity signaling effects on service quality?
method: Correspondence experiment sending randomized email inquiries to listed agents in 20 U.S. cities, crossing client-signaled gender (via name choice) with agent gender (from license records), and measuring response rate, response latency, and information completeness.
data: State real estate license databases (public records) to identify agent gender, combined with active MLS listings for contact information; experimental emails to be sent and coded by research team.
key_finding: Gender-concordant pairs plausibly show 10–20% higher response rates and 15–25% longer informative responses, consistent with prominent identity-signal homophily effects reducing perceived intergroup threat.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the 'prominent identity signals in intergroup interactions' experimental design to real estate agent-client gender pairing, extending correspondence audit literature beyond race/ethnicity to gender-based service heterogeneity.
nn_title: Restricted access: Real estate agent response to client race, ethnicity, gender,
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does the number of competing offers disclosed to a seller shift their moral framing of negotiation from promise-keeping toward performance-or-pay, reducing remorse over rejecting lower but sincere bids?
method: Survey experiment with a nationally representative panel of recent home sellers, randomizing the salience of competing-offer disclosure in a vignette scenario and measuring moral obligation ratings, regret, and hypothetical willingness to accept lower offers.
data: Qualtrics or Lucid nationally representative panel of U.S. homeowners who sold within the past five years (N ≈ 2,500), with embedded randomized vignette manipulation and validated moral judgment scales.
key_finding: Sellers shown a multiple-offer frame plausibly rate rejection of lower bids as 8–15 scale points more permissible and report 20–35% lower anticipated regret, consistent with liquidated-damages-clause effects on moral perception of contract breach.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'liquidated damages shifts promise-to-pay perception' behavioral finding to seller moral psychology in competitive bidding contexts, using a survey experiment instead of mortgage vignettes.
nn_title: An Experimental Analysis of the Impact of Intermediaries on the Outcome of Barga
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do mixed-gender negotiating teams in commercial real estate transactions achieve higher joint surplus and fewer impasses than single-gender teams, mediated by reduced status conflict and enhanced psychological safety?
method: Analysis of MSCI/NCREIF transaction-level dataset merging deal records with LinkedIn-sourced deal-team gender composition data, using within-property-type variation in team gender diversity to predict price deviations from appraisal, time-to-close, and deal fall-through rates.
data: NCREIF transaction database merged with publicly available deal announcements from real estate news archives and LinkedIn profiles for deal-team composition, covering U.S. institutional transactions 2010–2022.
key_finding: Gender-diverse deal teams plausibly achieve prices 1–4% closer to independent appraisal estimates and show 15–25% lower deal fall-through rates, consistent with gender diversity reducing status conflict and improving integrative negotiation.
novelty_rationale: Transfers the evolutionary-psychology 'gender diversity moderates status conflict' framework to real estate deal-making, exploiting observable deal-team composition variation rather than laboratory manipulation.
nn_title: The Gender Gap in Real Estate Sales: Negotiation Skill or Agent Selection?
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do property tax assessment appeals produce systematically biased outcomes based on the sequencing of comparative evidence presented — with earlier-presented comparables exerting disproportionate anchoring influence on assessors?
method: Analysis of administrative appeal hearing records from a large U.S. county, coding the order in which comparable sales were introduced and regressing final assessment adjustments on the deviation of early versus late comparables from true market value, controlling for property characteristics.
data: Cook County (Illinois) Board of Review appeal records (publicly available FOIA data), merged with CoreLogic comparable sales data to reconstruct the comparables used in each hearing, covering 2010–2020.
key_finding: Comparables presented first in hearing sequences plausibly exert 2–3 times greater influence on final adjustments per dollar of deviation than later-presented comparables, consistent with memory retrieval primacy effects in political judgment.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'memory retrieval order explains incumbency advantage' framework to quasi-judicial assessment hearings, treating early versus late comparable presentation as a natural source of ordering variation.
nn_title: The Effects of Property Tax Protests on the Assessment Uniformity of Residential
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Does exposure to gradually rising sea-level risk disclosures over multiple years produce weaker housing price responses than sudden, salient one-time risk revelation events, consistent with gradual materialization reducing threat salience?
method: Event study design comparing hedonic price responses to FEMA flood map gradual revision processes (multi-year preliminary-to-final map updates) versus sudden, unanticipated map revisions in matched coastal communities, using DiD with coastal property transaction data.
data: FEMA FIRM map revision histories (public), CoreLogic or ZTRAX deed records for coastal U.S. properties, and NFIP flood insurance take-up rates (FEMA public data), covering 2000–2022.
key_finding: Gradual flood map revisions plausibly produce 40–60% smaller price adjustments than equivalent sudden revisions, consistent with the 'out of sight, out of mind' gradual public-bad materialization effect.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'gradually materializing public bad reduces behavioral response' laboratory finding to climate risk disclosure policy, providing causal evidence on whether gradual versus sudden risk revelation matters for housing markets.
nn_title: Going Underwater? Flood Risk Belief Heterogeneity and Coastal Home Price Dynamic
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do homebuyers who use peer referrals to find their real estate agent achieve better price outcomes than those who hire agents through digital search platforms, because peer-referral agents exert more effort under reputation-preservation incentives?
method: Instrumental variables regression using moving-related agent referrals (buyers who relocated from out-of-state and thus had no local referral network) as an instrument for digital-search agent selection, comparing sale prices and time-on-market conditional on property characteristics.
data: Redfin or Zillow transaction data with buyer origin-state information (available in deed records for some states), or NAR survey microdata linking buyer agent-finding method to transaction outcomes, covering 2010–2020.
key_finding: Referral-obtained agents plausibly achieve purchase prices 0.8–2.5% closer to AVM estimates and 10–20% faster closings than digitally-found agents, consistent with referral-based reputation discipline improving agent effort.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'referral aversion and third-party help exchange' social brokerage framework to agent selection, treating referral networks as reputation-enforcement mechanisms rather than mere information channels.
nn_title: The Disciplining Effect of Concern for Referrals: Evidence from Real Estate Agen
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the Big Five personality trait of conscientiousness predict timely mortgage payments and lower default rates beyond standard underwriting variables, and can personality screening improve credit risk prediction at origination?
method: Meta-regression of existing studies linking self-reported personality measures (from HRS, NLSY, BHPS) to mortgage delinquency outcomes, supplemented by original analysis using the Panel Study of Income Dynamics personality module merged with Black Knight mortgage performance data.
data: PSID 2015 and 2017 waves with Big Five personality module, merged with administrative mortgage performance records through the PSID-mortgage supplement, covering approximately 3,000 mortgaged homeowner households.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in conscientiousness plausibly predicts 15–30% lower 90-day delinquency probability, with effect sizes comparable to a 20–40 point FICO improvement after controlling for standard credit variables.
novelty_rationale: Applies the Big Five earnings meta-analysis framework specifically to mortgage credit risk, leveraging the PSID personality-mortgage data linkage to provide causal identification beyond cross-sectional association.
nn_title: Consumer Ruthlessness and Mortgage Default during the 2007 to 2009 Housing Bust
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do homeowners who receive framing-concordant tax benefit information — gains frame for itemizers, loss frame for those switching to standard deduction after TCJA — show greater compliance with tax-optimal mortgage refinancing decisions?
method: Randomized survey experiment with a representative panel of recent homeowners, varying whether the TCJA's mortgage interest deduction change is framed as a gain (standard deduction increase) or a loss (MID value reduction), then measuring stated refinancing intentions and actual refinancing rates in a six-month follow-up.
data: Qualtrics panel survey linked to public HMDA refinancing records for panel respondents (via ZIP-level matching), with IRS Statistics of Income data for actual refinancing take-up validation, covering 2020–2022.
key_finding: Loss-framed messaging about MID changes plausibly increases refinancing intention by 15–25 percentage points and actual refinancing rates by 5–10 percentage points relative to gain framing, consistent with contextual loss framing activating harm-avoidance behavior.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'contextual loss framing influences risk behavior in spatial decisions' experimental framework to household tax-mortgage optimization, connecting behavioral framing theory to a high-stakes real financial decision.
nn_title: Homeownership and taxes: How the TCJA altered the tax code's treatment of housin
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Does providing homebuyers with a personalized 'total cost of ownership' recommendation — a single aggregated figure covering mortgage, taxes, insurance and maintenance — reduce cognitive complexity and lead to better-calibrated house price offers than presenting individual cost components?
method: Randomized field experiment with a mortgage counseling nonprofit, randomly assigning clients to receive standard itemized cost disclosures versus aggregated total-cost-of-ownership summaries, then tracking offer prices, appraisal gaps, and six-month payment performance.
data: NeighborWorks America counseling agency administrative records from participating agencies in 10 cities, linked to HMDA origination records and credit bureau performance data through a research data agreement, covering new participants 2022–2025.
key_finding: Aggregated-cost clients plausibly make offers 2–5% closer to appraisal values and show 15–25% lower 90-day delinquency rates at six months, consistent with simplified plan sorting improving cost-effective selection in health insurance experiments.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the 'more intelligent choice architecture through plan sorting by total expected cost' health insurance RCT design to residential mortgage counseling, where analogous information complexity problems have not been experimentally tested.
nn_title: Paying Too Much? Borrower Sophistication and Overpayment in the U.S. Mortgage Ma
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Do real estate investors who hold larger portfolios make riskier individual asset acquisition decisions because responsibility for each asset is diffused across the portfolio, analogous to group responsibility diffusion?
method: Regression analysis of NCREIF property-level acquisition data, testing whether institutional investors with larger existing portfolio sizes pay higher premiums (relative to appraisal) and acquire properties with higher vacancy rates, using instrumental variables based on legacy portfolio size inherited through fund manager transitions.
data: NCREIF NPI property-level transaction and appraisal database merged with pension fund annual reports identifying fund manager changes, covering U.S. institutional acquisitions 1990–2020.
key_finding: A doubling of portfolio size plausibly increases acquisition price premiums by 3–7% and raises acquired property vacancy rates by 2–5 percentage points, consistent with diffused responsibility reducing individual asset scrutiny.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'diffusion of responsibility for actions with advice' framework to institutional real estate portfolio management, where portfolio scale creates a structural analog to group decision-making responsibility diffusion.
nn_title: Institutional common ownership and firm value: Evidence from real estate investm
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does requiring mortgage applicants to explicitly justify their income and asset declarations — as mandated under enhanced CFPB documentation rules — reduce loan-level misrepresentation and subsequent default compared to stated-income documentation?
method: Difference-in-differences using the staggered state-level implementation of Ability-to-Repay rules post-Dodd-Frank, comparing default rates between loans originated under enhanced documentation requirements versus legacy stated-income loans for the same lenders.
data: Black Knight McDash loan-level performance data merged with CFPB HMDA documentation-type indicators and state ATR implementation dates, covering originations 2010–2016 with performance through 2020.
key_finding: Enhanced documentation requirements plausibly reduce 24-month default rates by 20–35% for loans in the 90–95 LTV range, consistent with process accountability reducing misrepresentation even when justifications are not externally verified.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'justifying the judgment process reduces misreporting' process-accountability framework to regulatory mortgage documentation design, using the natural variation in ATR implementation timing as identification.
nn_title: The Failure and Promise of Mandated Consumer Mortgage Disclosures: Evidence from
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do real estate investment trust CEOs who exhibit overconfidence in domain-irrelevant private matters — as revealed by their personal real estate transaction behavior — subsequently pay higher premiums in REIT portfolio acquisitions?
method: Linking CEO personal property transaction records (from deed databases) to REIT acquisition CAR data, constructing a personal-real-estate overpayment proxy (personal purchase price relative to AVM), and testing whether high personal overpayment predicts larger REIT acquisition premiums using panel regressions with CEO fixed effects.
data: REIT CEO personal deed transaction records from CoreLogic matched to CRSP/Compustat REIT panel and SNL Real Estate acquisition announcement data, covering REIT CEOs of S&P 500 firms 2005–2022.
key_finding: CEOs whose personal home purchases exceed AVM estimates by one standard deviation plausibly pay REIT acquisition premiums 2–5% higher, consistent with incidental confidence transfer from the personal to the professional domain.
novelty_rationale: Operationalizes the 'incidental confidence influences self-interested behaviors across domains' framework using observable CEO personal real estate transactions as an out-of-domain confidence proxy for professional acquisition behavior.
nn_title: CEO Overconfidence, REIT Investment Activity and Performance
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the introduction of a dominant but inferior listing option on real estate platforms — such as a high-commission full-service tier that makes mid-tier discount brokerage look comparatively attractive — increase market share for discount brokers through asymmetric dominance?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered introduction of premium-tier listing packages by Realtor.com and Zillow across MSAs, comparing discount brokerage market share (measured from MLS co-broke fee data) before and after premium-tier introduction.
data: NAR co-broke fee data from MLS transaction records (available in research agreements), combined with listing platform fee structure changes documented in regulatory filings and news archives, covering 2015–2022.
key_finding: Premium-tier introduction plausibly increases discount broker market share by 3–8 percentage points in affected MSAs, consistent with decoy-effect attraction toward the mid-tier option when an inferior but similar high-tier option is present.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'attraction decoy effect shifts preferences toward target options' finding to brokerage market structure, treating platform pricing tiers as a natural decoy manipulation in a real-market setting.
nn_title: Discount Brokerage in Residential Real Estate Markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do renters who experience a sudden salient building failure event — such as a structural collapse or fire in their building — subsequently exhibit heightened domain-specific risk aversion in subsequent housing search, demanding larger rent discounts for properties in older buildings?
method: Hedonic analysis of rental listings and lease transactions in cities that experienced high-profile residential building failures (e.g., Champlain Towers South vicinity, Chicago high-rise fire), comparing search behavior and accepted rents for older versus newer buildings before and after the salient event.
data: Zillow rental listing and transaction data matched to building vintage data from county assessor records, covering rental markets in Miami-Dade, Chicago, and three comparable control cities 2018–2023.
key_finding: Post-failure rent discounts for buildings over 40 years old plausibly increase by 3–8% in affected cities relative to matched controls, consistent with visceral pain/fear experiences activating domain-specific risk aversion in subsequent housing decisions.
novelty_rationale: Applies the 'visceral factor of pain relates to domain-specific risk attitudes' framework to housing rental decisions, using real building failure events as a natural manipulation of domain-specific fear activation.
nn_title: Perception of risk and user cost in property markets: Evidence from Surfside par
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do housing market participants who are asked to make predictions immediately after observing a long streak of price increases systematically predict streak reversal (gambler's fallacy) while those given fragmented non-streak price histories predict continuation, and does this pattern distort actual transaction prices?
method: Regression analysis of repeat-sale price changes in ZIP codes with varying prior price streak characteristics (monotone appreciation versus volatile histories), testing whether the gap between list prices and transaction prices — a revealed seller confidence measure — varies with streak length using ZTRAX panel data.
data: ZTRAX repeat-sale transaction records merged with FHFA ZIP-code-level house price index histories, identifying ZIP codes with 4+ consecutive quarters of appreciation versus mixed histories, covering 2000–2022.
key_finding: Sellers in long-streak ZIPs plausibly set list prices 2–5% above model-predicted values relative to mixed-history ZIPs, and actual transaction prices exceed AVM benchmarks by 1–3% during streak periods but revert 2–4% after streak breaks.
novelty_rationale: Transfers the 'representativeness heuristic and gambler's fallacy in lottery choices' finding to real estate pricing, testing whether streak-based prior histories induce reversal expectations that are reflected in actual transactions.
nn_title: Do House Price Levels Anticipate Subsequent Price Changes within Metropolitan Ar
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the presence of a peer homeowner who visibly defaults (foreclosure filing visible on public records) in an immediate neighborhood block reduce the moral cost of default for nearby underwater homeowners, and does this effect operate through perceived social permission rather than information about collateral values?
method: Triple-difference design exploiting variation in foreclosure filing public disclosure timing across states (judicial versus non-judicial), comparing strategic default rates among otherwise-similar underwater neighbors of foreclosed properties in high- versus low-disclosure-speed states, controlling for local price declines.
data: Black Knight McDash loan performance data merged with ATTOM property foreclosure records and state-level judicial/non-judicial classification, covering underwater mortgages in 2009–2014, with ZIP-code and lender fixed effects.
key_finding: High-disclosure states plausibly show 25–40% larger neighbor strategic default contagion effects than low-disclosure states, consistent with visible foreclosure public records providing 'social permission' signals distinct from collateral value information channels.
novelty_rationale: Distinguishes the social permission mechanism from information channels in foreclosure contagion by exploiting variation in the visibility of peer defaults, applying the 'signaling selfish expectations boosts cooperation among would-be free riders' logic in reverse.
nn_title: Fear, Shame and Guilt: Economic and Behavioral Motivations for Strategic Default
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'more-is-better' heuristic when evaluating condominium floor levels, paying monotonically increasing premiums even when engineering and energy-cost evidence shows diminishing or reversing returns at very high floors?
method: Regression discontinuity design applied to transaction-level condominium data, testing for price discontinuities at architectural thresholds (e.g., floors above structural transition points) where objective quality plateaus but the heuristic predicts continued premiums.
data: Transaction-level condominium records from South Korea, Hong Kong, or Singapore (used in published studies on top-floor discounts and floor-level premiums) covering at least 10 years of sales across high-rise buildings of varying heights.
key_finding: Buyers likely pay a monotonically increasing floor premium that overshoots fundamental value above the 20th floor, with excess premiums of 2–5% consistent with a more-is-better heuristic rather than rational valuation of views or noise reduction.
novelty_rationale: Applies the anticommons 'more-is-better' heuristic literature to vertical real estate pricing, testing whether a specific cognitive bias—not anchoring or loss aversion—explains the well-documented but poorly explained floor-level gradient.
nn_title: Hedonic price and depreciation indexes for residential housing: A longitudinal a
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does requiring homeowners to verbally justify their self-assessed property value before receiving an automated valuation model estimate reduce anchoring on their prior self-assessment, improving the accuracy of subsequent value estimates?
method: Field experiment partnered with a mortgage lender or refinancing platform, randomly assigning applicants to a process-justification condition (explain valuation rationale before seeing AVM) versus control (see AVM first), measuring deviation of self-assessed value from subsequent arm's-length appraisal.
data: Mortgage refinancing application data combined with Home Equity Conversion Mortgage counseling records and HECM appraisal data (used in published HECM accuracy studies), supplemented by lender administrative data accessible via research partnership.
key_finding: Process justification likely reduces self-assessment overestimation by 3–6 percentage points relative to control, with larger effects for underwater homeowners who have the strongest incentive to anchor on favorable prior beliefs.
novelty_rationale: Translates the process-accountability-without-accuracy-improvement finding from JDM to real estate self-valuation, testing whether justification requirements decouple anchoring from accuracy in a high-stakes domain.
nn_title: Valuation Bias in Commercial Appraisal: A Transaction Price Feedback Experiment
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Do real estate agents exhibit a 'ratings pattern heuristic,' such that buyers discount agents whose past transactions show unusually high sale-to-list price ratios as implausibly skilled rather than genuinely expert?
method: Hedonic regression analysis with agent fixed effects using MLS transaction data, testing whether agents with objectively superior past performance metrics attract fewer future listings, interacting performance consistency with deviation from the average agent pattern.
data: MLS transaction records linked to agent identifiers covering multiple years in major U.S. markets (used in published agent expertise studies), supplemented by Zillow or Redfin agent review data for reputation measures.
key_finding: Agents with consistently above-average sale-to-list ratios likely attract 8–15% fewer future seller clients than agents with average but more typical performance patterns, consistent with the ratings-pattern credibility penalty.
novelty_rationale: Applies the credibility-pattern heuristic from JDM—where being right in unexpected ways reduces perceived expertise—to real estate brokerage, offering a novel explanation for why top-performing agents do not always capture proportional market share.
nn_title: The Disciplining Effect of Concern for Referrals: Evidence from Real Estate Agen
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the sequential order in which potential homebuyers retrieve neighborhood attributes from memory (safety first versus schools first) systematically predict which attribute receives the larger implicit price weight in their eventual purchase decision?
method: Online survey experiment with a representative sample of recent homebuyers, using an aspect-listing methodology where participants list neighborhood considerations in natural order, then linking order of retrieval to transaction prices paid for school quality versus crime rate attributes via hedonic regression.
data: Survey data collected from recent homebuyers combined with CoreLogic or Zillow transaction records geocoded to school district and crime rate data (standard in published school capitalization studies) across 10–15 major U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_finding: Buyers who spontaneously retrieve school quality first in memory likely pay a 4–8% larger school quality premium, while those retrieving safety first pay a larger crime discount, suggesting memory retrieval order causally shapes implicit prices.
novelty_rationale: Imports query theory and memory retrieval sequence methodology from political psychology into hedonic pricing, offering a cognitive mechanism for the large and variable cross-study dispersion in school and crime capitalization estimates.
nn_title: Simultaneity in the market for housing characteristics
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do homeowners with higher gist-reasoning ability—who represent house price trends categorically ('prices are rising') rather than verbatim ('prices rose 4.3%')—make better-calibrated refinancing decisions that reduce their failure-to-refinance rate?
method: Survey-based measurement of gist reasoning and numeracy administered to a sample of mortgage holders, linked via anonymized identifiers to administrative mortgage performance records, estimating hazard models of refinancing timing controlling for interest rate incentives.
data: Freddie Mac Single Family Loan-Level Dataset or HMDA data linked to a financial literacy survey instrument administered via the American Life Panel or FINRA Investor Education Foundation survey (both used in published financial literacy studies).
key_finding: High-gist-reasoning homeowners are likely 20–35% more likely to refinance within the optimal window even controlling for numeracy, suggesting categorical trend representation rather than precise numerical processing drives timely mortgage decisions.
novelty_rationale: Applies fuzzy-trace theory's distinction between gist and verbatim representations—well-established in medical and financial decisions—to mortgage refinancing, providing a new cognitive explanation for the failure-to-refinance puzzle.
nn_title: Sources of Inaction in Household Finance: Evidence from the Danish Mortgage Mark
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do sellers who receive a competing offer quickly after listing anchor on that offer's speed as a quality signal and accept worse final terms than sellers who receive their first offer after a longer search?
method: Analysis of MLS records with offer timing data, exploiting variation in days-to-first-offer across similar properties in the same submarket, instrumenting for offer speed using seasonal listing patterns, and estimating the effect on final sale-to-list price ratio.
data: MLS transaction records with days-on-market and listing price revision history for residential properties in markets where ShowingTime or similar platforms track showing requests (commercially available, used in published TOM studies).
key_finding: Sellers who receive their first offer within 48 hours likely accept prices 1.5–3% lower than sellers who wait 7–14 days for an equivalent offer, consistent with preference for quicker offers creating a temporal reference point that distorts negotiation.
novelty_rationale: Applies temporal reference point and evaluation mode theory from JDM to the seller-side of real estate negotiation, providing a behavioral explanation for why hot-market sellers sometimes underperform in final price despite strong demand.
nn_title: List Prices, Sale Prices and Marketing Time: An Application to U.S. Housing Mark
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'proportion dominance' bias in charitable or community-benefit disclosures attached to real estate listings—donating more to community foundations when framed as helping a small identifiable neighborhood group versus a large metropolitan housing fund?
method: Randomized field experiment embedded in a real estate platform or HOA community offering optional community contributions at closing, varying whether the recipient is described as a small local group versus a large regional fund, measuring opt-in rates and contribution amounts.
data: Transaction-level data from a real estate platform or title company that offers optional charitable contributions at closing, combined with deed records for property characteristics and neighborhood demographics from Census data.
key_finding: Buyers framed with a small identifiable neighborhood group likely contribute 40–70% more and opt in at 15–25 percentage points higher rates than those offered a large metropolitan fund, consistent with proportion dominance in charitable giving.
novelty_rationale: Imports the identifiable victim and proportion dominance literature from charitable giving into real estate closing decisions, with direct implications for how affordable housing contributions and community benefit agreements should be framed.
nn_title: Does the number of houses a broker shows depend on a homeseeker's race?
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does the provision of energy efficiency information in a 1-in-X format (e.g., '1 in 8 homes at this efficiency level exceeds heating costs of $3,000') versus an N-in-NX format produce different capitalization of energy labels into residential transaction prices?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting sharp energy label thresholds (A, B, C ratings) in countries with mandatory Energy Performance Certificates, comparing price discontinuities in submarkets where local media adopted 1-in-X framing versus percentage framing in the same period.
data: Energy Performance Certificate registers linked to transaction records in the Netherlands, UK, or Ireland (used in published energy label capitalization studies), combined with local newspaper coverage data to classify framing adoption.
key_finding: The 1-in-X numerical format likely increases the measured price discontinuity at energy label thresholds by 1–3 percentage points relative to percentage framing, suggesting format-induced probability distortion amplifies capitalization beyond fundamental energy cost differences.
novelty_rationale: Applies the experimentally validated 1-in-X format bias to a real-world hedonic pricing setting, providing a cognitive explanation for the well-documented but theoretically puzzling variation in energy label capitalization across markets and time periods.
nn_title: Energy labels, misreporting, and heuristic hedonic models
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do real estate investors making portfolio-level acquisition decisions on behalf of an institutional fund exhibit greater risk aversion than individual investors purchasing for their own account, due to a heightened sense of responsibility for fiduciary outcomes?
method: Matched comparison of bid-to-ask price ratios, hold-out rates at auction, and leverage choices between institutional and individual buyers in commercial real estate auctions, exploiting variation in fund mandate specificity as a proxy for perceived responsibility intensity.
data: CoStar or RCA transaction database covering commercial property auctions with buyer type classification (institution versus individual/private), combined with fund mandate data from Preqin for institutional buyers, covering 2005–2023.
key_finding: Institutional fund managers likely bid 3–7% lower at auction and require 1.5–2 percentage points higher going-in cap rates than private investors for observationally equivalent assets, consistent with responsibility-induced risk aversion rather than information differences.
novelty_rationale: Applies the experimental finding that group representatives are more risk averse due to responsibility to a large-scale field setting in commercial real estate, offering a behavioral explanation for institutional investors' documented tendency to underbid at auctions.
nn_title: Institutional Investors’ Preferences for REIT Stocks
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the complexity of a choice set—specifically the number of mortgage products presented simultaneously—reduce the weight borrowers place on interest rate spread relative to fees, consistent with attentional dilution?
method: Analysis of HMDA loan application data combined with a lender's product menu width at origination, using variation in the number of products offered by different lenders to the same borrower profile to test whether wider menus predict lower sensitivity to rate-fee tradeoffs.
data: HMDA loan-level data (2018–2023) linked to lender product offerings from rate comparison platforms (Bankrate, LendingTree), with borrower shopping behavior identified using the methodology in published mortgage shopping studies.
key_finding: Borrowers facing lenders with 5 or more distinct mortgage products likely choose loans with 8–15 basis points higher effective rates than borrowers facing 2-product menus, consistent with attentional dilution reducing rate sensitivity as menu size grows.
novelty_rationale: Applies the attentional dilution effect—where more attributes reduce weight on any single attribute—to mortgage product choice, offering a behavioral explanation for why lenders with complex product menus may systematically extract higher margins.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do property appraisers who receive feedback that their previous valuations were 'too high' (relative to transaction prices) subsequently apply more conservative adjustments to distressed comparable sales, reducing appraisal inflation?
method: Quasi-experimental analysis of appraisal outcomes before and after lender feedback programs, exploiting staggered adoption of automated feedback systems across lenders, estimating the effect of directional feedback on subsequent appraisal inflation conditional on comparable selection.
data: Collateral Underwriter (CU) appraisal database accessed via Fannie Mae research agreement, combined with CoreLogic appraisal records and lender feedback flag data (used in published appraisal bias studies), covering 2012–2022.
key_finding: Appraisers who received 'too high' feedback in prior assignments likely reduce subsequent valuations by 1.2–2.5% on average, with the adjustment concentrated in markets with high foreclosure comparable availability, consistent with the experimental feedback-induced conservatism finding.
novelty_rationale: Translates the controlled appraisal feedback experiment from the corpus into a large-scale quasi-experiment, identifying whether real-world lender feedback systems produce the asymmetric conservatism documented in laboratory settings.
nn_title: Valuation Bias in Commercial Appraisal: A Transaction Price Feedback Experiment
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Does the introduction of a decoy listing option—an overpriced, inferior comparable listed simultaneously on the same platform—increase the sale probability and price of a target property through asymmetric dominance?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis exploiting MLS listing patterns where a new comparable listing enters a submarket during the marketing period of an existing property, classifying new entrants as decoys (higher price, inferior attributes) versus non-decoys using hedonic residuals.
data: MLS transaction and listing records covering at least 5 years in multiple metropolitan areas, with full price history, attribute data, and listing entry/exit dates (standard in published TOM and listing price studies), available from state MLS cooperatives.
key_finding: Properties whose marketing periods overlap with the entry of a dominated decoy listing likely experience 1.5–3% higher final sale prices and 15–25% shorter time on market, with effects concentrated in price segments where comparables are sparse.
novelty_rationale: Applies the asymmetric dominance (attraction) effect to naturally occurring real estate listing competition, moving beyond controlled experiments to test whether decoy dynamics emerge organically from listing behavior.
nn_title: List Prices, Sale Prices and Marketing Time: An Application to U.S. Housing Mark
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do older homeowners (65+) exhibit systematically different decision strategies when evaluating reverse mortgage options compared to younger borrowers, consistent with lifespan differences in adaptive multi-attribute decision-making?
method: Linked analysis of HECM counseling records and subsequent loan take-up decisions, using counselor-provided attribute information sets of varying complexity to test whether older applicants show lower sensitivity to product attribute tradeoffs and higher anchoring on appraised value.
data: HUD HECM counseling dataset combined with FHA HECM origination records (used in published reverse mortgage studies), with counselor session data on information presentation, covering 2010–2022.
key_finding: Homeowners over 75 are likely 30–45% less sensitive to interest rate spreads and loan-to-value tradeoffs than those aged 62–70, with decision quality (proximity to actuarially fair terms) declining monotonically with age, consistent with reduced adaptive decision-making.
novelty_rationale: Applies the lifespan decision-making literature's finding of reduced adaptive strategy use in older adults to reverse mortgage decisions, with direct consumer protection implications for HECM counseling design.
nn_title: Reverse Mortgage Loans: A Quantitative Analysis
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do commercial real estate brokers who occupy central positions in local professional networks charge higher transaction fees and achieve higher prices, but face diminishing returns as centrality increases due to perceived conflicts of interest?
method: Network analysis of commercial real estate transaction co-brokerage relationships using CoStar transaction data, constructing broker centrality measures, and estimating the nonlinear relationship between centrality and deal outcomes (price, TOM, commission rate) using polynomial regression.
data: CoStar commercial transaction database with broker identifiers, co-brokerage flags, transaction prices, and deal characteristics covering 2005–2022 in major U.S. markets (used in published REIT and commercial market studies).
key_finding: Broker centrality increases transaction prices up to a moderate threshold (top quartile) but shows flat or negative returns beyond that, consistent with very central brokers being perceived as embedded in conflicts that reduce their negotiating credibility.
novelty_rationale: Applies the network commitment and influence susceptibility literature to real estate brokerage, testing whether the embedding forces of network position create a nonlinear rather than monotone relationship with deal performance.
nn_title: Multiple listing service participation in the real estate brokerage industry: Co
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homebuyers who receive mortgage rate information from an unofficial peer source (friend's recent rate) anchor more strongly on that rate and shop less than those who receive official lender quotes, resulting in higher final rates?
method: Survey experiment administered to recent homebuyers via a housing research panel, randomizing whether participants are first shown a friend's reported rate versus an official lender quote, measuring subsequent search effort (number of lenders contacted) and final rate achieved.
data: Survey data collected via the American Life Panel or a mortgage lender's customer survey, linked via ZIP code to HMDA loan-level rate data to validate self-reported rates, with comparison to the published mortgage shopping savings literature.
key_finding: Buyers exposed to unofficial peer rate information first likely contact 0.8 fewer lenders and pay 9–14 basis points higher rates than those receiving official quotes first, consistent with unofficial information reducing search motivation through false precision anchoring.
novelty_rationale: Applies the official versus unofficial information tax compliance literature to mortgage rate shopping, testing whether information source credibility shapes search behavior in a domain with large financial consequences.
nn_title: Paying Too Much? Borrower Sophistication and Overpayment in the U.S. Mortgage Ma
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Does the gradual accumulation of visible neighborhood vacancy—modeled as a slowly materializing public bad—produce less behavioral response from nearby homeowners (reduced maintenance, delayed listing) than an equivalent sudden concentration of vacancies?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis comparing home improvement permit activity and listing behavior in neighborhoods that experienced gradual vacancy accumulation over 3+ years versus those that experienced sudden vacancy spikes (from factory closures or natural disasters), using parcel-level data.
data: American Community Survey vacancy data linked to permit records from city building departments, CoreLogic transaction and listing data, and plant closure announcements from WARN Act filings and news archives, covering multiple MSAs.
key_finding: Neighborhoods with gradual vacancy accumulation likely show 20–35% lower maintenance permit activity and 15–25% longer listing delays than neighborhoods with equivalent sudden vacancy shocks, consistent with the 'out of sight, out of mind' gradual public bad effect.
novelty_rationale: Applies the gradually materializing public bad experimental finding to neighborhood externalities, providing a cognitive explanation for why urban decay in gradually declining neighborhoods proves harder to reverse than post-disaster recovery.
nn_title: Foreclosure externalities and Vacant Property Registration Ordinances
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Do REIT CEOs who exhibit incidental high confidence from strong recent stock performance make larger, riskier acquisitions in unrelated property types, consistent with confidence transfer across domains?
method: Panel regression analysis linking REIT CEO-level confidence proxies (constructed from earnings call linguistic measures and insider trading patterns) to subsequent cross-sector acquisition activity and deal leverage, exploiting exogenous variation in confidence from market-wide return shocks.
data: REIT earnings call transcripts (WRDS) linked to FactSet Mergerstat acquisition records, CEO insider trading data from SEC Form 4 filings, and REIT property-type classification from NAREIT, covering 2000–2022.
key_finding: REIT CEOs in the top quintile of incidental confidence measure following market-wide booms are likely 40–60% more likely to make cross-sector acquisitions and pay 4–7% higher prices, with the effect reversing for property-type-specific confidence signals.
novelty_rationale: Applies the incidental confidence transfer mechanism—where success in one domain inflates self-assessment in another—to REIT capital allocation, distinguishing it from existing overconfidence research that treats confidence as domain-general.
nn_title: CEO Overconfidence, REIT Investment Activity and Performance
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do prospective tenants who are shown rental listings where energy costs and rent are presented as a combined total cost exhibit higher willingness to pay for energy-efficient units than those shown rent and energy costs as separate line items?
method: Randomized survey experiment with representative sample of renters, presenting otherwise identical rental listings in combined versus separate cost formats, measuring stated willingness to pay for energy-efficient versus standard units and implicit prices for efficiency.
data: Survey data administered via a large rental platform (Zillow, Apartments.com) or academic research panel, linked to actual listing characteristics and local utility rate data from EIA, with sample drawn from markets where energy performance disclosure is mandatory.
key_finding: Combined cost presentation likely increases willingness to pay for high-efficiency units by 8–15% relative to separate presentation, suggesting that segregated cost framing systematically suppresses energy efficiency capitalization in rental markets.
novelty_rationale: Applies the evaluation mode and attribute evaluability literature to rental housing energy disclosure policy, providing a cognitive mechanism for the documented gap between energy efficiency's cost savings and its market capitalization.
nn_title: The capitalization of energy efficiency: Evidence from the housing market
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do homeowners who experience a sharp, salient house price decline exhibit a 'chasing losses' pattern—listing at above-market prices and refusing price reductions—that is stronger than predicted by loss aversion or anchoring alone?
method: Analysis of MLS listing histories with price revision data, identifying homeowners who purchased near the market peak and subsequently listed during price declines, testing whether negative unrealized returns predict listing price markup and number of price reductions using regression with purchase-price fixed effects.
data: CoreLogic or Zillow transaction and listing price history data linked by property to purchase price records, covering the 2006–2012 bust and the 2022 correction in major U.S. metros, with seller characteristics from county assessor records.
key_finding: Homeowners with unrealized losses of 10–20% are likely to set listing prices 5–9% above estimated market value and require 2–3 additional price reductions before selling, with the markup increasing nonlinearly with loss magnitude consistent with affect-driven loss chasing.
novelty_rationale: Applies the affect-driven chasing losses framework—where negative emotional states increase risk-seeking—to seller pricing strategy, distinguishing emotional loss-chasing from rational prospect-theory loss aversion through its focus on nonlinearity at large losses.
nn_title: Listing Price, Time on Market, and Ultimate Selling Price: Causes and Effects of
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does corporate landlord status—where accountability is diffused across organizational hierarchy—reduce the psychological inhibition against filing evictions compared to individual landlords, even controlling for tenant payment behavior and property characteristics?
method: Matched comparison of eviction filing rates between corporate and individual landlords for rental units with similar rent-to-income ratios, delinquency histories, and neighborhood characteristics, using eviction court records linked to deed ownership data.
data: Eviction Lab national eviction filing database linked to county deed records and corporate ownership data from the National Consumer Law Center or ATTOM Data, with tenant income proxied by Census tract data, covering 2015–2022 in 10+ cities.
key_finding: Corporate landlords likely file evictions at rates 25–45% higher than individual landlords for tenants with identical arrears levels, with the gap largest for multi-layered ownership structures (LLCs within LLCs) where diffusion of responsibility is most complete.
novelty_rationale: Applies the diffusion of responsibility for actions framework to landlord-tenant relations, providing a behavioral organizational mechanism for the documented corporate landlord eviction premium beyond profit-motive or portfolio-optimization explanations.
nn_title: The rise of corporate landlords: An examination of behavioral differences in the
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do real estate professionals who work in female-minority brokerage teams exhibit lower stereotyping in racial correspondence experiments—responding more equitably to minority buyer inquiries—than agents in homogeneous teams?
method: Large-scale correspondence experiment sending paired email inquiries from White and minority (Black, Hispanic) prospective buyers to listing agents, coding agent team gender and racial composition from license records, and estimating the interaction between team diversity and differential response rates.
