data: Real estate agent license records from state regulatory agencies linked to brokerage affiliation data, combined with a new correspondence experiment using synthetic buyer emails in 15–20 major U.S. markets, building on the design of published audit studies.
key_finding: Agents in gender- and racially-diverse brokerage teams likely show 30–50% smaller response rate gaps between White and minority inquiries than agents in homogeneous teams, consistent with diverse teams reducing social identity threat and stereotype activation.
novelty_rationale: Applies the social identity threat reduction through peer connection finding to real estate discrimination, testing whether organizational team composition—not just individual agent characteristics—moderates discriminatory behavior in audit settings.
nn_title: Restricted access: Real estate agent response to client race, ethnicity, gender,
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does revealing a homebuyer's prior purchase price history to listing agents during negotiation—as occurs when agents research buyer's past transactions—increase the final sale price through anchoring on the buyer's revealed wealth ceiling?
method: Hedonic regression exploiting variation in whether buyers' prior transaction history is easily discoverable in public records (states with online deed access versus restricted access), testing whether buyers in high-transparency states pay systematically higher prices controlling for property and buyer characteristics.
data: CoreLogic buyer history data linked to transaction prices across states with varying levels of online deed record accessibility, coded using state-level public records laws, covering 2010–2022 with buyer repeat-purchase identification.
key_finding: Buyers in high-transparency deed access states likely pay 1.5–3% more for comparable properties, with the premium larger for buyers whose prior purchase was at a higher price point, consistent with agents using disclosed wealth information as an anchor in negotiations.
novelty_rationale: Applies the revealed preference inference and strategic information use literature to real estate negotiation, testing whether information transparency laws unintentionally arm sellers with anchoring tools that extract surplus from buyers.
nn_title: Examining Both Sides of the Transaction: Bargaining in the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homeowners whose neighbors have visibly completed energy efficiency retrofits (rooftop solar, heat pumps) subsequently undertake their own retrofits at higher rates, consistent with social norm signaling from low-ranking group members who are perceived as better norm sources?
method: Spatial difference-in-differences analysis using permit records for solar installations and HVAC replacements, testing whether visible neighbor retrofits (on street-facing versus rear-facing roofs) predict subsequent permits for adjacent properties, exploiting building orientation as an instrument for visibility.
data: Building permit records from city open data portals linked to parcel-level property characteristics, Google Street View satellite imagery for solar panel detection (used in published energy adoption studies), and Census tract socioeconomic data for 20+ U.S. cities.
key_finding: Visible neighbor solar installations on street-facing roofs likely increase adjacent homeowner retrofit probability by 15–25% within 18 months, with the effect strongest in middle-income neighborhoods where peer behavior is most informative about norm adoption.
novelty_rationale: Applies the finding that low-ranking group members are perceived as better norm sources to neighborhood energy adoption, predicting that middle-class early adopters—not wealthy visible innovators—generate the strongest social contagion in retrofit decisions.
nn_title: Understanding housing market responses to stringent energy codes
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers who are randomly assigned to justify their mortgage choice to a counselor before signing exhibit lower 60-day delinquency rates than those who sign without justification, regardless of whether counseling changes their product choice?
method: Randomized controlled trial embedded in an HUD-approved mortgage counseling program, assigning applicants to a process justification condition (articulate reasons for chosen product) versus standard counseling, tracking 12-month delinquency via credit bureau records.
data: HUD housing counseling agency administrative data linked to credit bureau records via the National Foreclosure Mitigation Counseling program database, combined with HMDA origination data to characterize the loan chosen, covering new originations in 2024–2025.
key_finding: Borrowers who justify their mortgage choice are likely to show 15–25% lower 60-day delinquency rates within 12 months, with larger effects for ARM borrowers and those choosing non-traditional products, consistent with justification improving decision quality without changing strategy use.
novelty_rationale: Applies the process accountability and justification literature—which found null effects on accuracy in lab tasks—to mortgage counseling, testing whether field-stakes conditions produce the behavioral discipline effects that laboratory tasks cannot detect.
nn_title: Delinquencies, Default and Borrowers' Strategic Behavior toward the Modification
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do real estate listing platforms that display property views using dynamic sequential presentation—revealing rooms one at a time versus all at once—produce different implicit prices for interior quality attributes by exploiting peak-end rule effects?
method: Field experiment on a major real estate listing platform, randomly assigning properties to sequential (room-by-room reveal) versus simultaneous (full gallery) presentation formats, measuring click-through rates, inquiry rates, and final sale prices while coding which rooms appear last in the sequence.
data: Platform experiment data from a major listing service (Zillow, Realtor.com) accessible via research partnership, linked to MLS transaction outcomes and property characteristic data, with room quality coded from listing photos using published machine learning image classification methods.
key_finding: Sequential presentation ending with the highest-quality room likely increases inquiry rates by 12–20% and final sale prices by 1.5–3% relative to simultaneous presentation, with the effect mediated by subjective property quality ratings that overweight the final image.
novelty_rationale: Applies the peak-end rule and duration neglect from hedonic experience research to online real estate marketing, testing whether the sequence of visual information presentation creates systematic valuation biases that listing agents can exploit.
nn_title: The Effects of Charm Listing Prices on House Transaction Prices
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homebuyers who recently experienced a windfall gain (e.g., stock market appreciation) in an unrelated domain subsequently pay higher prices for residential properties due to incidental confidence transfer?
method: Instrumental variables estimation using exogenous variation in local stock market wealth shocks as a shifter of incidental confidence, with household-level transaction data matched to brokerage account records or zip-code-level equity wealth indices, testing whether windfall-exposed buyers pay systematically higher sale-to-list price ratios.
data: CoreLogic or Zillow transaction-level data matched to zip-code equity wealth estimates from the Federal Reserve Distributional Financial Accounts (2000–2023), identifying buyers by zip code of prior residence and local stock market exposure.
key_finding: Buyers in zip codes experiencing large recent equity gains pay 1–3% higher sale-to-list price ratios, consistent with incidental confidence transfer inflating willingness to pay beyond fundamentals.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the incidental confidence transfer paradigm from psychology to housing markets, offering a new behavioral channel—distinct from wealth effects—to explain overbidding that is rarely studied in real estate.
nn_title: Idiosyncratic Risk in Housing Markets
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers who face a larger choice set of loan products pay higher effective interest rates due to attentional dilution of the rate attribute?
method: Regression discontinuity or difference-in-differences exploiting regulatory changes that altered the number of mortgage products available in specific markets, testing whether borrowers in higher-product-count lender environments pay higher spreads after controlling for credit risk, using the attentional dilution logic that multiplying non-rate attributes reduces weight placed on the rate dimension.
data: HMDA loan-level origination data (2007–2023) combined with lender product-count data from RateWatch or proprietary rate surveys used in published mortgage pricing research, covering millions of originations across lenders with heterogeneous product menus.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in lender product count is associated with 5–15 basis point higher effective rates for otherwise identical borrowers, consistent with attentional dilution reducing rate-sensitivity.
novelty_rationale: Applies the attentional dilution mechanism from multi-attribute choice research to mortgage markets, providing a new behavioral explanation for the finding that many borrowers pay above-market rates even when shopping.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do homeowners exhibit negative time discounting for home sale decisions—preferring to sell sooner during declining markets even at lower prices—because anticipated dread of further price declines amplifies perceived waiting costs?
method: Hazard model analysis of time-on-market and final sale price for listings originated in declining versus appreciating price regimes, using the Genesove-Mayer loss aversion framework extended with a dread-based discounting parameter estimated from seller reservation price proxies (listing price cuts) and local price trend data.
data: Multiple Listing Service data from ATTOM or CoreLogic for major metros (2006–2012 bust period and 2018–2023), with repeat-sales price indices from FHFA providing local price trend measures, covering tens of millions of listings.
key_finding: Sellers in declining markets accept prices 2–5% below those predicted by financial option models, with the excess discount increasing in the steepness of the recent price trend, consistent with dread-driven negative discounting.
novelty_rationale: Bridges the anticipated dread literature from intertemporal choice psychology to housing seller behavior, offering a psychologically grounded mechanism distinct from standard loss aversion for why sellers capitulate in falling markets.
nn_title: Search Benefit in Housing Markets: An Inverted U‐Shaped Price and TOM Relation
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do institutional corporate landlords in multifamily housing face systematically lower moral accountability for maintenance failures than individual landlords because diffused organizational responsibility reduces perceived culpability?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis comparing housing code violation rates and tenant complaint filings before and after buildings are acquired by corporate versus individual investors, using ownership changes recorded in deed records as treatment events, with the diffusion-of-responsibility mechanism tested by examining whether larger corporate ownership structures predict larger post-acquisition violation increases.
data: Deed transfer records from county assessor files linked to housing code violation databases from New York City, Chicago, or Los Angeles open-data portals (2010–2023), combined with the NMHC/Urban Institute corporate landlord ownership datasets used in published rental market research.
key_finding: Buildings acquired by large corporate landlords experience 15–30% more housing code violations per unit relative to individually owned comparables, with the effect increasing in organizational size, consistent with diffused responsibility reducing maintenance accountability.
novelty_rationale: Applies diffusion-of-responsibility theory from social psychology to the corporate landlord debate, providing a psychological mechanism for observed service quality differences that goes beyond standard incentive-based explanations.
nn_title: The rise of corporate landlords: An examination of behavioral differences in the
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a zero-outcome preference in flood risk evaluation, disproportionately favoring properties located just outside flood zone boundaries even when the marginal risk reduction at that boundary is small relative to properties deeper outside the zone?
method: Regression discontinuity design using FEMA flood zone boundary maps, comparing transaction prices of properties just inside versus just outside Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA) boundaries, testing whether the price discontinuity at the categorical boundary exceeds what actuarially fair flood insurance cost differentials would predict, with a structural test of the zero-risk premium.
data: CoreLogic or Zillow transaction data merged with FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer parcel-level flood zone designations and NFIP policy and claims data (2000–2023), replicating the spatial regression discontinuity designs used in prior flood risk capitalization studies.
key_finding: Properties just outside the SFHA boundary command a premium 3–7% larger than actuarially justified flood cost differences, consistent with buyers placing disproportionate value on the categorical zero-flood-zone designation rather than marginal risk reductions.
novelty_rationale: Applies the zero-outcome preference (much-ado-about-nothing effect) from life-saving decision psychology to flood risk valuation, generating a testable prediction about price discontinuities that existing flood risk hedonic studies have not examined.
nn_title: Dutch dilemma: Housing prices and flood risk exposure
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do forecasters of metropolitan house prices exhibit systematic underconfidence in their stated probability intervals while nonetheless showing good discriminative ability across markets?
method: Calibration and discrimination analysis applied to a panel of professional house price forecasters, decomposing forecast accuracy into calibration (interval coverage) and resolution components analogous to the geopolitical forecasting literature, using Brier scores and log scores to characterize forecaster skill across boom, bust, and recovery phases.
data: Zillow, Pulsenomics, and Wall Street Journal housing forecast survey data matched to FHFA realized price outcomes (2012–2023), following the forecast evaluation methodology used in the geopolitical intelligence forecasting literature and similar to approaches in existing real estate return predictability papers.
key_finding: Professional house price forecasters show good discriminative ability across markets but systematic underconfidence (intervals too wide), with calibration improving in markets with more transparent transaction data, paralleling findings from geopolitical forecasting research.
novelty_rationale: Imports the calibration-discrimination decomposition from forecasting psychology into housing price prediction, providing the first systematic characterization of whether professional real estate forecasters are under- or overconfident rather than simply assessing directional accuracy.
nn_title: House Price Expectations: Unbiasedness and Efficiency of Forecasters
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a positive skew preference in housing markets, systematically overpaying for properties in high-variance neighborhoods with a small probability of outsized appreciation relative to expected value?
method: Cross-sectional hedonic analysis augmenting standard price models with measures of neighborhood return distribution skewness estimated from repeat-sales data, testing whether buyers pay premiums for right-skewed return distributions even after controlling for expected appreciation and variance, with subsample tests by buyer leverage and investment horizon.
data: CoreLogic repeat-sales transaction database (2000–2023) used to construct zip-code-level return distribution moments, merged with buyer characteristics from HMDA and assessed property values from county assessor records, covering all major U.S. metros.
key_finding: Buyers pay a 1–3% premium per unit increase in neighborhood return skewness conditional on expected return and variance, consistent with positive-skew preferences, with larger premiums among cash buyers and in markets with high investor activity.
novelty_rationale: Applies the positive skew preference from gambling and lottery research to housing investment decisions, generating testable hedonic price predictions about distribution moments beyond mean and variance that have not been examined in the real estate literature.
nn_title: Do House Price Levels Anticipate Subsequent Price Changes within Metropolitan Ar
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do homeowners who are told that their self-reported home value estimate is lower than the market estimate subsequently adjust their consumption and mortgage borrowing upward disproportionately relative to the value revision, consistent with confidence-manipulation effects on financial behavior?
method: Matching of American Housing Survey self-reported home values to Zillow automated valuation model estimates at the unit level, using cases where official reappraisal data creates quasi-random upward revisions to test whether households receiving positive value surprises increase home equity extraction and durable consumption beyond the wealth effect predicted by life-cycle models.
data: American Housing Survey panel data with self-reported values linked to Zillow or FHFA zip-code price indices and the Federal Reserve's Survey of Consumer Finances for consumption outcomes (2001–2021), following the methodology of existing housing wealth effect papers.
key_finding: Homeowners receiving a positive confidence shock from learning their home is worth more than expected increase home equity borrowing by 20–40% more than the implied wealth effect predicts, consistent with confidence amplification of financial risk-taking.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the confidence manipulation paradigm from JDM psychology to housing wealth research, distinguishing a confidence channel from a pure wealth channel in the housing consumption literature.
nn_title: Home Price Expectations and Spending: Evidence from a Field Experiment
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Do property tax appeal decisions exhibit a 'constraint neglect' bias in which assessors and appeal boards fail to account for the information constraints faced by initial appraisers, systematically penalizing appraisers who operated in low-information environments?
method: Analysis of property tax appeal outcomes linked to assessor-level information quality proxies (number of comparables, time since last arm's-length sale, property atypicality), testing whether appeal success rates are higher for properties where the initial appraiser faced genuine information scarcity, holding initial error magnitude constant.
data: Administrative property tax appeal records from Cook County (Illinois), Fulton County (Georgia), or New York City (all of which publish appeal databases) linked to assessor parcel files and MLS comparable sales data (2010–2023), following the methodology of existing assessment uniformity papers.
key_finding: Appeal boards are 15–25% more likely to reduce assessed values for properties in low-information environments even holding over-assessment rates constant, suggesting constraint neglect leads to unfair penalization of assessors facing harder valuation problems.
novelty_rationale: Applies the constraint neglect bias from judgment and decision making to property tax administration, providing a new behavioral explanation for assessment appeal patterns beyond strategic filing by sophisticated property owners.
nn_title: The Effects of Property Tax Protests on the Assessment Uniformity of Residential
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do homebuyers who evaluate mortgage products in joint (side-by-side) evaluation mode choose lower-cost loans more frequently than buyers evaluating products in separate sequential mode, with the effect mediated by differential attention to the total-cost attribute?
method: Regression analysis exploiting cross-lender variation in online mortgage comparison platform designs—some presenting options simultaneously, others sequentially—combined with loan-level HMDA pricing outcomes, using lender platform design as an instrument for evaluation mode to test whether joint evaluation reduces the rate spread paid by observably similar borrowers.
data: CFPB HMDA data (2018–2023) merged with lender-level data on online platform interface characteristics obtained from web-scraping or lender disclosure filings, following the identification strategies in existing mortgage rate shopping papers.
key_finding: Borrowers using joint-evaluation mortgage platforms pay 8–15 basis points lower rate spreads than comparable borrowers on sequential-presentation platforms, consistent with evaluation mode shifting attention toward total cost.
novelty_rationale: Directly translates evaluation mode effects from consumer choice research to mortgage market design, providing causal evidence on whether platform interface choices—not just consumer sophistication—determine how much borrowers overpay.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do real estate agents who receive positive performance feedback (high sales volume ranking) in one period subsequently take on riskier listings—overpriced properties or unusual assets—in the following period due to incidental confidence spillover?
method: Agent fixed-effects panel regression using MLS data where agent-level quarterly sales volume rankings are constructed and linked to subsequent listing characteristics (initial overpricing relative to eventual sale price, property atypicality, time-on-market), testing whether top-ranked agents accept riskier listings the following quarter.
data: MLS transaction databases with agent identifiers for large metro areas (2005–2023), following the agent expertise and performance methodology of existing real estate brokerage papers, with listing-level characteristics constructed from hedonic residuals.
key_finding: Agents in the top quintile of prior-quarter sales volume accept listings with 3–5% higher initial overpricing and 20% longer eventual time-on-market in the following quarter, consistent with incidental high confidence expanding risk tolerance in listing acquisition.
novelty_rationale: Applies the incidental confidence transfer mechanism to agent behavior in real estate, providing a new explanation for cyclical overpricing and listing strategy heterogeneity that complements standard incentive-based models of agent behavior.
nn_title: The Disciplining Effect of Concern for Referrals: Evidence from Real Estate Agen
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers exhibit a 'peak-end' evaluation of their mortgage experience such that satisfaction and referral behavior are disproportionately determined by the closing experience and the peak stress moment rather than the cumulative process?
method: Analysis of mortgage customer satisfaction survey data linked to loan-level origination characteristics, testing whether satisfaction scores and subsequent referral behavior are better predicted by closing-day outcomes (rate lock expiration near closing, last-minute appraisal issues) and peak-stress events (appraisal gap, income re-verification) than by average process duration or total cost.
data: Fannie Mae National Housing Survey or J.D. Power mortgage satisfaction data linked to origination characteristics from HMDA (2015–2023), following survey methodology used in existing mortgage choice and borrower behavior papers.
key_finding: Closing-day outcomes explain 30–40% more variance in satisfaction than process duration, and peak-stress events during underwriting reduce satisfaction and referrals by an amount equivalent to a 20–30 basis point rate increase, consistent with the peak-end rule.
novelty_rationale: Imports the peak-end rule from hedonic psychology into mortgage market research, with implications for lender process design and for understanding why borrower satisfaction does not align with objectively measured loan quality.
nn_title: The Relative Termination Experience of Adjustable to Fixed-Rate Mortgages
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do renters in jurisdictions with stronger tenant protection laws exhibit lower residential mobility in response to improved job matches because housing regulation functions as an embedding force that increases switching costs beyond financial factors?
method: Difference-in-differences estimation exploiting staggered enactment of just-cause eviction protection laws across U.S. cities, measuring changes in within-city residential mobility rates and job-worker match quality (proxied by wage residuals) among renter households before and after reform, using the embedding forces framework to predict asymmetric mobility effects.
data: American Community Survey microdata linked to IPUMS (2010–2023) for mobility outcomes, combined with city-level just-cause eviction law passage dates from the Urban Displacement Project and Eviction Lab policy databases used in published rental regulation papers.
key_finding: Just-cause eviction protection reduces residential mobility by 10–20% among renters and reduces the correlation between job-match improvement and residential relocation, consistent with housing regulation creating psychological and procedural embedding forces beyond financial lock-in.
novelty_rationale: Applies the network embedding forces framework to tenant protection policy, generating a testable prediction that goes beyond standard lock-in explanations by distinguishing psychological commitment from financial moving costs.
nn_title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evi
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do homebuyers who are told their preferred neighborhood ranks low on safety (a negatively framed attribute) subsequently overweight that attribute in their final purchase decision relative to buyers presented the same information as a positive crime-reduction framing?
method: Exploiting natural variation in how neighborhood crime statistics are presented across online real estate platforms (some showing crime index scores, others showing percentage improvement), testing whether buyers in negative-frame markets pay lower prices for identical properties in moderate-crime neighborhoods using a border discontinuity or matched-market design.
data: Zillow or Redfin transaction data matched to neighborhood crime data from the FBI Uniform Crime Reports and NeighborhoodScout, with platform-level presentation format variation identified from web archive data (2015–2023), following hedonic methodology from existing crime externality papers.
key_finding: Buyers in markets where crime is framed negatively (absolute crime rate) discount comparable properties by 2–4% more than buyers in markets where the same data is framed positively (crime reduction percentage), with the effect largest for buyers entering unfamiliar neighborhoods.
novelty_rationale: Applies attribute framing effects from psychology to real estate search, providing causal evidence that information presentation format—not just underlying risk—shapes property valuation and extending attribute framing research to high-stakes durable goods.
nn_title: Potential crime risk and housing market responses
assigned_label: Crime's economic effects on property and wages

research_question: Do younger (novice) homebuyers show lower sensitivity to environmental regularities such as seasonal price patterns and inventory cycles compared to experienced repeat buyers, analogous to expert-novice differences in forecasting?
method: Signal detection analysis of buyer offer timing and bid-to-list price ratios across the seasonal housing cycle, comparing first-time versus repeat buyers using HMDA purchase loan data merged with local inventory and price seasonality measures, testing whether repeat buyers time offers more accurately relative to seasonal price troughs.
data: HMDA first-time homebuyer indicator merged with NAR seasonality data and local MLS listing inventory measures from Zillow (2010–2023), following the tenure choice and buyer behavior methodology of existing housing market papers, with signal detection theory applied analogously to published sports forecasting research.
key_finding: Repeat buyers pay prices 1–2% lower than first-time buyers for comparable properties by better timing their offers relative to seasonal inventory peaks, with the expert advantage larger in markets with stronger seasonality, consistent with expert sensitivity to environmental regularities.
novelty_rationale: Applies signal detection theory and the expert-novice forecasting framework to housing market timing behavior, providing a psychologically grounded account of first-time buyer price premiums that distinguishes market-timing skill from selection effects.
nn_title: Hot and Cold Seasons in the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do REIT managers who make capital allocation decisions on behalf of institutional shareholders exhibit lower acquisition price premiums than owner-operators of private real estate because accountability to principals induces greater risk aversion?
method: Matched-sample comparison of acquisition prices paid by externally managed REITs (accountable to board and shareholders), internally managed REITs, and private real estate funds for comparable properties using transaction-level data, testing the group-representative risk aversion mechanism with accountability intensity proxied by institutional ownership concentration.
data: CoStar or RCA commercial real estate transaction database merged with REIT ownership and governance data from SNL Financial (2000–2023), following the REIT acquisition methodology of existing papers on CEO overconfidence and REIT investment.
key_finding: Externally managed REITs with high institutional ownership pay 3–6% lower acquisition premiums than internally managed REITs or private buyers, consistent with principal accountability inducing risk aversion in group representatives.
novelty_rationale: Applies the group-representative risk aversion mechanism from social psychology to REIT corporate governance, generating predictions about organizational structure and acquisition pricing that complement existing agency cost explanations.
nn_title: The Effect of Corporate Governance on Investment: Evidence from Real Estate Inve
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do homeowners exhibit future self-continuity gradients in mortgage prepayment behavior, with higher future self-continuity predicting greater voluntary prepayment and wealth accumulation through housing?
method: Cross-sectional regression analysis linking Survey of Consumer Finances financial planning horizon measures (a proxy for future self-continuity) to mortgage prepayment behavior and home equity accumulation, using instrumental variables based on early-life savings program participation to address endogeneity of planning orientation.
data: Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances (1998–2022) with detailed mortgage and financial planning variables, merged with PSID wealth panel data for longitudinal home equity outcomes, following the methodology of existing mortgage behavior and household finance papers.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in financial planning horizon is associated with 15–25% higher voluntary prepayment rates and $15,000–$30,000 more home equity after ten years, with the effect concentrated among middle-income households where the housing wealth channel matters most.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the future self-continuity framework from savings psychology to mortgage behavior, providing a psychologically grounded explanation for heterogeneous prepayment rates beyond financial option theory.
nn_title: The drivers of sustainable homeownership
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Does the presence of a 'charm listed' decoy property on a listing platform—priced just below a round-number threshold but with clearly inferior attributes—increase the sale price of adjacent comparable properties by making the target appear relatively more attractive?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis using MLS data to identify time windows when charm-priced inferior listings appear and disappear from local search results, testing whether comparable non-charm-listed properties in the same micro-market sell for higher prices during periods of decoy presence using a within-neighborhood identification strategy.
data: Zillow or Redfin listing-level data with full price history and listing characteristics for major metros (2012–2023), following the charm pricing methodology of existing real estate listing research and the decoy effect identification designs from the experimental choice literature.
key_finding: Comparable properties in micro-markets with active charm-priced inferior listings sell for 0.5–1.5% more during the decoy listing window, with the effect larger in markets with fewer listings and higher buyer search costs, consistent with attraction effect logic.
novelty_rationale: Tests the attraction/decoy effect from multi-attribute choice theory in real market conditions using observational data, extending the charm pricing literature from focal property effects to cross-property spillover effects.
nn_title: The Effects of Charm Listing Prices on House Transaction Prices
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homeowners misinterpret gradual neighborhood demographic change as evidence of stable housing fundamentals, exhibiting an 'outcome density bias' in which the slow accumulation of visible neighborhood signals fails to update price expectations appropriately?
method: Panel regression linking the speed of demographic change (rate of income or racial composition shift measured from decennial and ACS data) to the gap between homeowner self-reported values and hedonic market estimates, testing whether gradual-transition neighborhoods show larger and more persistent self-assessment biases than neighborhoods undergoing rapid demographic change.
data: American Housing Survey self-reported values linked to Census tract demographic change measures from the 2000, 2010, and 2020 decennial Census and ACS (2001–2021), following the homeowner valuation accuracy methodology of existing papers on self-reported versus estimated home values.
key_finding: Homeowners in gradually changing neighborhoods overestimate home values by 5–10% more than those in rapidly changing comparable neighborhoods, consistent with gradual signal accumulation failing to trigger belief updating relative to salient fast-change contexts.
novelty_rationale: Applies the outcome density bias and gradual materialization literature from causal learning research to housing price expectation formation, providing a psychologically grounded mechanism for the persistence of homeowner over-optimism in declining neighborhoods.
nn_title: Do House Price Levels Anticipate Subsequent Price Changes within Metropolitan Ar
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do real estate developers exhibit negative skew aversion in land acquisition decisions, systematically avoiding parcels with positively skewed development option value distributions in favor of lower-variance, lower-expected-value sites?
method: Analysis of land auction data linking parcel characteristics (topography, regulatory uncertainty, proximity to growth edge) to option value distribution moments estimated from a real options model, testing whether developers' actual bid prices exhibit a discount for positive skewness relative to symmetric distributions of equivalent expected value.
data: Singapore Land Authority or Hong Kong government land auction records used in existing land auction papers, merged with parcel-level development outcome data and GIS topographic measures, covering several hundred to several thousand auctions over 2000–2020.
key_finding: Developers discount land parcels with positively skewed development value distributions by 5–10% relative to symmetric-value parcels of equal expected value, consistent with negative skewness aversion in high-stakes irreversible investment decisions.
novelty_rationale: Applies distributional preference theory—specifically the boundary conditions for positive-skew bias—to land developer decision-making, generating novel predictions about real options exercise that complement existing risk aversion models of development timing.
nn_title: Risk aversion and urban land development options
assigned_label: Real Options Theory in Land Development

research_question: Do commercial mortgage servicers in CMBS pools exhibit diffusion of responsibility for loss mitigation decisions when multiple institutional parties share oversight, resulting in worse outcomes for investors compared to concentrated servicing arrangements?
method: Within-deal variation in servicer oversight structure exploiting CMBS trust documents and servicer-trustee affiliation data, testing whether loans with more dispersed oversight (multiple servicers, more trustees per loan) experience higher loss-given-default and longer resolution times than observably similar loans with concentrated accountability, using the diffusion-of-responsibility framework.
data: Trepp CMBS loan-level database with servicer and trustee identity linked to realized loss outcomes (2000–2020), following the CMBS servicer conflict-of-interest methodology of existing CMBS governance papers, covering hundreds of thousands of commercial mortgages.
key_finding: Loans with three or more distinct oversight parties experience 8–15% higher realized losses and 30–60 day longer resolution timelines than loans with concentrated servicer accountability, consistent with diffused responsibility reducing mitigation effort.
novelty_rationale: Applies diffusion-of-responsibility theory to CMBS governance, complementing existing conflict-of-interest explanations by identifying a psychological accountability mechanism distinct from financial incentive misalignment.
nn_title: Trustee affiliation and servicer oversight: Evidence from CMBS markets
assigned_label: Securitization, Risk Retention, and Intermediation

research_question: Do homebuyers who receive mortgage pre-approval information framed as 'you qualify for up to X' anchor their subsequent offer prices at higher levels than buyers receiving the same information framed as 'the median buyer in your income bracket borrows Y,' consistent with upper-bound anchoring effects?
method: Regression analysis using cross-lender variation in pre-approval letter framing conventions (maximum approval amount versus typical borrowing amount), identified from lender disclosure practices and borrower survey data, testing whether the framing of credit limits predicts sale-to-income ratios and bid-to-list price ratios among first-time buyers.
data: CFPB Mortgage Survey data or HMDA buyer characteristics linked to CoreLogic transaction prices (2015–2023), with lender-level pre-approval framing practices identified from a sample of pre-approval letters collected through FOI or lender disclosure databases.
key_finding: Buyers receiving maximum-approval framing purchase homes 3–6% more expensive and submit bids 2–4% closer to list price than buyers receiving typical-borrowing framing, with the effect largest among first-time buyers who lack prior market experience as an anchor correction mechanism.
novelty_rationale: Applies the anchoring literature from JDM to the credit pre-approval process, providing the first test of whether lender communication framing—rather than credit constraints per se—influences house price formation through buyer offer behavior.
nn_title: Paying Too Much? Borrower Sophistication and Overpayment in the U.S. Mortgage Ma
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Do sellers who receive an early offer quickly after listing treat that offer as a temporal reference point that reduces their willingness to wait for better offers, leading to lower ultimate sale prices compared to sellers whose first offer arrives after a longer initial search period?
method: Regression discontinuity in the timing of first offer arrival—exploiting the discontinuity between listings that receive a first offer within the first week versus in weeks two or three—testing whether properties receiving very early offers sell for lower prices and experience shorter subsequent marketing periods, after controlling for property quality and market conditions.
data: MLS data with offer arrival timestamps available through Redfin or broker cooperative data agreements (2015–2023), used in several published papers on market tightness and bidding wars, with days-to-first-offer and final sale price as key variables.
key_finding: Sellers receiving a first offer within three days of listing sell for 1–2% less than comparable sellers receiving their first offer after ten or more days, consistent with early offers establishing a temporal reference point that shortens optimal waiting and reduces bargaining patience.
novelty_rationale: Applies temporal reference point and preference-for-quicker-offers research to seller search behavior, generating predictions about listing strategy and seller patience that extend the existing search model literature by adding a psychological timing mechanism.
nn_title: List Prices, Sale Prices and Marketing Time: An Application to U.S. Housing Mark
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homeowners who hold properties as part of multigenerational household arrangements exhibit different strategic default thresholds than single-household owners because shared residential accountability reduces the moral permissibility of walking away?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the sharp increase in multigenerational coresidence following the 2008 crisis, comparing default and modification take-up rates among mortgage borrowers in multigenerational versus single-generation households identified from American Community Survey and HMDA data, testing whether social accountability moderates default thresholds holding financial incentives constant.
data: HMDA origination data linked to ACS household composition measures at the census tract level (2008–2016), combined with CoreLogic or Black Knight mortgage performance data for default outcomes, following the strategic default and household composition methodology of existing mortgage papers.
key_finding: Multigenerational households exhibit default rates 10–20% lower than single-generation households with identical negative equity positions, consistent with shared social accountability raising the moral cost of default and reducing strategic default incentives.
novelty_rationale: Applies moral accountability and shared responsibility mechanisms from social psychology to strategic mortgage default, providing a household composition channel for default heterogeneity that complements existing financial option and social norm explanations.
nn_title: Macroeconomic Effects of Bankruptcy and Foreclosure Policies
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do carbon-intensive commercial real estate portfolios face higher costs of downside tail risk protection, and does this premium increase when climate policy receives heightened public attention?
method: Option market analysis examining put option pricing on REIT equities sorted by portfolio-level carbon intensity, with event-study tests around major climate policy announcements and IPCC report releases.
data: REIT carbon emissions data from GRESB and CDP combined with OptionMetrics for daily option pricing data and Google Trends for climate attention measures, covering 2010–2023.
key_finding: Carbon-intensive REITs likely face 15–30% higher put option premia, with premia rising sharply around salient climate events, mirroring the carbon tail-risk premium documented in equity markets.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the carbon tail-risk option pricing design from Hsu et al. (2023) to REITs, testing whether the real asset underpinning of REIT portfolios amplifies or dampens the carbon tail-risk premium relative to ordinary equities.
nn_title: Carbon Tail Risk
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How do abnormally warm local temperatures affect homebuyers' search intensity, housing sentiment, and transaction prices in residential real estate markets?
method: Panel regression linking county-level temperature anomalies to Zillow/Redfin search-volume indices, Google Trends housing queries, and hedonic transaction prices, with placebo tests using cold anomalies.
data: NOAA daily temperature station data merged with Zillow Research transaction-level data and Google Trends housing search indices for all U.S. counties, 2010–2023.
key_finding: Warm anomalies plausibly increase immediate housing search and short-run prices by 0.5–2%, with stronger effects in cold-climate metros, analogous to the attention effects of temperature on climate beliefs documented in Choi et al.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the local-temperature-attention design from behavioral finance to housing markets, testing whether salient physical climate experiences shift housing demand and willingness-to-pay in real time rather than through long-run risk capitalization.
nn_title: Hot and Cold Seasons in the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does housing market exposure during childhood causally affect the long-run wealth accumulation, homeownership, and financial portfolio choices of adults who grew up in different local housing cycles?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting cohort-level variation in local house-price growth during ages 0–18, instrumenting local prices with Saiz supply elasticity interacted with national interest rate cycles.
data: Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) linked to childhood county FHFA house price indices and Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances for adult financial outcomes, 1970–2020.
key_finding: Growing up in high-appreciation markets likely raises adult homeownership rates by 3–6 percentage points but reduces financial portfolio diversification, consistent with the long-run neighborhood effects literature.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the public-housing-demolition long-run outcomes design from Chetty et al. to the universal housing market, estimating causal scarring or enrichment effects of childhood housing environment on lifetime wealth trajectories.
nn_title: House price growth when children are teenagers: A path to higher earnings?
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Do households' home price expectations exhibit asymmetric effects on consumption—with rising expectations increasing spending for homeowners but contracting it for renters—consistent with a field-experiment assignment of beliefs?
method: Instrumental variables strategy using cross-sectional variation in local Zillow Home Value Forecast exposure and newspaper sentiment indices as exogenous shifters of household price expectations, linked to consumption outcomes.
data: Consumer Expenditure Survey linked to Zillow MSA-level forecasts and local newspaper housing sentiment indices, 2010–2022, distinguishing homeowner and renter subsamples.
key_finding: Homeowners' consumption responses to expected price growth are likely small and symmetric due to offsetting wealth-cost effects, while renters reduce consumption when expectations rise, replicating the field-experiment pattern of Fuster et al.
novelty_rationale: Brings the home-price-expectations field-experiment logic to observational panel data using quasi-experimental variation in expectation shifters, testing the homeowner/renter asymmetry on consumption at scale.
nn_title: Home Price Expectations and Spending: Evidence from a Field Experiment
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: What discount rate does the housing market embed for long-horizon flood and sea-level-rise risk, and does the term structure of these discount rates slope downward as climate risk models suggest?
method: Asset pricing model estimated from repeat-sales pairs of coastal versus inland properties across holding horizons of 1–30 years, extracting horizon-specific implicit discount rates for flood exposure.
data: CoreLogic repeat-sales transaction data for coastal U.S. counties merged with FEMA flood zone maps and NOAA sea-level-rise projections, covering 1990–2022.
key_finding: The housing market likely embeds a downward-sloping term structure of climate discount rates (higher for near-term physical events, lower for distant sea-level-rise scenarios), paralleling the 2.6% long-run rate Giglio et al. document in leasehold markets.
novelty_rationale: Extends the Giglio et al. leasehold-discount approach to flood risk specifically, using holding-horizon variation in repeat sales rather than lease-length variation to trace the term structure of climate discount rates in residential markets.
nn_title: Is the Risk of Sea Level Rise Capitalized in Residential Real Estate?
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do government-sponsored enterprise uniform mortgage pricing policies redistribute welfare across U.S. regions by subsidizing high-risk coastal climate zones at the expense of lower-risk inland borrowers?
method: Spatial model of collateralized borrowing calibrated to GSE guarantee fee uniformity, estimating regional welfare transfers implied by risk-adjusted counterfactual private pricing using Monte Carlo climate-adjusted default simulations.
data: Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac loan-level performance data combined with MSA-level climate risk scores from First Street Foundation and FEMA flood maps, covering 2000–2020.
key_finding: GSE uniform pricing plausibly generates $5–15 billion in annual implicit transfers from low-climate-risk inland regions to high-risk coastal regions, analogous to the regional redistribution documented in Hurst et al. for default risk.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the Hurst et al. regional mortgage redistribution spatial model to the climate risk dimension, quantifying the implicit climate cross-subsidy in GSE pricing before any climate-adjusted reform.
nn_title: Regional Redistribution through the US Mortgage Market
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: Does extreme heat exposure during pregnancy causally reduce birth weight in ways that are capitalized into residential property prices in affected neighborhoods through altered local demographics?
method: Two-stage design: first estimate heat-birth-weight effects using county-year variation in gestational heat exposure; second, use predicted neighborhood birth-weight changes as an instrument for demographic shifts and hedonic price effects.
data: Vital Statistics birth records with county of residence linked to NOAA temperature data and Zillow transaction prices, 2000–2020, focusing on Sun Belt metros.
key_finding: Neighborhoods with persistent gestational heat exposure likely show 0.5–2% price discounts after controlling for amenities, reflecting both direct disamenity and compositional demographic effects on local school quality.
novelty_rationale: Chains the climate-birth-weight mechanism from Deschênes et al. to the housing market, testing whether the downstream human capital effects of climate shocks are ultimately capitalized into residential property values.
nn_title: Estimating location premia using fixed effects—A semi‐local approach
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do housing voucher recipients reduce labor supply in ways that interact with local rental market tightness, amplifying or dampening the labor supply disincentive in high-cost versus low-cost housing markets?
method: Heterogeneous treatment effects analysis of the Moving to Opportunity experiment and Section 8 lottery data, exploiting variation in local rent-to-income ratios at the destination market to estimate market-tightness moderation of labor supply effects.
data: HUD's Moving to Opportunity experimental data and HUD administrative voucher records linked to IRS earnings records, stratified by destination MSA rent indices from HUD Fair Market Rents.
key_finding: Voucher-induced labor supply reductions are likely 2–3 times larger in tight rental markets where the subsidy value relative to earnings is highest, extending the Romich et al. voucher labor supply findings to a housing-market-equilibrium context.
novelty_rationale: Combines the randomized voucher labor-supply design from Jacob and Ludwig with housing market equilibrium conditions, testing whether local rental market tightness moderates the well-documented labor supply disincentive from in-kind housing transfers.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: Do life-cycle portfolio adjustments around house purchase—specifically, equity market exit and V-shaped reentry—vary systematically with local housing market cycle phases, creating procyclical financial fragility?
method: Dynamic event study of household financial portfolio composition in a 7-year window around house purchase, with difference-in-differences across purchases made in booming versus declining local markets using Saiz supply elasticity as an instrument.
data: Survey of Consumer Finances panel waves and Federal Reserve Flow of Funds linked to FHFA MSA price indices, supplemented by Swedish administrative household data where available.
key_finding: Households purchasing at market peaks likely show deeper and more prolonged equity market exit, with V-shaped recovery delayed by 1–2 additional years, amplifying wealth inequality between boom and bust cohorts.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the household portfolio V-shape design from Vestman et al. to housing market cycle variation, testing whether procyclical housing purchases create systematic financial fragility through portfolio crowding-out dynamics.
nn_title: Optimal Life Cycle Portfolio Choice with Housing Market Cycles
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Does federal crop insurance analog reasoning apply to government flood insurance—specifically, does NFIP coverage reduce homeowner adaptation investments and increase flood-zone housing values relative to uninsured comparable properties?
method: Difference-in-differences comparing flood adaptation investments (elevation certificates, retrofits) and house prices between NFIP-covered properties and comparable non-flood-zone properties, exploiting NFIP community enrollment discontinuities.
data: NFIP policy and claims data from FEMA linked to CoreLogic transaction records, FEMA elevation certificate database, and permit records for flood mitigation investments, 2000–2022.
key_finding: NFIP coverage likely reduces homeowner adaptation spending by 20–35% and inflates flood-zone property values by 3–7% relative to risk-adjusted benchmarks, mirroring the crop insurance adaptation disincentive from Barnett et al.
novelty_rationale: Directly transplants the federal crop insurance moral hazard and adaptation disincentive design to the NFIP, providing the first hedonic test of whether government flood insurance crowds out private adaptation investments in housing markets.
nn_title: Dutch dilemma: Housing prices and flood risk exposure
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do regional differences in climate risk create systematic heterogeneity in commercial mortgage credit spreads that is currently being cross-subsidized by uniform CMBS pricing?
method: Cross-sectional regression of CMBS loan spreads on property-level physical climate risk scores, controlling for traditional underwriting metrics, with counterfactual simulation of risk-adjusted spread schedules.
data: BlackRock Aladdin or RMS climate risk scores matched to the TREPP CMBS loan-level database covering $2+ trillion in commercial mortgages, 2010–2022.
key_finding: Climate-exposed commercial properties likely pay 5–25 basis points less than their risk-adjusted spread should imply, with cross-subsidies flowing from low-risk Midwest/Mountain properties to high-risk coastal and hurricane-exposed markets.
novelty_rationale: Applies the Hurst et al. spatial redistribution model to commercial mortgage markets, quantifying implicit climate cross-subsidies in CMBS pricing using the novel combination of climate risk scoring services with granular loan-level data.
nn_title: Pricing Credit Risk for Mortgages: Credit Risk Spreads and Heterogeneity across
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: How does heat exposure during critical phases of housing construction affect subsequent structural quality, building durability, and downstream default rates on construction loans?
method: Regression of building inspection failure rates, warranty claims, and downstream mortgage default on growing-degree-day heat exposure during the construction phase, using permit timing and NOAA weather data for exogenous variation.
data: U.S. Census Bureau Building Permits Survey linked to local weather station data, combined with FHFA/CoreLogic mortgage default records matched by vintage and county, 2000–2020.
key_finding: Properties constructed during high heat-stress periods likely show 5–15% higher inspection failure rates and 10–20% elevated downstream default rates, connecting climate shocks to building quality through a construction labor productivity channel.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the agricultural output and random weather fluctuation design from Schlenker and Roberts to residential construction quality, introducing a novel climate-real-estate channel that operates through physical production rather than demand or amenity.
nn_title: When climate meets real estate: A survey of the literature
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do institutional real estate investors who share common ownership across multiple markets exhibit coordinated rent-setting behavior that reduces competition and raises rents above competitive levels?
method: Panel regression of apartment rent growth on institutional common ownership measures (modified MHHI delta constructed from NCREIF/CoStar institutional ownership data), with IV using exogenous variation from pension fund asset allocation mandates.
data: CoStar commercial lease transaction data merged with NCREIF property ownership records and Preqin institutional investor data covering U.S. multifamily markets, 2005–2022.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in institutional common ownership likely raises apartment rents by 1–3%, with stronger effects in concentrated markets, mirroring the common-ownership competition reduction documented in airline and banking markets.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the Azar et al. common-ownership anticompetitive effects methodology from product markets to rental housing, exploiting the growing institutional landlord literature and providing new welfare implications for housing affordability policy.
nn_title: Institutional common ownership and firm value: Evidence from real estate investm
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do households exhibit present bias in mortgage refinancing decisions—specifically, do they exhibit hyperbolic discounting by delaying optimal refinancing despite clear financial gains, and does housing wealth moderate this bias?
method: Structural estimation of quasi-hyperbolic discounting parameters from observed refinancing delay distributions, using the Fannie Mae Mortgage Performance dataset to identify the share of suboptimal delay attributable to beta < 1 versus transaction costs.
data: Fannie Mae Single-Family Loan Performance dataset with monthly observation of refinancing decisions, linked to Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey rates and FHFA house price indices, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Present-biased households likely exhibit beta parameters of 0.7–0.85 implied by observed refinancing delays, with lower housing equity amplifying hyperbolic delay consistent with liquidity-constrained present bias documented in consumer finance.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the structural quasi-hyperbolic discounting estimation approach from consumption literature to mortgage refinancing, separating genuine time-inconsistency from rational inattention and transaction cost explanations for the well-documented refinancing puzzle.
nn_title: Home Ownership, Household Leverage and Hyperbolic Discounting
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does the willingness to pay to avoid violent crime in residential neighborhoods exhibit dynamic, forward-looking pricing that significantly exceeds myopic hedonic estimates, and has this gap widened with improved crime-information technology?
method: Dynamic hedonic pricing model with forward-looking household behavior estimated on repeat-sales data, comparing implicit crime-price elasticities pre- and post-widespread adoption of crime-mapping apps using a difference-in-differences on app launch dates.
data: CoreLogic repeat-sales transaction data merged with FBI Uniform Crime Reports and NeighborhoodScout/SpotCrime app adoption data across U.S. metros, 2005–2022.
key_finding: Dynamic willingness-to-pay for crime reduction likely exceeds static hedonic estimates by 30–50%, with the gap widening after crime app adoption as information frictions fall, parallel to the dynamic crime-WTP finding of Bishop and Murphy.
novelty_rationale: Applies the dynamic hedonic WTP model from environmental economics to crime-housing pricing, adding a novel information-technology dimension by testing whether digital crime-mapping tools shift the dynamic-static WTP gap.
nn_title: Estimating the Willingness to Pay to Avoid Violent Crime: A Dynamic Approach
assigned_label: Crime's economic effects on property and wages

research_question: Do households who grow up in public housing or voucher-assisted housing show differential long-run homeownership and wealth trajectories relative to demographically similar waitlist-lottery losers, and how does neighborhood quality mediate these effects?
method: Long-run follow-up study using HUD administrative records from housing assistance lotteries linked to IRS earnings, credit bureau, and CoreLogic ownership records to track 20-year homeownership and wealth outcomes.
data: HUD Moving to Opportunity experimental data and Chicago Housing Authority lottery records linked to IRS Form 1040 records, credit bureau files, and CoreLogic deed records through IRS data linkage agreements.
key_finding: Housing assistance recipients from higher-quality neighborhoods likely achieve homeownership rates 5–10 percentage points higher after 15 years, with neighborhood quality being a stronger mediator than tenure status itself, extending Chetty et al. to asset-building outcomes.
novelty_rationale: Applies the long-run public housing demolition cohort design from Chyn to homeownership and wealth-building outcomes, the first randomized study to trace causal pathways from assisted housing receipt to intergenerational real estate wealth accumulation.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Do optimal life-cycle housing portfolio choices change fundamentally when house price cycles exhibit persistent serial correlation, and do households caught in upswing cycles rationally over-lever compared to those entering in downswings?
method: Estimated life-cycle model with endogenous rent-versus-own and leverage decisions calibrated to MSA-level house price autocorrelation estimates, tested against PSID household-level data on leverage choices across entry cohorts.
data: PSID household-level data on homeownership, LTV ratios, and mortgage choices merged with FHFA MSA quarterly price indices for entry-cohort cycle phase classification, 1970–2020.
key_finding: Households entering housing markets during upswing phases likely accept 8–12 percentage points higher LTV ratios than fundamentally justified, consistent with the speculative motive from Yao and Zhang's life-cycle model with serially correlated prices.
novelty_rationale: Applies the optimal life-cycle portfolio choice model with housing cycles from Yao and Zhang to empirical leverage decisions, providing the first direct test of whether observed household leverage patterns match the model's speculative motive predictions.
nn_title: Optimal Life Cycle Portfolio Choice with Housing Market Cycles
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Can defensive investments in energy efficiency—analogous to pharmaceutical purchases in air quality research—account for a substantial share of the total willingness to pay for green building certification?
method: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered adoption of ENERGY STAR and LEED certification in commercial buildings, decomposing rent and value premia into direct certification effects versus operating cost (defensive investment) savings.
data: CoStar commercial lease transaction data merged with EPA ENERGY STAR certification records and CBECS energy use data, supplemented by utility bill data from DOE Building Performance Database, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Defensive investment (energy cost) savings likely account for 30–50% of the green building rent premium, with the remainder attributable to certification signaling and productivity amenity, paralleling Deschênes et al.'s finding that defensive investments constitute over one-third of air quality WTP.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the defensive investment decomposition framework from the air quality literature to commercial real estate green certification, separating the 'insurance value' of energy cost savings from the pure amenity/signaling value of green labels.
nn_title: Green Noise or Green Value? Measuring the Effects of Environmental Certification
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does housing market tightness—measured by listing inventory and days-on-market—affect climate risk capitalization, with tight markets under-pricing flood and wildfire risk relative to slack markets where buyers have more search time?
method: Panel regression of climate risk discount (first-sale versus resale price gap for flood/fire-zone properties) on local housing market tightness measures, exploiting business-cycle variation in inventory as exogenous variation in buyer search time.
data: Zillow/Realtor.com inventory and days-on-market data merged with FEMA flood zone maps, CAL FIRE wildfire hazard zone data, and CoreLogic transaction prices for U.S. metros, 2005–2022.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in market tightness likely reduces flood risk capitalization by 1.5–3%, consistent with rational inattention models where time-constrained buyers underweight probabilistic hazard information.
novelty_rationale: Combines the hot-and-cold housing market search framework with climate risk capitalization, generating the novel prediction that tight markets systematically underprice physical climate risk through a rational search-cost-attention mechanism.
nn_title: When climate meets real estate: A survey of the literature
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does municipal climate adaptation investment—such as seawall construction and managed retreat programs—generate capitalization effects in nearby property markets, and does the form of financing (special districts vs. general obligation bonds) affect the distribution of these benefits?
method: Regression discontinuity and difference-in-differences exploiting the spatial boundaries of municipal adaptation investment zones and the timing of bond referenda, comparing price effects within versus outside protected areas by financing mechanism.
data: CoreLogic transaction data for coastal U.S. municipalities merged with FEMA Hazard Mitigation Grant data, municipal bond records from MSRB, and Army Corps of Engineers coastal protection project databases, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Seawall and managed retreat investments likely increase nearby property values by 5–15%, but general-obligation financing redistributes costs to unprotected inland properties while special district financing concentrates both benefits and costs among protected parcels.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the special district financing capitalization design from Cheung to climate adaptation infrastructure, providing the first empirical test of how municipal adaptation investment financing mechanisms affect the geographic distribution of climate risk reduction benefits.
nn_title: Capitalization of interjurisdictional fiscal differentials: An alternative appro
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do high-income households shift from growth to value REIT investment strategies as they age and improve their balance sheets, analogous to life-cycle equity portfolio tilts documented in household finance?
method: Empirical analysis of REIT investor portfolio tilts (value vs. growth REITs defined by price-to-NAV ratios) across investor age and balance sheet quality using administrative brokerage data, with regression analysis controlling for market conditions.
data: Vanguard or Fidelity administrative brokerage data on individual REIT fund holdings (available through academic partnerships) linked to investor age and account wealth, 2005–2020.
key_finding: Older investors with stronger balance sheets likely tilt 15–25% more toward value REITs, mirroring the equity market result of Betermier et al. and suggesting life-cycle risk management operates within asset classes as well as across them.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the Betermier et al. who-are-value-investors design from equity markets to REITs, testing whether the same life-cycle balance-sheet mechanism drives within-real-estate investment style differences.
nn_title: On the Portfolio Properties of Real Estate in Good Times and Bad Times1
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does limited housing market participation among renters relative to homeowners reflect preference heterogeneity in savings motives rather than credit constraints, as evidenced by difference-in-differences around quasi-random homeownership shocks?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis of housing wealth and financial portfolio choices around quasi-random homeownership shocks from policy changes in down payment assistance programs, separating preference-based from constraint-based non-participation.
data: PSID household panel data merged with IRS tax records and state down payment assistance program records, with geographic variation in program availability as an instrument for homeownership transitions, 2000–2020.
key_finding: Renters who transition to homeownership via down payment assistance show minimal reduction in risky financial asset holdings, supporting the preference-heterogeneity interpretation from Vestman et al. over the crowding-out hypothesis.
novelty_rationale: Applies the Vestman et al. limited stock market participation design—distinguishing preference from constraint explanations—to housing market participation, using quasi-random homeownership shocks to causally test whether tenure choice reflects underlying savings preferences.
nn_title: Limited Stock Market Participation Among Renters and Homeowners
assigned_label: Household Housing Decisions and Wealth

research_question: Do public housing demolitions in U.S. cities generate positive externalities on nearby private housing markets that offset the fiscal cost of relocation assistance, and does this externality vary with the initial level of concentrated poverty?
method: Difference-in-differences estimating spillover effects on nearby private residential property values around public housing demolition events, using the timing and location of HOPE VI demolitions as exogenous variation, stratified by initial poverty concentration.
data: HUD HOPE VI demolition records linked to CoreLogic transaction data and Census tract poverty rates, covering all U.S. HOPE VI demolitions from 1993 to 2010 with pre/post price trajectories.
key_finding: Demolitions of severely distressed public housing likely generate positive externality price effects of 5–15% within 0.5 miles, but only when initial poverty concentration exceeds 40%, consistent with threshold effects from the Chyn neighborhood-scarring literature.
novelty_rationale: Extends the Chyn long-run outcomes framework to the externality channel, testing whether the private housing market spillovers from demolition are large enough to justify relocation assistance on fiscal grounds—a direct policy-cost-benefit connection not previously made.
nn_title: Blowing it up and knocking it down: The local and city-wide effects of demolishi
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Does segregation in residential real estate markets—proxied by racial composition discontinuities at neighborhood boundaries—generate measurable economic costs in the form of elevated mortgage default rates and reduced housing market liquidity at racial interface zones?
method: Regression discontinuity comparing mortgage default rates and days-on-market on either side of sharp racial composition boundaries identified algorithmically from Census block data, with placebo tests using non-racial demographic boundaries.
data: HMDA loan-level origination and performance data merged with CoreLogic transaction records and Census block-level racial composition data, covering all U.S. metro areas 2000–2020.
key_finding: Properties at racial interface boundaries likely experience 15–25% longer marketing times and 10–20% higher default rates relative to interior neighborhood comparables, quantifying direct economic costs of residential segregation beyond previously documented price-level effects.
novelty_rationale: Adapts the regression discontinuity design from redlining boundary studies to quantify the ongoing economic costs of residential segregation through mortgage performance and liquidity channels, connecting the segregation-conflict literature from Iyer et al. to housing market outcomes.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Do firms located near climate-exposed real estate assets face elevated equity tail risk premia—specifically, are their put option premia systematically higher—and does this effect operate through collateral value channels or direct operational disruption channels?
method: Option market analysis of put premia for publicly listed firms sorted by owned real estate climate exposure, with channel decomposition using balance sheet data on collateral-intensive versus operationally-dependent real estate holdings.
data: Compustat corporate real estate holdings data merged with First Street Foundation climate risk scores, OptionMetrics daily options data, and FEMA disaster declarations, 2010–2023.
key_finding: Firms with climate-exposed real estate collateral likely face 10–25 basis point higher annualized put premia, with the effect concentrated in collateral-intensive firms, extending the carbon tail-risk option design to physical climate risk in the real asset base.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the Hsu et al. carbon tail-risk option pricing approach to physical climate risk in corporate real estate holdings, distinguishing collateral-channel from operational-disruption-channel mechanisms for the first time using option market data.
nn_title: Carbon Tail Risk
assigned_label: Climate Risk and Financial Markets

research_question: Do value-oriented homebuyers (purchasing below-median price-to-rent ratio properties) outperform growth-oriented buyers over the subsequent decade, and does this value premium vary with buyer age and balance-sheet strength?
method: Life-cycle portfolio analysis linking buyer characteristics from HMDA and deed records to subsequent appreciation outcomes at the zip-code level, with Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional regressions decomposing returns into value versus growth components across buyer cohorts stratified by age and leverage
data: HMDA loan-level origination data merged with CoreLogic deed transfer records and FHFA zip-code-level house price indices covering 2000–2020 across U.S. metropolitan areas
key_finding: Value properties (low price-to-rent) may outperform growth properties by 2–4% annually on a risk-adjusted basis, with the premium larger for middle-aged buyers whose balance sheets are strongest, consistent with risk-based explanations for value investing
novelty_rationale: Transplants the 'Who Are the Value and Growth Investors?' life-cycle framework from equities to housing, asking whether the same age-and-balance-sheet gradient in value tilts operates in direct real estate markets where transaction costs and illiquidity interact with investor horizon.
nn_title: Value versus Growth International Real Estate Investment
assigned_label: Real Estate Return Measurement and Volatility Estimation
