research_question: Does a uniform national mortgage pricing policy by the GSEs—charging identical guarantee fees regardless of local climate flood risk—create regional welfare redistribution analogous to the cross-subsidies identified in GSE uniform pricing across default risk regions?
method: Spatial model of mortgage guarantee fee incidence calibrated to county-level expected annual flood loss from FEMA NFIP data, comparing actuarially fair flood-risk-adjusted fees to observed uniform GSE fees and computing implicit transfer amounts across climate-exposed and climate-safe counties.
data: FEMA National Flood Insurance Program policy and claims data (2000–2022), Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae single-family loan-level datasets, and CoreLogic property location data matched to FEMA Special Flood Hazard Areas.
key_finding: GSE uniform guarantee fees likely generate annual transfers on the order of hundreds of millions of dollars from inland low-risk counties to coastal high-risk counties, with redistribution magnitudes varying significantly with climate scenario assumptions.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the regional redistribution via uniform GSE pricing mechanism to the climate risk dimension, quantifying an unexplored fiscal incidence channel of federal mortgage market intervention.
nn_title: Climate risk in mortgage markets: Evidence from Hurricanes Harvey and Irma
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do abnormally warm local temperatures cause homeowners to list properties for sale at higher rates, revealing revealed-preference evidence that heat stress accelerates residential mobility decisions?
method: Panel regression of weekly new listing counts from Zillow listing data on county-level temperature anomalies (deviations from 30-year normals from NOAA), with county and week-of-year fixed effects, instrumented by exogenous atmospheric pressure patterns to address endogeneity of local temperatures.
data: Zillow Research new listings data at the county-week level (2012–2023), NOAA daily temperature station data aggregated to counties, and NCEP atmospheric pressure reanalysis data.
key_finding: A one standard deviation increase in summer temperature anomaly may increase new listing rates by 2–5%, consistent with heat disamenity accelerating departure from heat-exposed markets, with larger effects in inland metropolitan areas without coastal cooling.
novelty_rationale: Applies the attention-to-climate revealed-preference framework from financial markets to the housing supply margin, testing whether heat stress generates measurable mobility responses distinct from amenity capitalization studies.
nn_title: Do House Price Levels Anticipate Subsequent Price Changes within Metropolitan Ar
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do farm mortgage lenders—community banks with concentrated agricultural real estate exposure—tighten residential mortgage credit in nearby rural towns during droughts, transmitting climate shocks from agricultural land markets to local housing credit availability?
method: Difference-in-differences comparing residential mortgage origination volumes and approval rates in HMDA data for counties served primarily by agricultural community banks versus counties served by diversified lenders, exploiting county-level PDSI drought index variation as the climate treatment.
data: FFIEC Home Mortgage Disclosure Act data (2000–2020), FDIC call reports identifying bank agricultural loan concentrations, NOAA Palmer Drought Severity Index at the county level.
key_finding: Residential mortgage originations in rural counties served by agriculture-concentrated lenders likely decline 8–15% during severe drought years relative to similarly rural counties served by diversified lenders, revealing a novel climate-housing credit transmission channel.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the agricultural climate shock transmission literature to housing credit markets, identifying a previously unmeasured channel through which farm sector distress propagates to rural residential real estate.
nn_title: Expression of Concern: Mortgage Finance and Climate Change: Securitization Dynam
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does providing homebuyers with expert long-run house price growth forecasts—exogenously varied in a field experiment—differentially affect purchase decisions and mortgage leverage among renters versus owners, paralleling the asymmetric spending response to home price expectations found in consumer finance experiments?
method: Survey-embedded field experiment with random assignment of optimistic, pessimistic, and baseline expert house price forecasts to recent homebuyers recruited from Redfin and Zillow user panels, measuring intended and realized leverage choices, purchase timing, and down payment amounts.
data: Original survey data collected from verified recent homebuyers and active home searchers in 10 major MSAs (2024–2025), linked to deed records for realized transaction outcomes.
key_finding: Renters shown optimistic forecasts likely increase target leverage and accelerate purchase timing substantially more than owners shown the same information, with pessimistic forecasts causing greater search exit among renters, consistent with asymmetric option-value responses.
novelty_rationale: Directly replicates the home price expectations field experiment in a purchase-decision rather than spending context, testing whether the wealth-income offset that makes owners inelastic to expectations also attenuates renter purchase behavior.
nn_title: Home Price Expectations and Spending: Evidence from a Field Experiment
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Do value-oriented commercial real estate investors—those acquiring properties at below-replacement-cost prices—outperform growth-oriented investors on a risk-adjusted basis in private markets, and does this value premium reflect behavioral mispricing or rational compensation for illiquidity risk?
method: Portfolio-level holding-period return analysis using NCREIF Transaction Based Index data, classifying acquisitions as value or growth based on cap rate relative to market at purchase, then applying Fama-French-style factor models adapted for private real estate using FTSE NAREIT factors as systematic risk controls.
data: NCREIF Property Index transaction-level data (1990–2022) with acquisition price and cap rate information, supplemented by CoStar sales comparables for property-type and market benchmarks.
key_finding: Value commercial real estate portfolios may outperform growth portfolios by 150–250 basis points annually on a risk-adjusted basis, with the premium largest for retail and office properties and smallest for industrial, and unexplained by time-varying systematic risk.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the international value-versus-growth real estate framework rigorously to U.S. private commercial markets using transaction-based rather than appraisal-based returns, resolving the smoothing confound in prior work.
nn_title: Value versus Growth International Real Estate Investment
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does public housing demolition in Chicago generate measurable positive externalities on nearby private residential property values and tenant labor market outcomes, and do these effects operate through neighborhood composition changes or direct amenity improvements?
method: Quasi-experimental comparison of housing transaction prices and earnings trajectories for residents in census tracts adjacent to demolished CHA projects versus nearby non-demolished projects, using variation in demolition timing across the Plan for Transformation as the identifying instrument.
data: Illinois Department of Employment Security earnings records linked to CHA tenant relocation records, Cook County Assessor property transaction data (1995–2015), and CHA demolition chronology from public records.
key_finding: Properties within 500 meters of demolished public housing sites likely appreciate 5–12% over five years post-demolition, with effects concentrated in blocks where demolition reduced visible blight, while former residents exhibit modest labor market improvements dependent on destination neighborhood quality.
novelty_rationale: Directly applies the Moved to Opportunity quasi-experimental framework to both the property value externality and tenant labor market channels simultaneously, using a cleaner identification strategy than prior demolition studies.
nn_title: Blowing it up and knocking it down: The local and city-wide effects of demolishi
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the long-run social cost of housing supply restrictions—measured as cumulative welfare losses from inflated rents, reduced labor mobility, and misallocated consumption—exhibit fat-tail properties that make the expected cost substantially larger than point estimates suggest?
method: Monte Carlo simulation calibrating a dynamic general equilibrium housing market model with uncertain supply elasticity, rent growth, and mobility parameters drawn from the empirical distribution of estimates in the meta-analytic literature, computing the full distribution of welfare losses from land use regulation across parameter draws.
data: Meta-analytic compilation of published supply elasticity estimates (Saiz 2010; Gyourko et al. 2019; Hsieh and Moretti 2019), calibrated using ACS housing cost and mobility data and BLS wage premium estimates across regulated and unregulated markets.
key_finding: The distribution of estimated welfare costs from supply restrictions likely exhibits positive skewness with a heavy right tail, implying that the mean social cost exceeds the median by 40–80%, analogous to fat-tail results in climate damage economics.
novelty_rationale: Imports the fat-tail social cost methodology from climate economics to quantify parameter uncertainty in housing supply restriction welfare analysis, challenging the practice of reporting only point-estimate costs of zoning.
nn_title: Housing supply and housing bubbles
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Do households who receive housing vouchers and relocate to opportunity areas exhibit reduced labor force participation and earnings—analogous to the documented labor supply disincentive from housing assistance—and does this effect vary with neighborhood employment accessibility?
method: Instrumental variable analysis using voucher lottery wait-list outcomes in multiple public housing authorities, comparing labor force participation and quarterly earnings trajectories for lottery winners and losers at two and five years post-lottery, with heterogeneous treatment effects estimated by destination neighborhood job accessibility index.
data: HUD Moving to Work agency administrative data on voucher lottery outcomes linked to SSA earnings records and IRS tax filings, combined with EPA Smart Location Database walkability and job accessibility measures at the census tract level.
key_finding: Voucher recipients who relocate to high-opportunity, low-transit-access suburban areas may experience larger labor supply reductions than those relocating to transit-rich neighborhoods, suggesting job access mediates the labor supply disincentive documented in prior lottery studies.
novelty_rationale: Extends the housing voucher labor supply experiment to test whether transit and job accessibility heterogeneity explains the labor supply disincentive, a mechanism unexplored in the existing voucher lottery literature.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: Do commercial real estate markets in cities with high carbon transition risk—measured by the share of employment in carbon-intensive industries—exhibit systematically lower cap rates and higher vacancy rates as investors price stranded asset risk into local property fundamentals?
method: Cross-sectional panel regression of CBRE cap rate and CoStar vacancy data for office and industrial properties across 60 U.S. MSAs on a carbon transition exposure index constructed from BLS industry employment shares weighted by sectoral carbon intensity from EPA greenhouse gas reporting data, with controls for population growth, income, and interest rates.
data: CBRE Cap Rate Survey (2010–2022), CoStar vacancy and asking rent data, EPA Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program facility-level emissions linked to county employment from BLS QCEW, and CRSP equity returns for locally headquartered firms.
key_finding: Office cap rates in high carbon-transition-risk MSAs may be 40–80 basis points higher than in comparable low-risk markets after controlling for fundamentals, with industrial properties showing the reverse as logistics demand offsets transition risk from manufacturing decline.
novelty_rationale: Applies the global carbon transition risk pricing framework from equity markets to private commercial real estate fundamentals, testing whether private market cap rates incorporate transition risk that listed REIT valuations have been shown to partially reflect.
nn_title: Commercial real estate and air pollution
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do institutional real estate investors with greater portfolio exposure to physically climate-exposed markets reduce acquisition activity in those markets following salient extreme weather events, analogous to institutional investors reducing ownership of locally shocked firms during COVID-19?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis comparing NCREIF institutional investor acquisition and disposition volumes in markets directly hit by major hurricane or wildfire events versus geographically matched control markets, exploiting variation in investor headquarters location and pre-event portfolio concentration as cross-sectional moderators.
data: NCREIF Property Index transaction-level data with buyer and seller institution identifiers (2000–2022), NOAA storm track and USFS wildfire perimeter data, and TREPP CMBS origination data for deal-level geographic exposure.
key_finding: Institutional investors headquartered in coastal cities with local portfolio exposure to hurricane-struck markets likely reduce acquisition activity by 20–35% in affected markets for two to three years post-event, with effects concentrated among investors with concentrated rather than diversified geographic exposure.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the COVID-19 institutional investor local shock response framework to climate event shocks in private real estate, testing whether physical climate events trigger portfolio rebalancing analogous to financial crisis responses.
nn_title: Climate change and commercial real estate: Evidence from Hurricane Sandy
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does residential segregation measured by the dissimilarity index causally affect mortgage delinquency rates at the MSA level, operating through reduced economic integration and limited employment network effects for minority borrowers, rather than purely individual credit characteristics?
method: Instrumental variables regression of MSA-level FHA mortgage delinquency rates on the racial dissimilarity index, instrumenting segregation with historical 1940 railroad network topology following the standard historical instrument approach, with controls for median income, unemployment, and local house price growth.
data: HMDA loan-level origination and performance data aggregated to MSA level (2000–2019), Census Bureau American Community Survey segregation measures, FHFA House Price Index, and historical railroad network GIS data from Nardis and Nardis (2007).
key_finding: A one standard deviation increase in residential segregation may raise MSA-level FHA mortgage delinquency rates by 15–25 basis points after controlling for credit characteristics, consistent with segregation limiting employment network access and income stability for minority borrowers.
novelty_rationale: Brings causal identification from the segregation-riot economics literature to mortgage performance, testing whether neighborhood-level economic integration—not just individual creditworthiness—predicts portfolio-level default risk.
nn_title: Residential segregation and interracial economic disparities: A simultaneous-equ
assigned_label: Racial residential segregation patterns and causes

research_question: Do private real estate fund managers who engage in personal home purchases or refinancings during active deal periods exhibit worse fund performance and higher risk-taking in their professional portfolios, analogous to documented productivity losses among hedge fund managers during personal real estate transactions?
method: Matched panel analysis linking real estate private equity fund manager personal mortgage origination records from HMDA (identified via name and address matching to fund manager biographical data) with fund-quarter performance data from Preqin, using transaction timing to construct a personal real estate activity indicator.
data: Preqin private real estate fund performance data (2000–2020), HMDA mortgage origination records, and Hamilton Lane fund manager biographical databases, with personal transactions identified through name-address matching algorithms.
key_finding: Fund managers who originate personal mortgages during active acquisition periods likely exhibit 80–150 basis point lower fund IRRs in the subsequent two to four quarters and increase portfolio leverage, consistent with divided attention and behavioral bias spillovers from personal financial stress.
novelty_rationale: Directly replicates the hedge fund manager personal real estate distraction study in the private equity real estate context, where the mechanism may be stronger given the active management nature of value-add and opportunistic strategies.
nn_title: How do Personal Real Estate Transactions Affect Productivity and Risk Taking? Ev
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does income segregation across housing submarkets within U.S. metropolitan areas—measured by the between-submarket variance in median household income—generate negative externalities on property tax revenue adequacy and local public school quality, creating self-reinforcing fiscal cycles?
method: Panel regression of district-level per-pupil school funding and standardized test scores on a within-MSA income segregation index constructed from ACS census tract data, using state school finance reform legislation as an instrument for property-tax-based funding variation, with MSA and year fixed effects.
data: Census Bureau American Community Survey (2005–2019) census tract income data, Stanford Education Data Archive district-level test scores, Census of Governments school finance data, and Stanford Law School's Education Finance Litigation Database for reform timing.
key_finding: Higher within-MSA income segregation likely reduces average district per-pupil funding by $300–$600 annually and lowers math proficiency rates by 2–4 percentage points, with effects concentrated in districts serving below-median-income neighborhoods where property tax bases are thin.
novelty_rationale: Connects the housing submarket segregation literature to fiscal externalities through a panel identification strategy, quantifying the capitalization feedback from segregated housing markets to public school quality in a causal framework.
nn_title: School finance, spatial income segregation, and the nature of communities
assigned_label: School quality capitalization into housing prices

research_question: Do green building certification requirements—analogous to energy efficiency mandates analyzed in the climate policy literature—generate carbon leakage by shifting high-energy-use tenants from certified to non-certified buildings within the same market, without reducing aggregate commercial building emissions?
method: Difference-in-differences analysis comparing EPA ENERGY STAR scores and estimated energy consumption of buildings in jurisdictions that adopted mandatory benchmarking and disclosure laws versus synthetic control jurisdictions, testing whether tenant relocation to non-certified stock offsets certified building emission reductions.
data: EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager building-level energy data (2008–2022), CBECS Commercial Buildings Energy Consumption Survey, CoStar building characteristics and tenant industry data, and DOE Building Energy Codes Program legislative database for disclosure law adoption timing.
key_finding: Mandatory benchmarking adoption may reduce certified building energy intensity by 8–15% while increasing energy consumption in the non-certified stock by 4–8%, consistent with partial carbon leakage, with net market-level emission reductions significantly smaller than certified building effects suggest.
novelty_rationale: Imports the vertical targeting and leakage framework from climate policy into commercial real estate energy regulation, testing whether building-level green mandates achieve market-level emission reductions or merely shift consumption across the stock.
nn_title: On the Value of Environmental Certification in the Commercial Real Estate Market
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do households in U.S. states with more generous bankruptcy homestead exemptions exhibit lower willingness to pay for flood zone properties—as revealed by hedonic price discontinuities at FEMA flood zone boundaries—because exemptions provide an alternative form of downside risk protection?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area boundaries, estimating the flood zone price discount separately for each state and regressing the estimated discount on state homestead exemption generosity, controlling for state-level flood exposure, housing supply elasticity, and income.
data: Zillow transaction-level data (2010–2020) matched to FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer parcel-level flood zone designations, NFIP insurance take-up rates from FEMA, and state homestead exemption dollar limits from the Consumer Bankruptcy Project.
key_finding: States with higher homestead exemptions may exhibit 20–40% smaller flood zone price discounts at FEMA boundaries, consistent with households treating bankruptcy protection as a partial substitute for flood risk hedging, particularly in lower-income markets with high NFIP non-participation.
novelty_rationale: Combines the bankruptcy exemption housing literature with flood risk capitalization research to test whether public risk-sharing institutions substitute for private hedging in housing markets, a novel interaction unexplored in either strand.
nn_title: Who Bears Flood Risk? Evidence from Mortgage Markets in Florida
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does the announcement of major carbon capture and storage facility siting decisions generate house price effects in nearby residential markets, with communities perceiving industrial risk disamenities outweighing local employment benefits?
method: Event study difference-in-differences comparing residential property transaction prices within 5 miles of announced and approved CCS facility sites to matched control areas in the same county, using the staggered timing of DOE-funded CCS project announcements as treatment events.
data: Zillow and CoreLogic residential transaction data (2005–2022), DOE National Energy Technology Laboratory CCS project database with announcement dates and facility GPS coordinates, and EPA Risk Management Plan data for comparable industrial facility comparisons.
key_finding: CCS facility announcements may generate house price declines of 3–8% within 1 mile of planned sites, with effects diminishing beyond 3 miles, and positive employment effects potentially reversing the discount in labor-scarce rural markets.
novelty_rationale: Applies the event study negative externality methodology to a climate mitigation infrastructure that has received no real estate economics attention, addressing an increasingly policy-relevant siting question as CCS deployment accelerates.
nn_title: Does Climate Change Affect Real Estate Prices? Only If You Believe In It
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: Do mortgage lenders optimally set stochastic mortgage insurance premiums—analogous to risk-adjusted carbon price frameworks—by incorporating tail risk in local house price distributions, and does failure to do so create systematic cross-subsidies from low-variance to high-variance markets?
method: Construction of actuarially fair mortgage insurance premiums using Monte Carlo simulation of local house price return distributions estimated from Zillow repeat-sales indexes, comparing simulated risk-adjusted premiums to actual FHA mortgage insurance premiums and calculating regional cross-subsidies.
data: FHA Single Family Loan Level Dataset (2000–2020), Zillow Research house price indexes at the MSA level (1996–2022), FHFA House Price Index for volatility calibration, and HUD actuarial reviews of the Mutual Mortgage Insurance Fund.
key_finding: Actuarially fair FHA premiums likely vary from 30 basis points in low-volatility Midwest markets to over 150 basis points in high-volatility coastal markets, implying annual cross-subsidies of several billion dollars from Midwest to coastal borrowers under uniform premium structures.
novelty_rationale: Applies the risk-adjusted social cost methodology from climate economics to mortgage insurance pricing, quantifying for the first time the full regional cross-subsidy structure embedded in FHA's uniform premium policy.
nn_title: Pricing Credit Risk for Mortgages: Credit Risk Spreads and Heterogeneity across
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: Do households in communities targeted by place-based green infrastructure investments—urban tree canopy programs, permeable pavement, bioswales—exhibit higher residential mobility and faster neighborhood income growth, consistent with environmental gentrification rather than broad-based amenity provision?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting variation in EPA Green Infrastructure grant timing and location across census tracts in 20 major cities, comparing tract-level median household income, racial composition, and housing transaction prices before and after investment, with triple-difference tests for renter versus owner-occupier outcomes.
data: EPA Green Infrastructure grant database with project geocoordinates and completion dates, Census Bureau ACS five-year estimates at tract level (2005–2021), Zillow transaction-level data, and HUD CHAS housing affordability data.
key_finding: Green infrastructure investments may increase nearby median household income by 3–7% over five years, with the effect concentrated in already-appreciating neighborhoods, while low-income census tracts see smaller amenity capitalization and higher displacement rates among renters.
novelty_rationale: Imports the defensive investment—direct benefit decomposition framework from environmental economics to test whether green infrastructure generates genuine broad amenity gains or primarily redistributes housing access through capitalization-driven displacement.
nn_title: The price effects of greening vacant lots: How neighborhood attributes matter
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the stochastic risk of thermohaline circulation disruption—proxied by Gulf Stream variability metrics—affect coastal real estate markets in the U.S. Northeast through temperature uncertainty channels distinct from direct sea-level-rise risk?
method: Panel regression of coastal MSA house price growth on NOAA Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation strength indices lagged one and two years, controlling for actual sea surface temperature anomalies, FEMA flood zone coverage, and local income growth, with heterogeneous effects estimated by coastal elevation vulnerability.
data: NOAA RAPID AMOC monitoring data (2004–2022), Zillow MSA-level house price indexes, NOAA sea surface temperature anomaly data, FEMA SFHA coverage ratios from the National Flood Hazard Layer, and USGS coastal elevation data.
key_finding: AMOC weakening signals may exhibit modest but statistically detectable negative associations with Northeast coastal house price growth 12–18 months later, consistent with sophisticated investor updating on long-run climate risk channels beyond headline sea-level projections.
novelty_rationale: Introduces thermohaline circulation dynamics—a catastrophic climate tipping point—as a novel risk variable in coastal real estate pricing, testing whether private markets respond to low-probability, high-impact oceanographic signals distinct from current flood risk metrics.
nn_title: Is the Risk of Sea Level Rise Capitalized in Residential Real Estate?
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do housing markets exhibit opt-in adaptation to climate risk analogous to the voluntary development restriction framework—where homeowners in high-flood-risk zones who voluntarily purchase elevation certificates receive measurable price premiums that reduce adverse selection in the NFIP?
method: Hedonic regression comparing sale prices of properties with and without voluntarily obtained FEMA elevation certificates in the same flood zone, using instrumental variables based on local surveying firm density and cost to address selection into certification, estimating the implicit market price of voluntary flood risk disclosure.
data: FEMA elevation certificate database matched to CoreLogic property transaction records in Florida, Texas, and Louisiana (2005–2020), NFIP policy and claims data at the parcel level, and local surveying firm density from Census County Business Patterns.
key_finding: Voluntarily certified properties may command a 3–7% price premium over uncertified properties in the same flood zone, with premiums larger in markets with high NFIP non-participation, suggesting voluntary disclosure reduces information asymmetry and buyer risk compensation.
novelty_rationale: Applies the voluntary conservation restriction valuation framework to flood risk disclosure, testing whether market-based climate adaptation through voluntary certification generates measurable price signals that could substitute for mandatory disclosure mandates.
nn_title: Dutch dilemma: Housing prices and flood risk exposure
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do short-term rental platforms—by converting residential units to quasi-commercial use—generate tax leakage analogous to the vacation rental evasion problem, and does targeted enforcement through platform data-sharing agreements increase property tax compliance among Airbnb host-landlords?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting variation in the timing and scope of data-sharing agreements between Airbnb and state tax authorities across U.S. states, comparing short-term rental income reporting rates and local property tax assessment compliance before and after agreement adoption using IRS Statistics of Income and county assessor data.
data: IRS Statistics of Income rental income data at the state level (2013–2021), AirDNA Airbnb listing data with host identifiers, county assessor property classification and assessment data from ATTOM, and state tax authority data-sharing agreement dates from news archives and state revenue department records.
key_finding: Data-sharing agreements between tax authorities and Airbnb may increase declared short-term rental income by 15–30% and raise property reclassification from residential to commercial assessment in high-density Airbnb markets by 3–8 percentage points.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the vacation rental tax evasion deterrence framework from Spain to the U.S. context using platform data-sharing as the exogenous enforcement variation, connecting the Airbnb regulation literature to tax compliance research.
nn_title: How do short‐term rental regulations affect market outcomes? Evidence from San F
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do households who grew up in low-income public housing neighborhoods exhibit systematically lower rates of homeownership and higher rates of mortgage delinquency in adulthood, and are these effects mediated by differences in financial literacy, credit history, and neighborhood peer effects rather than income alone?
method: Longitudinal cohort analysis following children assigned to public housing via lottery or waiting list in Chicago, Boston, and New York, tracking homeownership transitions, mortgage origination outcomes, and delinquency rates in adulthood using HUD administrative records linked to credit bureau data, with mediation analysis decomposing effects through financial literacy survey measures.
data: HUD Moving to Opportunity longitudinal follow-up data, Chicago Housing Authority tenant records linked to Equifax credit panel data via the FRBNY Consumer Credit Panel, and HMDA mortgage origination records matched by name and address.
key_finding: Public housing childhood residents may achieve homeownership rates 8–15 percentage points lower than income-matched comparison children, with 40–60% of the gap explained by credit history differences and neighborhood peer effects on financial behavior rather than income constraints alone.
novelty_rationale: Applies the long-run effects of public housing childhood exposure framework to adult mortgage market outcomes, identifying a novel pathway from neighborhood disadvantage to housing wealth inequality through mortgage access rather than only labor market channels.
nn_title: The Effects of Homeownership on Children's Outcomes: Real Effects or Self‐Select
assigned_label: Household tenure choice and homeownership decisions

research_question: Does the optimal timing of commercial real estate redevelopment exhibit procyclical bias when developers use debt financing—analogous to the leveraged city theoretical prediction that leverage causes early option exercise—and is this bias larger for developers with higher loan-to-value ratios at acquisition?
method: Empirical test of the leveraged city model using CoStar demolition and redevelopment permit data matched to TREPP CMBS origination records, testing whether properties acquired with higher LTV loans are demolished and redeveloped sooner than observationally equivalent properties acquired with lower leverage, controlling for property age, location, and market conditions.
data: CoStar building demolition and construction permit records (2000–2020), TREPP CMBS loan origination database with LTV ratios and origination dates, CoStar property transaction history, and local construction permit records from the Census Bureau Building Permits Survey.
key_finding: Properties acquired with LTV ratios above 70% may be redeveloped 2–4 years earlier than comparable properties with LTV ratios below 50%, with the timing acceleration larger in markets with higher land value growth, consistent with debt-induced early option exercise reducing irreversibility premiums.
novelty_rationale: Provides the first empirical test of the leveraged city real options model using matched loan and redevelopment data, directly testing whether developer leverage causes the procyclical development timing predicted by theory.
nn_title: The Leveraged City
assigned_label: Real Options Theory in Land Development

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'compassion fade' analog when evaluating aggregate versus individual flood risk disclosures, paying larger price discounts for single high-risk properties than for portfolios of similarly exposed homes?
method: Hedonic regression with difference-in-differences exploiting FEMA flood map revisions that reclassify individual parcels versus entire census tracts, comparing price responses across disclosure scales.
data: CoreLogic deeds and FEMA National Flood Insurance Program policy data matched to flood zone designations, covering approximately 20 million transactions in coastal MSAs from 2000–2022.
key_finding: Individual property flood risk disclosures may generate 2–4x larger price discounts per exposed dollar than equivalent aggregate neighborhood-level disclosures, consistent with compassion-fade mechanisms documented in charitable giving.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the compassion-fade psychological mechanism from charitable giving research to environmental risk capitalization, testing whether risk salience is attenuated by aggregation in real estate markets.
nn_title: When risk does not discount: Flood history and rising property valuations
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does the left-digit anchoring bias that distorts property tax capitalization also distort homebuyer valuation of listed prices, such that homes priced just below $100,000 thresholds sell at premiums that cannot be explained by search or rational inattention models?
method: Regression discontinuity design using listing prices within $1,000 bands on either side of $X00,000 thresholds, with separate identification of listing-price charm effects and true transaction-price discontinuities using repeat-sales pairs.
data: Zillow transaction and listing-history data covering 80+ metropolitan areas from 2010–2023, supplemented by MLS data from research partnerships used in prior published work.
key_finding: Homes priced just below $100k thresholds are expected to sell at 0.3–0.8% premiums relative to homes priced just above, with effects larger in low-numeracy zip codes identified via Census educational attainment data.
novelty_rationale: Extends the left-digit bias literature from property tax perceptions to listing price perception, unifying two separate strands of behavioral real estate research under a single cognitive mechanism.
nn_title: Left‐digit bias in property taxes
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do real estate agents acting as group representatives—formally representing an institutional seller such as a bank or government agency—negotiate more risk-aversely and accept lower prices than agents representing individual sellers?
method: Hedonic regression comparing sale-price-to-list-price ratios and time-on-market for REO properties sold by institutional versus individual sellers, using seller-type classifications in deed records as a proxy for representative decision-making.
data: CoreLogic REO and distressed-sale deed records matched to MLS listing data from 2008–2016, covering approximately 3 million distressed transactions with identified seller entity types.
key_finding: Institutional-representative agents are expected to accept 3–6% lower prices and shorter marketing times than individual-seller agents, consistent with risk-aversion effects documented in group-representative decision experiments.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the experimental finding that group representatives are more risk-averse into a field setting, using institutional-versus-individual seller variation as a natural proxy for the representative role.
nn_title: Examining Both Sides of the Transaction: Bargaining in the Housing Market
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers who receive algorithmic automated underwriting approval show lower sensitivity to loan officer advice than borrowers processed manually, and does this over-reliance on algorithms increase default risk?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered adoption of Fannie Mae's Desktop Underwriter across lenders, comparing loan performance and rate-shopping behavior between algorithm-approved and manually underwritten loans.
data: HMDA loan-level origination data merged with Fannie Mae single-family loan performance datasets covering 1995–2010, spanning the rollout of automated underwriting systems.
key_finding: Algorithm-approved borrowers are expected to show 15–25% lower sensitivity to loan-officer rate negotiation and 10–20% higher conditional default rates after controlling for observable credit quality.
novelty_rationale: Applies the algorithmic advice reliance literature to mortgage markets, testing whether automation-induced complacency in borrowers generates adverse selection effects not captured by standard credit models.
nn_title: Do Borrowers Make Rational Choices on Points and Refinancing?
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Does experiencing a rapid house price decline in a buyer's first owned property permanently shift their risk preferences toward more conservative mortgage products in subsequent purchases?
method: Panel analysis of individual borrower mortgage product sequences using matched HMDA and credit bureau data, exploiting cross-MSA variation in price declines during 2007–2012 as a quasi-experiment in experienced losses.
data: FHFA and CoreLogic matched borrower-level datasets linking origination records across multiple mortgage transactions for the same borrower, covering repeat buyers from 1995–2022.
key_finding: Buyers who experienced a 20%+ price decline on their first home are expected to be 18–28 percentage points more likely to choose fixed-rate mortgages on subsequent purchases, consistent with experience-based risk preference updating.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the decisions-from-experience paradigm to housing, testing whether lived losses durably shift mortgage instrument preferences beyond what contemporaneous interest rate spreads would predict.
nn_title: The Borrower's Perceived Risk in Mortgage Choice
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit a 'zero-risk effect' in flood insurance decisions, strongly preferring properties outside any flood zone over properties in low-risk zones, even when expected annual losses favor the low-risk-zone purchase?
method: Hedonic price regression comparing the marginal price discount for low-risk (Zone X) versus high-risk (Zone AE) flood designations, testing whether the discount is discontinuously larger at the AE boundary than the continuous risk gradient would predict.
data: FEMA flood zone boundary GIS files matched to CoreLogic transaction data for coastal MSAs from 2000–2022, with National Flood Insurance Program claim histories for expected loss calibration.
key_finding: The price penalty for crossing the AE boundary is expected to be 2–3x larger than the gradient within the AE zone, consistent with a zero-risk effect where buyers over-weight categorical safety.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether the zero-risk preference anomaly from life-saving decision research applies to residential property risk pricing, predicting a nonlinear price-risk relationship at regulatory flood zone boundaries.
nn_title: Dutch dilemma: Housing prices and flood risk exposure
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do sellers exhibit an attraction-effect preference reversal when evaluating competing offers, over-valuing a third dominated offer that makes their preferred offer look relatively better, leading to anchoring distortions in final negotiated prices?
method: Regression analysis of MLS listing histories where properties received multiple simultaneous offers, testing whether the presence of a clearly inferior third offer (identified by agent-reported offer characteristics) predicts higher final sale prices.
data: MLS multiple-offer disclosure data from markets requiring agent disclosure of offer counts and terms, combined with final transaction prices from state deed records, covering 2015–2022.
key_finding: Listings with a clearly dominated third offer are expected to close 0.5–1.5% above comparable listings receiving only two offers, consistent with attraction-effect-induced anchor elevation.
novelty_rationale: Introduces the attraction decoy effect from consumer choice psychology to real estate negotiation, providing the first field evidence of context-dependent preference reversals in multi-offer property transactions.
nn_title: List Prices, Sale Prices and Marketing Time: An Application to U.S. Housing Mark
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do real estate appraisers exhibit overconfidence that is domain-general rather than task-specific, such that appraisers with higher overconfidence on standardized property valuation tasks also show larger bias in atypical or thin-market properties?
method: Cross-sectional regression of appraiser-level valuation bias (appraisal minus subsequent sale price) on an appraiser overconfidence index constructed from the variance and calibration of their historical appraisals, controlling for property type and market conditions.
data: Appraisal-level data from Freddie Mac or Fannie Mae appraisal datasets matched to subsequent sale prices, covering individual appraiser identifiers from 2005–2020.
key_finding: Appraisers in the top quartile of overconfidence are expected to show 40–60% larger systematic bias in atypical properties, with effects persistent across market cycles, consistent with generalized rather than task-specific overconfidence.
novelty_rationale: Applies new psychometric overconfidence measurement approaches to appraiser populations, testing whether individual-level overconfidence predicts systematic valuation bias in field settings.
nn_title: The influence of contract prices and relationships on appraisal bias
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Do homeowners exhibit a 'peak-end' evaluation bias in reporting their housing satisfaction, with recalled satisfaction dominated by the best period and the most recent period of ownership rather than the time-averaged experience?
method: Panel regression of homeowner satisfaction reports from the American Housing Survey on lagged satisfaction scores, local price appreciation peaks, and recent price changes, decomposing the relative weights of peak versus recent versus average experiences.
data: American Housing Survey longitudinal panel data (1985–2021) linking the same households across survey waves, merged with FHFA zip-code-level house price indices for peak and recent appreciation measures.
key_finding: Homeowner satisfaction is expected to weight peak appreciation periods approximately 1.5–2.5x more than average periods, with recent appreciation having comparable weight to the peak, consistent with peak-end evaluation.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the peak-end rule from hedonic psychology to housing satisfaction research, with implications for tenure decisions and the welfare effects of housing cycles.
nn_title: Do House Price Levels Anticipate Subsequent Price Changes within Metropolitan Ar
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the 'diffusion of responsibility' mechanism cause mortgage loan officers who follow algorithmic recommendation systems to originate riskier loans than those making fully discretionary decisions, because responsibility for outcomes is shared with the algorithm?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered introduction of loan recommendation overlays by different lenders, comparing ex-post default rates between algorithmically-guided and discretionary originations within the same lender before and after system adoption.
data: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan-level performance datasets merged with HMDA origination records, covering lender-specific technology adoption periods identifiable through HMDA reporter characteristics from 2000–2015.
key_finding: Algorithmic recommendation adoption is expected to increase conditional default rates by 8–15% through reduced loan officer due diligence, consistent with diffusion of responsibility reducing individual accountability.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether diffusion of responsibility—a well-documented social psychological mechanism—operates in mortgage origination when decision authority is shared between human agents and algorithmic systems.
nn_title: Do Borrowers Make Rational Choices on Points and Refinancing?
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit future self-discontinuity that leads them to systematically undervalue long-run energy cost savings from efficient homes, and is this undervaluation larger for younger buyers with weaker future self-continuity?
method: Hedonic regression of energy label price premiums on buyer age and proxies for future self-continuity (first-time buyer status, loan term chosen), using the energy certificate database matched to transaction records in the Netherlands or UK.
data: Dutch NVM or UK EPC register transaction data with energy label ratings merged to deed-level buyer characteristics, covering 2010–2022 with approximately 1–2 million transactions.
key_finding: Energy efficiency premiums are expected to be 30–50% smaller for buyers under 35 relative to buyers over 55, with the age gradient steeper in high-energy-cost periods, consistent with future self-continuity moderating intertemporal valuation.
novelty_rationale: Applies future self-continuity theory to explain persistent undervaluation of energy efficiency in housing, linking individual-level identity psychology to a major policy-relevant market failure.
nn_title: Energy labels, misreporting, and heuristic hedonic models
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do real estate investment trust managers exhibit the disposition effect—selling outperforming assets too early and holding underperforming assets too long—and is this effect attenuated when managers make decisions on behalf of large institutional owners?
method: Property-level holding period analysis of REIT dispositions using NCREIF property data, testing whether the probability of sale is higher for properties with unrealized gains than losses, with cross-sectional moderation by institutional ownership concentration.
data: NCREIF Property Index individual property records merged with REIT 10-K property disposition data and institutional ownership data from Thomson Reuters 13F filings, covering 1990–2020.
key_finding: REIT managers are expected to be 20–35% more likely to sell properties with above-average unrealized appreciation, with this disposition effect cut by 40–60% for REITs with concentrated institutional ownership, consistent with other-regarding attenuation.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether the disposition effect documented in stock markets operates in illiquid commercial real estate and whether institutional oversight attenuates it, as predicted by other-regarding decision-making research.
nn_title: Institutional common ownership and firm value: Evidence from real estate investm
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does warning borrowers about prepayment penalty costs at origination backfire by increasing attention to refinancing opportunities, ultimately increasing the probability of costly early refinancing among penalty-exempt borrowers?
method: Regression discontinuity using the regulatory threshold above which mandatory prepayment penalty disclosures are required in certain states, comparing early refinancing rates for loans just above versus just below the disclosure threshold.
data: Fannie Mae single-family loan performance data merged with state-level prepayment penalty disclosure regulations, covering approximately 30 million loans from 2000–2010 with prepayment event records.
key_finding: Mandatory disclosure of prepayment penalties is expected to increase early refinancing by 5–12% among borrowers near the threshold, consistent with warnings backfiring by increasing salience of the refinancing option.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the warning-backfire mechanism from behavioral decision research to mortgage markets, testing whether mandatory disclosure of penalties paradoxically accelerates the behavior it seeks to discourage.
nn_title: Prepayment Penalty as a Screening Mechanism for Default and Prepayment Risks
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do real estate agents exhibit homophily-driven trust that leads same-ethnicity agent-client pairs to achieve better transaction outcomes than cross-ethnicity pairs, even after controlling for information advantages and network effects?
method: Hedonic price regression with buyer-seller-agent ethnicity fixed effects identified through name-based ethnicity classification algorithms, decomposing the same-ethnicity price premium into information, trust, and network components using geographic variation in ethnic concentration.
data: CoreLogic deed data with agent license numbers merged to state license board records, combined with name-based ethnicity classification (using Census surname lists) for buyers, sellers, and agents, covering 2010–2022 for California, Texas, and Florida.
key_finding: Same-ethnicity agent-buyer pairs are expected to yield 0.8–1.8% higher transaction prices and 5–10 fewer days on market than cross-ethnicity pairs, with trust-component identification using variation in ethnic neighborhood density as an exclusion restriction.
novelty_rationale: Decomposes the ethnicity matching premium in real estate brokerage into trust versus information channels using behavioral trust theory, advancing beyond reduced-form discrimination studies.
nn_title: Ethnicity in housing markets: Buyers, sellers and agents
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do real estate developers in competitive markets exercise development options earlier than monopolist developers, and does this competitive preemption result in overbuilding during low-volatility periods and underbuilding during high-volatility periods?
method: Comparison of building permit issuance timing relative to market fundamentals across MSAs with varying developer market concentration (measured by HHI), using Markov-Switching GARCH models to identify high- versus low-volatility regimes.
data: Census Building Permit Survey merged with CoStar market concentration data and FHFA house price indices for 200+ MSAs from 1990–2022, with developer identity tracked through state contractor licensing records.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in developer market concentration is expected to delay option exercise by 4–8 months in normal volatility regimes but reduce overbuild by 15–25% in high-volatility regimes, consistent with equilibrium real options theory.
novelty_rationale: Tests the equilibrium real options prediction that competitive market structure distorts development timing in asymmetric ways across volatility regimes, a theoretical prediction that has never been empirically validated at scale.
nn_title: Risk aversion and urban land development options
assigned_label: Real Options Theory in Land Development

research_question: Does the sequential order in which house features are presented in online listings create an incumbency-like memory advantage, such that features listed first receive disproportionate weight in final offer prices?
method: Field experiment in partnership with a real estate platform randomly varying the ordering of property feature descriptions within listings, measuring subsequent inquiry rates, offer amounts, and final sale prices across listing cohorts.
data: Platform-generated randomization data from a major US listing portal (e.g., Realtor.com or Redfin), with A/B test infrastructure already used in published research on online listing effects, covering 2023–2024.
key_finding: Properties whose most desirable attributes are listed first are expected to generate 1–3% higher offers and 8–15% more inquiries than matched properties with identical features listed in reverse order, consistent with memory retrieval primacy effects.
novelty_rationale: Applies query-theory memory retrieval mechanisms from voting research to online real estate search, providing causal evidence that presentation order—rather than content—shapes property valuations.
nn_title: What Problem Do Intermediaries Solve? Evidence From Real Estate Markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do underwater homeowners who report positive home equity to surveyors (misrepresenting their situation) subsequently show similar mortgage default probabilities as those who accurately report negative equity, suggesting motivated reporting rather than genuine misperception?
method: Matching American Housing Survey self-reported equity to FHFA zip-code price indices and loan balance records to identify misreporters, then tracking subsequent default rates using credit bureau data to test whether behavioral patterns reveal genuine belief versus strategic misreporting.
data: American Housing Survey linked to CoreLogic loan performance records and FHFA price indices, exploiting the AHS longitudinal design to track the same households' subsequent delinquency from 2007–2013.
key_finding: Underwater homeowners who misreport positive equity are expected to show default hazard rates statistically indistinguishable from accurate negative-equity reporters, suggesting strategic misreporting rather than genuine money illusion.
novelty_rationale: Uses revealed preference from subsequent default behavior to adjudicate between genuine misperception and strategic misreporting of housing equity, applying behavioral economics methodology to a policy-relevant housing finance question.
nn_title: Fear, Shame and Guilt: Economic and Behavioral Motivations for Strategic Default
assigned_label: Mortgage Default, Delinquency, and Credit Loss

research_question: Do first-time homebuyers exhibit more loss aversion in their listing price strategies than experienced sellers, setting higher ask prices relative to their purchase price that lead to longer time-on-market without higher final sale prices?
method: Hedonic regression of list-price markup and time-on-market on seller experience (first-time versus repeat sellers identified through deed records), with loss aversion proxied by the gap between purchase price and estimated current market value.
data: CoreLogic deed transaction histories identifying buyer-seller sequences for the same individuals, merged with MLS listing data to recover list prices and days on market, covering 2000–2022 for 10 major MSAs.
key_finding: First-time sellers are expected to set list prices 1.5–3% higher relative to fundamentals when facing nominal losses, with 15–25% longer marketing times but no statistically significant difference in final sale price, consistent with loss aversion generating search frictions.
novelty_rationale: Isolates seller inexperience from loss aversion in housing markets by contrasting first-time and repeat sellers' pricing strategies, providing cleaner identification of the loss aversion mechanism than prior anchoring studies.
nn_title: List Prices, Sale Prices and Marketing Time: An Application to U.S. Housing Mark
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the 'more-is-better' heuristic lead commercial real estate investors to systematically overpay for larger portfolios of properties even when per-unit values do not justify a premium, and is this effect larger for less experienced buyers?
method: Hedonic regression comparing per-unit transaction prices for portfolio acquisitions versus individual property sales in commercial real estate, using CoStar transaction records with buyer identity to proxy experience and instrumenting portfolio size with seller forced-sale status.
data: CoStar commercial transaction database with portfolio deal flags, covering approximately 500,000 commercial transactions from 2000–2022, with buyer identity linked to experience measures via repeat transaction history.
key_finding: Portfolio transactions are expected to carry a 3–7% per-unit premium over equivalent individual sales for inexperienced buyers, reversing to a discount for experienced institutional buyers, consistent with a more-is-better heuristic that diminishes with market expertise.
novelty_rationale: Applies the anticommons and more-is-better heuristic from behavioral economics to commercial real estate portfolio pricing, providing the first large-sample field test of whether naïve bundling biases affect institutional property markets.
nn_title: Clientele Effects and Condo Conversions
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do gradual increases in neighborhood vacancy rates reduce residents' concern and mobility responses compared to equivalent sudden vacancy shocks, consistent with an 'out of sight, out of mind' salience mechanism?
method: Difference-in-differences comparing residential mobility rates and housing price responses across neighborhoods experiencing gradual versus sudden vacancy increases, exploiting the staggered timing of factory closures and retail anchor exits as exogenous shocks.
data: USPS vacancy records at the delivery-point level merged with IRS migration data and Zillow zip-code price indices, covering 2005–2019 across 500+ MSAs with plant closure events identified from WARN Act notices.
key_finding: Neighborhoods experiencing gradual vacancy accumulation are expected to show 20–35% lower residential outmigration and 10–20% smaller price declines over the same cumulative vacancy level relative to neighborhoods experiencing sudden vacancy shocks.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether the gradual materialization mechanism that reduces salience of public bads operates in housing markets, with direct policy implications for blight prevention and neighborhood stabilization programs.
nn_title: Old homes, externalities, and poor neighborhoods. A model of urban decline and r
assigned_label: Housing Market Efficiency and Pricing Anomalies

research_question: Does a sense of responsibility, created by co-signing arrangements or guarantor obligations on commercial mortgages, cause borrowers to adopt more conservative leverage and lower default rates than observationally similar non-guaranteed borrowers?
method: Comparison of default rates and LTV ratios between commercial mortgages with and without recourse guarantees or co-signers, using propensity score matching on loan and property characteristics with a within-lender design to control for selection.
data: Trepp CMBS loan-level performance data merged with property records, identifying recourse structure from loan origination documents, covering approximately 100,000 loans from 2000–2020 with full performance histories.
key_finding: Recourse and co-signed loans are expected to show 20–35% lower default hazard and 3–5 percentage points lower LTV at origination after matching, consistent with responsibility-induced risk aversion beyond the mechanical collateral effect.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether the sense-of-responsibility mechanism documented in group representative experiments operates in commercial lending, distinguishing psychological accountability effects from the mechanical financial incentives of recourse.
nn_title: Recourse and Residential Mortgage Default: Evidence from US States
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Do real estate mutual fund managers who receive relative performance feedback exhibit more extreme portfolio herding or contrarianism than managers receiving only absolute return information, analogous to competitive reference point effects?
method: Quarterly holdings analysis of real estate mutual funds from SEC 13F filings, constructing herding measures and testing whether managers whose prior-period relative rank fell below median show larger subsequent portfolio shifts than those above median.
data: SEC EDGAR 13F holdings data for real estate mutual funds merged with CRSP fund return data and REIT benchmark indices, covering approximately 200 funds from 1995–2022 with quarterly portfolio snapshots.
key_finding: Managers in the bottom tercile of relative performance are expected to show herding measures 30–50% larger than top-tercile managers in the following quarter, consistent with competitive loss framing increasing conformist risk-taking.
novelty_rationale: Applies competitive reference point and loss-framing theory from behavioral research to real estate fund management, linking manager rank anxiety to herding behavior and ultimately to REIT return comovement.
nn_title: Real Estate Mutual Funds: Herding, Momentum Trading and Performance
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Do mortgage borrowers exhibit a preference for quicker loan approval offers that leads them to accept worse terms from fast-responding lenders, and does this temporal preference bias vary with borrower financial sophistication?
method: Analysis of HMDA mortgage applications matched to approval timing records, testing whether borrowers who received faster approvals accepted higher APRs conditional on credit risk, with financial sophistication proxied by borrower income and education at the census tract level.
data: HMDA application-level data with approval dates merged to final loan terms from CoreLogic origination records and census tract educational attainment data, covering 2018–2022 with lender response time variation identified across applications.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation reduction in lender response time is expected to be associated with 4–8 basis points higher accepted APR, with the premium twice as large in census tracts with below-median college attainment.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether temporal reference point biases—the preference for quicker resolution documented in experimental settings—manifest in high-stakes mortgage markets with heterogeneous financial sophistication.
nn_title: Is competition a cure for confusion? Evidence from the residential mortgage mark
assigned_label: Mortgage Contract Design and Choice

research_question: Does the cultural tightness of a neighborhood—the degree to which social norms are strictly enforced—predict the strength of hedonic amenity capitalization, with tight-culture neighborhoods showing stronger price penalties for norm-violating land uses such as liquor stores or sex offender registrants?
method: Hedonic regression interacting distances to norm-violating amenities with a cultural tightness index constructed from voting patterns, civic organization density, and code enforcement frequency at the census tract level.
data: CoreLogic deed data merged with sex offender registry locations, liquor license records, and tract-level cultural tightness proxies from MIT Election Data Lab voting records and Census social capital measures, covering 2010–2022.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in neighborhood cultural tightness is expected to amplify the price penalty for sex offender proximity by 40–70% and the liquor store discount by 20–40%, consistent with stronger norm enforcement increasing the social cost of disamenities.
novelty_rationale: Introduces the cultural tightness construct from organizational psychology to hedonic pricing, providing a novel theoretical mechanism for the well-documented cross-neighborhood heterogeneity in disamenity capitalization.
nn_title: The Incidence of the Land Use Regulatory Tax
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do homeowners in markets with more active short-term rental platforms exhibit reduced maintenance expenditures on private home features while increasing investment in publicly visible attributes, consistent with audience-signaling behavior that responds to external reputation systems?
method: Difference-in-differences comparing American Housing Survey maintenance expenditure categories before and after Airbnb entry into metropolitan areas, distinguishing visible (kitchen, exterior) versus invisible (HVAC, foundation) maintenance categories.
data: American Housing Survey maintenance expenditure microdata (2005–2021) merged with Airbnb market penetration rates from Inside Airbnb data by metropolitan area, exploiting the staggered city-by-city platform rollout.
key_finding: Airbnb market entry is expected to increase visible maintenance expenditures by 8–15% and decrease invisible maintenance by 5–10% in affected metro areas, consistent with signaling theory predicting investment reallocation toward reputation-relevant attributes.
novelty_rationale: Tests whether external reputation mechanisms from the sharing economy reshape homeowner maintenance investment allocation, linking behavioral signaling theory to housing capital accumulation and long-run housing quality.
nn_title: How do short‐term rental regulations affect market outcomes? Evidence from San F
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do real estate listing agents who receive positive performance feedback (high sale-price-to-list-price ratios) subsequently increase their asking prices on new listings to levels that exceed fundamental value, analogous to the 'false feedback increases confidence' finding from psychology?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting the discrete threshold between above- and below-median sale-price-to-list-price ratios as a proxy for positive versus negative performance feedback, comparing subsequent listing prices relative to automated valuation model estimates for the same agent's next listings.
data: Multiple Listing Service transaction data from a large metropolitan area (as used in existing published studies on agent behavior), linked at the agent level across sequential listings to construct performance feedback histories and subsequent pricing decisions.
key_finding: Agents receiving above-median sale-to-list feedback price their next listings 1-3% above AVM-implied value relative to agents receiving below-median feedback, with the effect decaying after three to five subsequent transactions.
novelty_rationale: Transplants the confidence-manipulation-through-feedback design to agent pricing behavior, testing whether performance feedback inflates subsequent pricing decisions rather than improving calibration, a mechanism entirely absent from the real estate brokerage literature.
nn_title: The Disciplining Effect of Concern for Referrals: Evidence from Real Estate Agen
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do homebuyers exhibit negative time discounting for the experience of purchasing in a declining market—preferring to accelerate their purchase to 'get it over with' rather than wait for further price drops—consistent with the anticipated dread mechanism identified in psychology?
method: Survey-based experiment embedded in an existing homebuyer panel study, presenting randomly assigned vignettes describing identical declining-price trajectories with different expected durations before market stabilization, eliciting willingness to purchase now versus wait and measuring dread scores.
data: American Housing Survey supplemented with a new survey module, or replication using the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers housing intention questions linked to local Case-Shiller price trajectory data to proxy dread-relevant market conditions.
key_finding: Buyers in markets with persistently declining prices display purchase acceleration inconsistent with rational option-exercise models, with acceleration magnitude correlating with self-reported anxiety about waiting, explaining up to 15% of transaction volume in down markets.
novelty_rationale: Imports the anticipated-dread framework for negative intertemporal choice into housing demand, offering a behavioral explanation for purchase timing anomalies during price declines that is distinct from the standard option-value and liquidity explanations in the literature.
nn_title: House Search Duration in Hot and Cold Residential Markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Does the dynamic presentation of comparable sales—revealing them sequentially rather than simultaneously—alter homebuyer willingness to pay relative to static simultaneous presentation, consistent with evolving-choice-set preference reversals documented in psychology?
method: Between-subjects online experiment with a nationally representative sample of recent homebuyers, randomly assigning subjects to sequential versus simultaneous presentation of comparable sales data for an identical subject property and eliciting valuation and purchase-intention responses.
data: Original experimental data collected via an online panel (Qualtrics or similar) with recruitment of participants who purchased a home within the prior 24 months, with stimuli constructed from publicly available Zillow or Redfin listing data for real properties.
key_finding: Sequential presentation of comparables increases willingness to pay by 4-8% relative to simultaneous presentation when the final comparable shown is high-priced, with the effect mediated by recency weighting of the last-seen observation.
novelty_rationale: Translates the evolving-choice-set paradigm to real estate valuation, providing experimental evidence that the order and timing of comparable disclosure—a core institutional feature of the appraisal process—independently shapes valuations beyond the information content of the comparables themselves.
nn_title: Combining Revealed and Stated Data to Examine Housing Decisions Using Discrete C
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: Do mortgage servicers exhibit a 'constraint neglect' bias when evaluating borrower modification eligibility, failing to account for borrowers' liquidity constraints and instead judging modification decisions based on outcome (default or cure) rather than the quality of the modification decision given available information at the time?
method: Vignette-based experiment administered to mortgage servicing professionals, presenting identical borrower profiles with randomly assigned outcomes (successful cure versus subsequent default) and eliciting judgments of modification decision quality, controlling for observable borrower characteristics.
data: Primary survey data collected from mortgage servicing industry professionals recruited through industry association networks (Mortgage Bankers Association), supplemented by HAMP modification outcome data from the U.S. Treasury to calibrate vignette realism.
key_finding: Servicers rate modification decisions as lower quality when the borrower subsequently defaults regardless of whether the modification was objectively appropriate given information available at decision time, with outcome bias increasing with servicer experience and performance incentive intensity.
novelty_rationale: Imports the constraint-neglect and outcome-bias paradigm into mortgage servicing decisions, identifying a systematic judgment error with direct implications for modification program design and servicer compensation structures that has no precedent in the mortgage literature.
nn_title: Delinquencies, Default and Borrowers' Strategic Behavior toward the Modification
assigned_label: Mortgage market efficiency and credit availability

research_question: Do homeowners exhibit a 'gist-based' rather than verbatim processing of flood risk zone designations, treating categorical zone distinctions (AE versus X) as binary safe/unsafe signals while ignoring quantitative probability differences, consistent with fuzzy-trace theory predictions?
method: Survey experiment with a representative sample of coastal homeowners, randomly assigning verbatim (exact annual probability) versus gist (categorical zone label) presentation of flood risk and measuring insurance purchase intentions, risk perception accuracy, and willingness to accept property price discounts.
data: FEMA National Flood Insurance Program policyholder data linked to CoreLogic property records for flood zone classification, supplemented by original survey data from homeowners in FEMA-mapped coastal counties in Florida, Texas, and South Carolina.
key_finding: Homeowners receiving gist-framed flood risk information show bimodal insurance purchase behavior clustering around categorical zone boundaries rather than continuous probability gradients, with a 20-30% difference in purchase rates at zone boundaries that disappears with verbatim presentation.
novelty_rationale: Applies fuzzy-trace theory's gist-versus-verbatim distinction to flood risk communication in real estate, providing a cognitive mechanism for the well-documented discrete discontinuities in flood risk capitalization at FEMA zone boundaries rather than relying solely on insurance cost explanations.
nn_title: Going Underwater? Flood Risk Belief Heterogeneity and Coastal Home Price Dynamic
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Do real estate investors who make acquisitions on behalf of institutions (pension funds, REITs) exhibit greater loss aversion and risk aversion in bid prices than individual investors acting for themselves, consistent with the heightened sense of responsibility finding from group-representative decision research?
method: Matched-sample analysis comparing winning bid prices and bid-to-value ratios for institutional versus individual buyers in NCREIF property transactions and RCA commercial real estate data, controlling for property type, geography, and market conditions, with a survey module eliciting responsibility perceptions.
data: Real Capital Analytics commercial transaction database (widely used in published research) merged with buyer-type classifications, supplemented by NCREIF open-end fund return data and a survey of commercial real estate acquisition professionals.
key_finding: Institutional acquisition officers bid 3-6% below estimated fundamental value relative to private buyers of equivalent properties, consistent with responsibility-induced conservatism, with the discount increasing with fund size and board oversight intensity.
novelty_rationale: Tests the group-representative responsibility mechanism in the commercial real estate acquisition context, offering an alternative behavioral explanation for the documented institutional underperformance in asset acquisition relative to private buyers beyond standard agency cost theories.
nn_title: Institutional common ownership and firm value: Evidence from real estate investm
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does the prominence of identity signals—specifically whether a buyer's demographic background is salient or subtle in the transaction—affect real estate agent effort allocation and negotiated prices, consistent with the identity-signal-prominence mechanism from intergroup interaction research?
